experiments.title: Can Highlighting the Monthly Impact Boost Recurring Giving?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly giving callout above the donation form on the main donation page will increase monthly donor conversions, as framing monthly giving as impactful and rewarding will motivate recurring support without displacing larger one-time donations. Framing monthly giving as impactful and rewarding will motivate recurring support without displacing larger one-time donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2025 - 10/26/2025
The organization’s main donation page serves both committed donors and new visitors, playing a central role in overall giving. Unlike acquisition-focused pages meant to convert new audiences, this page often attracts high-value donors and returning supporters. As such, it presents a high-opportunity but high-risk space for testing.
In previous tests on acquisition donation pages, the organization found that adding a monthly gift callout that highlights the recurring impact and donor benefits increased the number of donors who chose to give monthly. Those tests indicated that, with the right framing, some page visitors can be persuaded to commit to becoming long-term donors.
This experiment asked whether that insight would transfer to the main donation page (without cannibalizing revenue from larger one-time gifts). Would prompting donors to “consider a monthly gift” increase monthly conversions? Or would it cause high-capacity donors to downshift from large one-time gifts to smaller recurring ones?
The Control used the standard donation page layout, which defaults to a one-time gift and includes no additional prompts about monthly giving.
The Treatment introduced a soft callout above the form that read: “Would you consider a monthly donation? Your monthly gift of $15 ensures families in spiritual crisis can find trusted help the moment they need it—and receive biblical support year-round. Plus, you’ll get to choose a new free resource every month for your family.”
No other changes were made to the page layout or the form’s functionality. We randomly split traffic 50/50 between both versions and ran this test for 1 month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test demonstrated that a soft monthly giving prompt above the donation form can significantly increase recurring donor conversions—without suppressing large gifts and with potential upside for smaller one-time donations.
The Treatment produced a 125% increase in monthly donor conversions (2.1% vs. 0.9%), with a statistically significant 99.3% level of confidence.
 	Recurring donor volume more than doubled (33 vs. 15), while the average monthly gift remained steady ($30 on Treatment vs. $31 on Control, after removing an outlier)
 	While the Treatment had fewer gifts over $1,000 (35 vs. 41), this difference was not statistically significant (44% level of confidence), indicating no reliable evidence that large one-time gifts were cannibalized.
smaller one-time gifts (under $1,000)
The Treatment saw a 9.5% lift in volume, with an 86.4% level of confidence -- this is a strong directional signal, though not statistically conclusive
 	The average gift amount also increased, from $132 on the Control to $140 on the Treatment
Next Steps for Testing:
Explore alternate monthly ask amounts (e.g., $20, $25, or $30) to assess the revenue impact of shifting the default suggested amount.
 	Refine the callout language or visual design to test whether urgency, impact framing, or reward emphasis further improves performance.
 	Test this approach on other key donation pathways (e.g., campaign pages, seasonal asks) to evaluate if the effect carries over beyond the main donation page.

experiments.title: How a donor goal on a high urgency campaign pushdown impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor goal to the February Sustainers Pushdown for homepage visitors will increase donor conversion metrics because potential donors will feel their monthly gift would have a larger impact on the goal.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/03/2025
During The Navigators’ February Sustainers Campaign, we decided to test adding a donor goal into the pushdown copy.
The first treatment variation set a blanket donor goal of 100 monthly donors, and the second treatment variation set donor goals for each amount depicted on the gift array (i.e. Will you be one of the 50 monthly donors to make a $15 monthly gift, 30 donors to make a $25 monthly gift, or 20 donors to make a $50 monthly gift today?)
The goal of this test was to determine whether The Navigators’ audience would respond positively to a defined donor goal and, if so, whether they were more motivated by an overarching goal or by a goal broken into smaller amounts that felt more attainable.
Our hypothesis was that breaking down the donor goal into smaller, specific amounts would make it feel more achievable, increasing the likelihood of giving. By seeing a clearer, tangible impact, donors would be more inclined to contribute, believing their gift could make a greater difference.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor goal to the February Sustainers Pushdown for homepage visitors significantly increased donor conversion metrics. The treatment variation that set donor goals for each amount depicted on the gift array resulted in a 266.8% increase in donations across all traffic compared to the control group, with a 96.8% statistical level of confidence. It is possible that Treatment #2 caused more confusion and friction on the page, rather than the very clear goal of 100 donors in Treatment #1. This suggests that The Navigators' audience reacts positively to clarity and set goal amounts. Furthermore, Treatment #2 had a 301% lift in revenue at a 97.4% confidence level. Not only did the overall donor goal motivate more people to give, but it also motivated them to give more than in the control group. In future experimentation, we should continue to incorporate donor goals into our fundraising campaigns. By setting specific, attainable donor goals, donors are more likely to feel their contributions will make a difference and be more motivated to give. Additionally, breaking the overall donor goal into smaller amounts can make it seem more achievable and encourage more donors to participate. Overall, the success of this experiment demonstrates the importance of setting donor goals and providing donors with a clear understanding of how their contributions can make an impact. This strategy can be utilized in future fundraising efforts to drive donor engagement and increase donations for The Navigators.

experiments.title: How Removing the $30 Premium Gift Threshold on a Marriage Offer decreases conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the $30 minimum gift requirement and offering the premium for a gift of any amount for donors visiting the “reconnected” donation page will achieve increased donation conversion rates because eliminating the minimum requirement lowers the psychological barrier to entry and makes it easier for more couples to say yes.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/12/2026 - 01/20/2026
Focus on the Family’s “Reconnected” content offer is designed to help married couples strengthen and restore their relationships. The donation page includes a premium incentive: a free resource written by the guest speaker featured in the video series.
Prior to this test, donors were required to give a gift of $30 or more to receive the book. While this threshold helped anchor the value of the premium, we questioned whether it might also discourage participation in any donation.
Because this offer often reaches people during a vulnerable or reflective moment in their marriage, we wanted to understand whether reducing the perceived cost of entry would invite more donors to give.
This ran as a split-url test on the donation page, with a 50 – 50 traffic split. The control noted that you’ll receive the book with a gift of $30 or more, while the treatment offered the premium with a gift of any amount. No imagery, giving form, or other copy was changed. Most visitors arrived to this page from meta advertising with strong messaging about the importance of reconnecting with your spouse today.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Removing the $30 minimum gift requirement significantly decreased conversion rate, resulting in nearly half as many donors compared to the control. This result reached 99.1% statistical confidence, indicating a reliably negative effect. The treatment also predictably dropped average gift size, falling from $34.20 to $16.18.
For this particular audience, the $30 threshold acted as a signal of value and commitment. Rather than feeling like a barrier, the minimum gift amount may have:
Reinforced that the content and premium were valuable Framed the gift as an intentional act of support, rather than a casual transaction Helped donors feel confident about what gift size is appropriate
By removing that threshold, the page may have unintentionally weakened urgency and reframed the gift as being less impactful.
In future experimentation, its important to consider the perceived value of the premium incentive in relation to its audience. This test performed well on another offer named "Proof you Need to Believe in Jesus" about scientific proof for religious believers. And previous testing with that offer has indicated that those visitors are more motivated by curiosity and conviction, where the gift feels like access to strengthening their beliefs, with a low barrier to entry.
For emotionally resonant content like this offer, donors may respond better to clear expectations, ensuring them that this resource is trustworthy and meaningful. Lowering the premium requirements might have reduced both participation and revenue because of the loss of perceived value in this case.

experiments.title: How decreasing the recommended gift amount also decreased conversion rate on a relationship content offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that lowering the recommended gift amount from $50 to $30 for visitors of the “Healing Parent and Adult Child Relationships”  content offer donation page will achieve an increased donation conversion rate because a lower default ask reduces psychological pressure, making it easier for more people to say yes.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/12/2026 - 01/20/2026
Focus on the Family is running a video series content offer called “Healing Parent and Adult Child Relationships.” This donation page includes a premium incentive: donors who give $30 or more will receive a copy of the book featured in the video series, and written by the guest speaker.
At the time of this test, the donation page featured a $50 “popular” gift amount, even though the premium threshold was $30. This created a potential mismatch between what donors were being told, and what the page implicitly suggested was the most appropriate gift.
We identified this as a possible source of friction before considering a GOAA (gift of any amount) test for this same page, so we wanted to address this misalignment first. If donors were to notice this misalignment when intending to receive the premium, they might question whether $30 was truly sufficient, or feel uncertain about which amount was expected.
This ran as a split-url test on the donation page, with a 50 – 50 traffic split. The control noted $50 as a popular gift amount, while the treatment noted $30 as popular. No copy, imagery, or premium messaging was changed. Most visitors arrived to this page from meta advertising with strong messaging about the importance of reconnecting with your child before its too late.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Aligning the suggested gift amount with the $30 premium threshold significantly decreased donation conversion rate, resulting in 66% fewer donors compared to the control. This outcome was statistically significant at 99.9% confidence, indicating a clear and meaningful negative impact.
Though the gift array appeared misaligned to the premium ask, the data suggests that it functioned as a strong value and intent signal.
Even with a $30 premium clearly stated elsewhere on the page, the higher default likely:
Anchored donors to a more serious level of commitmentReinforced the perceived value of the contentReduced ambiguity about what constituted a meaningful gift
By shifting the default to $30, the page may have unintentionally reframed the experience as more transactional (giving to get the book) rather than mission driven (giving to support their work), weakening donor motivation.
This result highlights that clarity is not always the primary driver of motivation. In some contexts, the donor may be able to ignore small inconsistencies if the overall framing of the donation ask signals strong importance and impact.
For future testing, we should keep in mind the type of content we are testing on. For emotionally resonant or mission-driven content, a higher default may increase confidence and commitment, rather than deter giving. If audiences feel compelled enough by the messaging of the offer, lowering the barrier may not be the best approach.

experiments.title: Will a specific resource in a dear reader drive more engagement than a general offer?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a Plugged In-branded resource in a Dear Reader for visitors to Plugged In’s review webpages will achieve an increase in email sign-ups because people are more likely to engage with a resource when it is closely aligned to the content they’re already consuming. because people are more likely to engage with a resource when it is closely aligned to the content they’re already consuming.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/16/2025
To capture more email subscribers, the team added a “Dear Reader” prompt at the bottom of review pages offering a free resource. The goal was to test whether visitors would be more motivated by a resource directly tied to Plugged In or a broader  tool.
Two offers were tested:
Control: A general video series, Praying the Scriptures Over Your Children.
Treatment: A Plugged In–branded ebook guide featuring curated, family-friendly movie picks.
The test explored whether contextual relevance — aligning the offer with the content just consumed — would increase conversions.
We split the website traffic 50/50 between the control and treatment, and ran this test for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While both offers generated equal click-through rates from the “Dear Reader” prompt, the general series led to significantly more email sign-ups than the Plugged In–branded movie guide.
The control offer produced 49 sign-ups, compared to 29 sign-ups for the Plugged In ebook — that's a 41.4% decrease for the treatment, with a statistically valid confidence level of 97.8%
 	From what we can see in the data, the click rates on the Dear Reader itself were identical between the Control and the Treatment, indicating equal interest in the offers initially. The drop-off occurred at the conversion stage, suggesting differences in how compelling or valuable the offers felt to users.
 	Interestingly, users who signed up for the Plugged In ebook were more likely to go on to donate, even though total sign-ups were lower.
 	7 donations followed the Control offer; 6 followed the Treatment — not a statistically significant difference.
 	Average gift was higher for the Treatment ($52 vs $40), after removing a $5,000 outlier from the Treatment.
 	No significant difference in recurring vs one-time gifts.
more motivated or mission-aligned subset of users
the parenting-focused  offer had broader appeal and stronger top-of-funnel performance
emotional depth and perceived value

experiments.title: Testing Form Friction: Will Requiring a Phone Number on a Content Offer Landing Page Hurt Sign-Ups from Facebook Traffic?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that requiring a mobile phone number on the sign-up form for a Facebook video series acquisition offer will result in a small drop in conversions but more valuable contacts because motivated users will tolerate the added friction in exchange for high-value content.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2025 - 11/11/2025
Focus on the Family has been working to grow their SMS program and wanted to test the impact of requiring a mobile phone number on a content sign-up form.
We decided to test this on a free video series titled Contagious Joy, which was being promoted on Facebook with acquisition ads. Visitors who clicked the ad would land on a sign-up page with a form asking for First Name, Last Name, and Email Address.
In the Treatment version of the page, a fourth required field—Mobile Phone Number—was added. All other elements of the page remained the same.
The test was set up as a split URL experiment, with 50% of traffic directed to the control version and 50% to the treatment.
This test aimed to answer a key question: How much friction can we introduce in the name of better data collection—without meaningfully hurting conversion rates?
We did expect a potential drop in sign-ups due to the added field but hypothesized that the value of acquiring phone numbers would outweigh the slight loss in volume.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results we saw were surprising. We had anticipated a slight drop in email conversions, but didn’t expect it to be as significant as it was. Requiring a phone number led to a 26.4% decrease in email sign-ups, with a 99.9% level of confidence—clear evidence that this added field created too much friction for users coming from Facebook ads.
We also saw an almost statistically significant 40% drop in transactions on the post-sign-up donation page (92.2% LOC), suggesting the impact extended beyond the initial form. While average gift amount and recurring donor conversion didn’t change, the decrease in overall donor volume is still meaningful.
For this acquisition audience, the added requirement of a phone number appeared to outweigh the perceived value of the free video series. This test shows that asking for too much, too soon—especially from new visitors—can hurt both lead generation and potential revenue.
Going forward, it may be more effective to collect phone numbers later in the journey such as on the donation page, once trust has been built or value has been delivered. Or test adding the Phone Number field on the sign-up page but do not make it required to try and reduce friction.

experiments.title: How defaulting a form to monthly & adding a monthly callout affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting the donation form to Monthly giving and adding a clear, impact-focused callout for users on acquisition offer donation pages will achieve an increase in monthly donor conversion because default bias nudges people toward the pre-selected choice, while a tangible impact message increases perceived efficacy and reduces decision friction—making monthly giving feel both easier and more meaningful.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2025 - 08/29/2025
An organization uses free video series offers, promoted primarily on Facebook and Instagram, to acquire new subscribers and donors. These campaigns have recently become highly efficient at driving new support, as optimizations significantly lowered the gross cost per donor (GCPD) and increased the volume of one-time donors acquired.
With this success, the team sought to improve the quality of donor acquisition by increasing the number of recurring donors acquired through this same channel. The key question: could making monthly giving more visually and textually prominent on the donation form encourage more people to begin a recurring commitment at the point of acquisition?
The Control used the standard acquisition donation form, which defaulted to a $30 one-time gift and contained no additional monthly-specific messaging.
The Treatment donation form was identical in design and copy but defaulted to $30 monthly giving. It also added a callout above the form that read:
“Would you consider a monthly donation? Your monthly gift of $30 will enable 50 people every month to get signed up for this free video series.”
The test ran across all active video series acquisition donation pages for 10 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Defaulting the donation form to Monthly and adding an impact-focused callout led to a 187.7% increase in monthly gifts (99.9% confidence), showing that defaults and impact framing can strongly influence recurring donor acquisition. The average gift also increased slightly ($33 → $36), though not with statistical confidence.

At the same time, one-time gifts decreased by 34.2% (99.9% confidence). However, this is because many of the people who would have given a one-time donation chose to give a monthly gift instead.

The takeaway: monthly defaults can meaningfully grow recurring donors but risk suppressing one-time gifts. Whether this is a net positive depends on the lifetime value of new monthly donors compared to the lost one-time gifts.

Future tests could evaluate long-term revenue impact, try highlighting monthly giving without changing the default, or explore post-donation monthly upgrade prompts.

experiments.title: How clarifying the perception of the problem on a main donation page impacted revenue and donor conversions

rows.name: Treatment - Improved Perception of the Problem

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that strengthening the perception of the problems that the organization is working to solve for visitors to the main donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because urgent, problem-oriented framing enhances perceived relevance and impact.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/18/2025 - 07/09/2025
Focus on the Family’s main donation page presents a high-leverage opportunity for revenue growth—if we can identify changes that lift donor conversion. Despite this potential, nearly every prior test on the page has either validated a decrease or yielded no significant improvement.
This experiment aimed to break that pattern by testing a new value proposition. The control copy offered a general appeal to “empower families with biblical resources,” while the treatment made the problem more vivid and urgent. It introduced specific pain points families face—financial strain, broken communication, cultural confusion, and spiritual drift—and positioned the donor’s gift as a direct response to these needs.
The test ran on the primary donation page for users who clicked the main “Donate” button in site navigation. This was our first test in a planned series of experiments to refine how FOTF frames its impact—and to better understand what kind of value proposition most effectively motivates donor action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test showed that deepening the emotional urgency and specificity of the value proposition can meaningfully shift donor behavior—particularly in prompting recurring giving.
The treatment, which emphasized the family crisis and FOTF’s role in meeting those needs, lifted overall donor conversion by 11.9% at an 82.4% level of confidence. While not at the desired 95% validation threshold, these results show that the reframed value proposition resonates more strongly with site visitors.
The most notable shift came in recurring gifts, where the treatment produced a 248.7% lift at a 97.1% confidence level. This indicates that donors exposed to the new framing may not only be more likely to give—but more likely to commit long-term. This behavioral shift could reflect an increased sense of urgency, credibility, or alignment with the organization’s long-term mission.
Interestingly, we observed a -3.8% decrease in one-time gifts, statistically validated at 98.4% confidence. However, the higher average gift size ($56.42 vs. $47.03) and greater total revenue ($51.6k vs. $42k) from the treatment suggest a net positive financial outcome, despite the slight decline in single transactions.
What does this mean?
The results point toward the power of emotionally resonant, problem-focused messaging to increase commitment and revenue—even if it slightly narrows broad appeal. Future tests might explore how to preserve or recover one-time volume while building on the strong gains in recurring giving and total revenue.Next steps: We will test this winning treatment language against a new version of the donation page copy that approaches the value proposition from an identity-based angle, to see which version works best for converting page visitors into donors

experiments.title: How does adding an exclusive offers slide-up to a landing page impact email acquisition rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by presenting users with additional video series options in a slide-up, they will be more likely to find content that resonates with them, which will lead to a higher email acquisition rate. By offering more choices, we expect to reduce friction in the decision-making process and increase the likelihood that users will opt in with their email addresses.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2025 - 03/24/2025
We hypothesized by providing visitors with alternative video series options, visitors would more likely to find content that resonates with them, thereby increasing the likelihood of signing up. The test was not about determining which series resonated more with people but rather whether offering additional options would lead to more email sign-ups.
To test this, we implemented an A/B test in VWO, splitting traffic evenly (50/50) between the Control and Treatment. The Control version of the landing page remained unchanged, while the Treatment featured a slide-up at the bottom of the page displaying three alternative video series options. We tested this on “Proof You Need,” their most successful video series we are running on Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran for 18 days, during which the control acquired 1,249 emails and the treatment acquired 1,155 emails. This resulted in a 6.8% decrease in email acquisition for the Treatment at a 95.6% level of confidence. While we initially hypothesized that offering more options would increase conversions, the results suggest that the slide-up may have unintentionally introduced decision friction. Users who arrived on the landing page were likely already primed to sign up based on the ad they clicked, but the presence of additional choices may have disrupted that intent, leading to hesitation rather than action. Instead of reinforcing their decision, the added options could have created uncertainty, ultimately reducing the overall conversion rate.

Based on these results, we recommend maintaining the current landing page design without the slide-up to avoid introducing additional friction in the sign-up process.

experiments.title: How should we layout the episode pages?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the featured resource CTA higher on the page for the broadcast episode pages will achieve more traffic getting to the donation page because the CTA is more visible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2025 - 05/02/2025
Each day tons of people go to their broadcast pages to pick through the episodes that they would like to listen to. On the episode pages you can watch the show, read more about the guests, and then towards the bottom of the page you have the opportunity to get the resources that they talked about on the show.
The resources offer leds the user to a donation page where they can get the resource with a gift. We thought, if we rearrange the page to make the resource CTA more at the top we could drive more traffic to the donation page and get more transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that moving the featured resource CTA higher on the broadcast episode pages significantly increased the traffic to the donation page. The treatment group, which had the CTA placed higher on the page, saw an impressive 18.9% increase in CTA button clicks for all traffic compared to the control group. This difference was statistically significant at a confidence level of 100.0%, indicating that the results are very reliable.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the placement of CTAs on the page to maximize engagement and conversion rates. Additionally, it would be beneficial to further analyze user behavior to understand why the higher placement of the CTA led to more clicks. This insight can help in optimizing the design and placement of CTAs across other pages on the website as well.

Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of CTA placement in driving user action. By utilizing this learning and continuing to test and optimize CTAs, Focus on the Family can further improve user engagement and increase donations on their broadcast episode pages.

experiments.title: Will Adding 3rd Party Validation Images Strengthen Trust on the Donation Page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding 3rd party validation images on the episodic donation page for users to see when they are about to click “donate” will achieve more transactions because the images will reinforce trust and credibility.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2025 - 04/21/2025
For this test, we wanted to see if we could increase transactions on the main episodic donation page by adding 3rd party validator images. These included organizations that partners with the organization.
We added these partnership icons to the bottom of the page right under the donate button where they could reinforce trust right when the user is about to click submit.
Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding 3rd party validation images on the episodic donation page did not increase transactions as hypothesized. In fact, it resulted in a 15.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic with a statistical level of confidence at 95.1%.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of changes to the donation page before implementing them. It is possible that the placement of the images may not have been optimal, as the user as to scroll past the donate button to even see the images, especially on mobile.

We also looked at these results from a new vs. returning user and found that the majority of traffic to this page was from new visitors. Our hypothesis would be that new visitors would be more moved by adding the 3rd party images that those who are returning. However, the data says new visitors are 19% less likely to give at a 98% LoC when the images are added. The majority of new visitors are coming in from a mobile or tablet device where the user would have to scroll past the point of decision, the donate button, to see the images.

experiments.title: How defaulting to a recurring gift on an acquisition donation page, Living with Contagious Joy, impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a recurring gift and emphasizing its broader impact for the donors will increase the number of recurring donations because it presents a manageable commitment and highlights the significance of ongoing support, encouraging more donors to commit to long-term giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/25/2025 - 04/10/2025
To increase recurring donations, we tested defaulting to a recurring gift option on the donation page for the high-traffic “Living with Contagious Joy” acquisition funnel, an ideal setting to measure the impact of this default on donor conversion.
We hypothesized that defaulting to a recurring gift option and emphasizing the impact of monthly donations would increase the number of donors choosing recurring contributions, ultimately boosting donor retention and long-term support for the mission.
For this iteration, we configured a VWO Split URL test with a new control and a treatment page, each receiving 50% of traffic. This setup ensured users blocking VWO redirects were excluded, preventing data distortion in the control group and yielding more accurate results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for 17 days and tested the impact of defaulting to a recurring gift option on the donation page within the “Living with Contagious Joy” acquisition funnel.

While the treatment successfully increased recurring transactions by 313.8% with a 98.2% level of confidence, it also led to a 45.2% decrease in one-time donations, resulting in an overall 33% drop in total transactions. This indicates that although more donors opted for monthly giving when it was pre-selected, the change significantly discouraged one-time donations.

Given these results, the test highlights a valuable trade-off: optimizing for recurring giving can improve long-term donor value, but may reduce total donor acquisition in the short term. Future testing could explore ways to elevate the visibility and value of monthly giving —such as using stronger messaging, layout hierarchy, or design emphasis. Additionally, tracking the retention and lifetime value of these newly acquired monthly donors will be critical to understanding if the short-term loss in total transactions is outweighed by long-term donor value.

experiments.title: Will longer copy help donors convert on a lower third slide up?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more copy value proposition for the lower third slide up will achieve more transactions because the additional value prop will provide social proof and clarity into what their gift is doing.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/13/2025
Focus on the Family is a global ministry that is a well recognized brand. They drive tons of traffic to their site daily.
Right now, they are in the middle of a sustainer drive. To support this campaign we added what we call a ‘lower third’ slideup element to their content pages. We wanted to see if long or short copy worked best on the these pages to convert donors. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. Long copy, which includes a value proposition for the lower third slideup, was more effective in converting donors compared to short copy.

2. The additional value proposition provided social proof and clarity, which likely contributed to the higher donation rate.

3. The treatment resulted in a significant increase in donations, with a 98.3% level of confidence that the results were not due to random chance.

In future experimentation, we should:

1. Continue testing different copy lengths and value propositions to further optimize donation conversions.

2. Consider incorporating social proof and clear explanations of how donations will be used in our messaging.

3. Regularly analyze and track the performance of different elements to identify what resonates best with our audience and drives the most meaningful actions.

experiments.title: How defaulting to a recurring gift on an acquisition donation page, Proof You Need, impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a recurring gift and emphasizing its broader impact for Focus on the Family donors will increase the number of recurring donations because it presents a manageable commitment and highlights the significance of ongoing support, encouraging more donors to commit to long-term giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/05/2025 - 03/05/2025
Focus on the Family is a nonprofit organization dedicated to providing guidance and resources to support families. To increase recurring donations, we tested defaulting to a recurring gift option on the donation page for the high-traffic “Proof You Need” acquisition funnel, an ideal setting to measure the impact of this default on donor conversion.
We hypothesized that defaulting to a recurring gift option and emphasizing the impact of monthly donations would increase the number of donors choosing recurring contributions, ultimately boosting donor retention and long-term support for Focus on the Family’s mission.
This experiment was previously tested from October 16 – November 12, with the original test defaulting to a $15 recurring gift. The results showed that defaulting to recurring significantly increased recurring donations—the treatment group, which defaulted to recurring, had 34 conversions compared to just 8 in the control group, marking a 330.0% increase in recurring donations at a 99.9% confidence level. Based on these results, we initially moved forward with defaulting other pages to recurring gifts. However, as we continued monitoring performance, we observed lower ROIs and encountered limitations in the donation form’s gift array, which restricted customization for one-time and monthly gift amounts. As a result, we ultimately reverted the default back to a one-time gift.
Towards the end of the year, we saw a noticeable increase in sustainer giving, prompting us to revisit this test.
For this iteration, we configured a VWO Split URL test with a new control and a variation, each receiving 50% of traffic. This setup ensured users blocking VWO redirects were excluded, preventing data distortion in the control group and yielding more accurate results.
Control: Defaulted to a one-time gift, with language asking for a one-time donation. The gift array included “$30, $50, $100, and $200,” with no pre-selected amount. The copy specifically asked for $30, while $50 was highlighted as a “popular” gift option. Note: No changes were made to the current live pages for Proof You Need for the control group.
Treatment: Defaulted to a recurring gift, with a gift array of “$15, $30, $50, and $100,” with $30 pre-selected.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Through this experiment, we observed that defaulting to a recurring gift option significantly increased recurring donations. The treatment group, which defaulted to recurring gifts, had 17 more recurring transactions than the control, resulting in an 830.7% increase in recurring transactions at a 100% confidence level.
However, overall transactions decreased slightly, with the treatment group converting 122 donations versus 141 in the control group. Revenue for the control was $5,520, while the treatment generated $4,470. Despite this initial decline in immediate revenue, the additional 17 recurring donors in the treatment group, with an average gift of $30 and an 85% retention rate, project to generate an additional $5,202 in revenue over time—demonstrating the long-term value of sustaining donors.
The decrease in overall transactions is likely due to the shift from one-time gifts to recurring donations. While immediate revenue per visitor declined, this result aligns with previous findings that indicate the long-term value of recurring donors outweighs short-term losses.
Next Steps
Default to Recurring Gifts for This Offer: Given the strong lift in recurring transactions and projected long-term revenue, we will permanently default this offer to recurring gifts.Test on Another Offer (Living with Contagious Joy): To further validate these findings, we will run a similar test on another offer, "Living with Contagious Joy," to assess whether the impact holds across different campaigns.Refine Messaging: Continue optimizing the way we present the recurring gift option to further encourage commitment while minimizing any deterrent effect on one-time donations.Monitor Long-Term Donor Impact: Track retention and lifetime value of these new recurring donors to ensure the strategy continues to drive sustainable growth for Focus on the Family.

experiments.title: Will Adding an Additional Donor Countdown Sticky Bar Message Help Increase Donor Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding this additional sticky bar message to the campaign giving page for calendar year end will achieve an increase in donor conversion because we highlighted a specific need and deadline to reach the goal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2024 - 12/28/2024
Following the success of another sticky bar test during a campaign, we wanted to know if we would see like results during calendar year-end.
Previously, we saw a great response when added an additional sticky bar alert message across the top of the donation page that included a dynamic daily donor countdown and the specific date and time we needed to reach our campaign goal.
By adding this message, we believed we would see similar results during our calendar year-end campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

1. Adding an additional sticky bar message with a dynamic daily donor countdown and a specific deadline to the campaign giving page resulted in a significant increase in donor conversion.

2. The treatment group experienced a substantial 69.7% increase in Revenue for All Traffic compared to the control group.

3. The statistical significance level of 99.5% also indicates that the results are highly reliable and not due to chance.

Implications for Future Experimentation:

1. Highlighting a specific need and deadline to reach a campaign goal can effectively motivate donors to take action.

2. Using dynamic elements such as daily donor countdowns can create a sense of urgency and encourage donors to contribute.

3. Future experiments should consider incorporating similar strategies to drive donor conversion and revenue growth.

4. It is essential to continue testing and iterating on different messaging and design elements to optimize donation pages for maximum impact.

Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of clear communication, urgency, and specific goals in fundraising campaigns. By leveraging these learnings in future experimentation, this organization can continue to improve its donor conversion rates and ultimately achieve its campaign objectives more effectively.

experiments.title: How defaulting to a recurring gift on an acquisition donation page, Proof You Need, impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a recurring gift and emphasizing its broader impact for Focus on the Family donors will increase the number of recurring donations because it presents a manageable commitment and highlights the significance of ongoing support, encouraging more donors to commit to long-term giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/16/2024 - 11/12/2024
Focus on the Family is a nonprofit organization dedicated to providing guidance and resources to support families. To increase recurring donations, we tested defaulting to a recurring gift option on the donation page for the high-traffic “Proof You Need” acquisition funnel, an ideal setting to measure the impact of this default on donor conversion.
We hypothesize that defaulting to a recurring gift option and emphasizing the impact of monthly donations will increase the number of donors choosing recurring contributions, ultimately boosting donor retention and long-term support for Focus on the Family’s mission.
Due to technical constraints, we set this up as a split URL test. Following our development team’s guidance, we configured a VWO Split URL test with a new control and a variation, each receiving 50% of traffic, while the original control received 0%. This configuration allowed us to filter out users blocking VWO redirects, ensuring they didn’t affect the main control group and were excluded from results, yielding more accurate data for redirected pages.
In this test, the control page defaulted to a one-time gift, with language asking for a one-time donation. The treatment page defaulted to a recurring monthly gift of $15, with the gift array adjusted to include $15, $30, $50, and $100, with $15 pre-selected. The treatment page was adjusted to ask for a monthly gift.
We ran a similar test on another acquisition donation page, “Reconnected,” as a separate experiment using the same setup to compare results across different offers to determine if the type of offer would impact the results. You can review the results of that concurrent experiment here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Through this experiment, we observed that defaulting to a recurring gift option significantly increased recurring donations. The treatment group, which defaulted to recurring gifts, had 34 conversions compared to just 8 in the control group, marking a 330.0% increase in recurring donations at a 99.9% confidence level. However, overall transactions decreased by 15.1%, with the treatment group converting 157 donations versus 187 in the control group. Additionally, revenue per visitor dropped by 22.4%, but this result only reached a 5.3% level of confidence, indicating that the difference is not statistically significant and suggesting no substantial negative impact on immediate revenue.
The decrease in overall transactions is likely due to the shift from one-time gifts to recurring donations. While the average gift size was slightly lower in the treatment group ($31.16 vs. $33.76), recurring donors provide more sustainable support over time. The long-term value of recurring donations, as seen in similar experiments, outweighs the short-term decrease in revenue.
Next Steps:
Default to Recurring Gifts: We recommend that Focus on the Family default to recurring gifts on all acquisition donation pages to drive long-term donor retention and sustainable support.Refine Messaging: Continue testing variations of messaging that emphasize the broader impact of recurring donations to further encourage donor commitment.Optimize Recurring Gift Amounts: Experiment with different recurring gift amounts to determine which options are most attractive to donors and maximize recurring revenue.Monitor Long-Term Donor Impact: Track the performance of recurring donors to assess their long-term retention and contribution to Focus on the Family’s mission and goals.

experiments.title: How defaulting to recurring gift on an acquisition donation page, Reconnected, impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to a recurring gift and emphasizing its broader impact for Focus on the Family donors will increase the number of recurring donations because it presents a manageable commitment and highlights the significance of ongoing support, encouraging more donors to commit to long-term giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/16/2024 - 11/12/2024
Focus on the Family is a nonprofit organization dedicated to providing guidance and resources to support families. To increase recurring donations, we tested defaulting to a recurring gift option on the donation page for the high-traffic “Reconnected” acquisition funnel, an ideal setting to measure the impact of this default on donor conversion.
We hypothesize that defaulting to a recurring gift option and emphasizing the impact of monthly donations will increase the number of donors choosing recurring contributions, ultimately boosting donor retention and long-term support for Focus on the Family’s mission.
Due to technical constraints, we set this up as a split URL test. Following our development team’s guidance, we configured a VWO Split URL test with a new control and a variation, each receiving 50% of traffic, while the original control received 0%. This configuration allowed us to filter out users blocking VWO redirects, ensuring they didn’t affect the main control group and were excluded from results, yielding more accurate data for redirected pages.
In this test, the control page defaulted to a one-time gift, with copy asking for a one-time donation. The treatment page defaulted to a recurring monthly gift of $15, with the gift array adjusted to include $15, $30, $50, and $100, with $15 pre-selected. The treatment page was adjusted to ask for a monthly gift.
We ran a similar test on another acquisition donation page, “Proof You Need,” as a separate experiment using the same setup to compare results across different offers. You can review the results of that concurrent experiment here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In a similar experiment conducted on a different donation page, we found that defaulting to a recurring gift option led to a significant increase in recurring donations. On this page, the treatment group, which defaulted to recurring gifts, had 13 conversions compared to just 3 in the control group, marking a 348.0% increase in recurring donations at a 99.9% confidence level. This result mirrors the impact seen in the "Proof You Need" test, reinforcing the effectiveness of defaulting to recurring gifts.
However, overall transactions decreased in the treatment group, with 67 conversions compared to 89 in the control group, resulting in a 22.1% decrease in conversion rate at an 89% confidence level. Revenue per visitor also dropped by 50.4%, from $2.37 to $1.50 per visitor. This result, though significant, had a low confidence level of 1.28%, suggesting that the drop in revenue is not as pronounced as the figures suggest. The average gift size also decreased in the treatment group ($27.99 vs. $34.42), which aligns with the expected shift toward smaller, recurring donations.
Despite the decrease in overall transactions and average gift size, the surge in recurring donations points to the long-term value of building a recurring donor base. The increase in recurring donors highlights a sustainable revenue stream that will contribute to Focus on the Family’s mission over time, even though immediate revenue was lower.
We believe that defaulting to recurring gifts, along with messaging that emphasizes their broader impact, made it easier for donors to commit to ongoing support. The resulting increase in recurring donations suggests that the strategy can help Focus on the Family build stronger, longer-term relationships with donors.
Next Steps:
Default to Recurring Gifts: We recommend continuing to default to recurring gifts on other donation pages to foster long-term donor engagement.Refine Messaging: Test additional messaging variations focused on the impact of recurring gifts to further motivate donors.Optimize Recurring Gift Amounts: Experiment with different recurring gift amounts to find the most effective pricing structure for donors.Monitor Long-Term Donor Impact: Track the performance of recurring donors to assess their retention and contribution to Focus on the Family’s mission.

experiments.title: Will adding a ticker to the bottom of the page increase clicks to the donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a scrolling ticker tape on the bottom of the hompage for the Wait No More Campaign will achieve more clicks than the donate button because the rotating message will draw attention to the tape.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2024 - 11/01/2024
This organization runs several high urgency campaigns a year that draw a ton of traffic to their donation pages.
For this test, we wanted to see if there was a better tactic to get people to the donation pages rather that the usual sticky donate button or the main donate buttons in the navigation menu
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the scrolling ticker tape on the bottom of the homepage for the Wait No More Campaign significantly outperformed the traditional donate button in terms of driving clicks to the donation page.

Specifically, the treatment group saw a 66.5% increase in CTA Clicked for All Traffic with a 97.6% statistical level of confidence. Additionally, there was a 665% increase in CTA Clicked for All Traffic visitors with a 98% level of confidence.

These results indicate that the rotating message on the ticker tape successfully drew attention and prompted more visitors to click on the call-to-action, leading to a substantial increase in donation page visits.

experiments.title: Will expanding value proposition on the donation page increase donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition for the main episodic donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion because it will be able to better prime the user to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/30/2024 - 10/01/2024
The main episodic donation page has always converted donors really well with little to no value proposition copy. Over the last several years we have proposed and executed sever tests on the page and still, nothing will perform better than the little to no copy that they have on the page natively.
For this test, we wanted to see if somebody who is not involved in the Focus on the Family account could come in with fresh eyes and see where this page could see improvements.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding value proposition copy to the main episodic donation page for Focus on the Family actually led to a significant decrease in donor conversion rates. The treatment group, which received the new value proposition copy, saw an 80.5% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic, with a 100.0% level of confidence. Additionally, Transactions - One-Time experienced a 796% decrease with a 100% level of confidence.

Moving forward, it is important to recognize that sometimes less is more when it comes to donation pages. In this case, the original page with minimal to no value proposition copy performed better in converting donors. It's crucial to continually test and iterate on different elements of the page to find the most effective combination for donor conversion. Future experimentation should focus on refining other aspects of the page, such as design, layout, and call-to-action buttons, while keeping in mind the impact of adding additional copy.

experiments.title: Will Adding the Form Fields to the Bottom of Articles Increase Sign Up Rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the Winsome Ways video series sign up form directly to the article pages for readers of FOTF’s pro-life article content will achieve an increase in email conversions because we removed a step in the sign up process and visitors could see just how quickly they could get something of value from their in-article experience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/27/2024 - 09/23/2024
Focus on the Family has a very healthy amount of traffic going to their KMI article pages. Within those pages, they typically include 2 “go deeper” type resources. On the bottom of the page they use a universal CTA system that we used 1 of 6 within this test. By testing the pro-life KMI bottom of article CTA, we wanted to know if adding the video series content offer form for Sharing Pro-Life Views in Winsome Ways would out perform the typical image and click to a standard video series landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that by adding the Winsome Ways video series sign-up form directly to the pro-life article pages, we were able to significantly increase email acquisitions by 95.2% for all traffic. This result was achieved with a high level of statistical confidence at 99.9%.
In future experimentation, we should continue to explore ways to streamline the user experience and reduce steps in the sign-up process. By directly offering valuable content within the article pages, we can capture the interest of visitors more effectively and increase conversions. Additionally, we should consider incorporating similar direct sign-up forms or offers on other relevant article pages to further enhance engagement and drive email acquisitions.

experiments.title: What Drives More Urgency A Popup or a Wikipedia-style banner?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that switching the style of an ask for the article pages will achieve a higher conversion rate because the new element will draw more attention to it because users have not seen it before.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2024 - 07/02/2024
An organization has been using wiki bars on their article pages for several high urgency campaigns. After an element has been present for a while on the page “banner blindness” can become present and the element might catch the eye of the users for them to take action.
For this test we wanted to see if switching the element on the article pages could increase transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that switching the style of an ask for the article pages actually led to a decrease in transactions. Despite the hypothesis that the new element would draw more attention due to novelty, the treatment group experienced a 38.4% decrease in Transactions - One-Time for All Traffic compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 96.2% confidence level, indicating a high level of certainty.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of changing elements on the page, especially when it comes to high urgency campaigns like the ones run by this organization. It is crucial to balance the need for catching the users' attention with the risk of causing confusion or decreased engagement. Additionally, it is essential to continually monitor and evaluate the performance of different elements to ensure that any changes made are positively impacting the desired outcomes.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing assumptions and continuously learning from the results to inform and improve future strategies and campaigns.

experiments.title: How a blog-style cultivation email impacts email engagement and content consumption
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a longer-form blog-style cultivation email for email subscribers will achieve more of our content being consumed and better email engagement because it gives the value away without requiring any further action . because  it gives the value away without requiring any further action. because  it gives the value away without requiring any further action. because  it gives the value away without requiring any further action . because  it gives the value away without requiring any further action. because  it gives the value away without requiring any further action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2024 - 08/27/2024
In previous experimentation, we’ve learned that shorter emails win higher click rates, but that is for appeal-style emails. We were interested in testing this same concept in a cultivation email to see if the added value would lead to better results.
Since this is a cultivation email, the concept is to see if giving away the value in the email gets more people to consume our content vs. making them take an additional step to click through to the resource. The resource that we did link in both emails was not gated — it was a PDF.
The real objective here isn’t so easily measured, so the results of this test can’t rely totally on quantitative data. Because it’s not a metric-based a/b test, but an operational test we will be tracking email replies to help inform our learning.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The longer treatment email resulted in a 69.8% decrease in clicks. However, the goal of the test wasn't to get more email clicks. What we are looking to for success in this test is the qualitative data and the email reply rate. Version A had just 1 email reply (.01% reply rate), while Version B had 7 email replies (.07% reply rate). The email replies to Version B all provided positive feedback about the blog-style format. Things to note include:
They appreciated the tips being given away right in the emailThey liked the plain-text design, formatting, and length of the contentThe advice was timelyThey appreciated not having to complete a form or take another step
Other than that, there were no significant metric differences between the two email versions, so we can conclude that the treatment version did not negatively impact anything.
Because of the positive feedback received regarding the blog-style email, we will begin to implement this email format into the mix for our weekly cultivation emails.

rows.name: The true meaning of NIO Summit

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more direct subject line for email subscribers will achieve a higher open rate  because it’s clear what information they will find in the email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2024 - 08/02/2024
Prior to our NIO Summit 2024 flash sale, we sent out an email to give our subscribers a heads-up about the upcoming sale in case they needed to get budget approval.
In an attempt to get higher visibility on the sale, we tested the subject line to see if a more direct subject line would increase email opens.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a more direct subject line for this type of NIO Summit email can significantly increase open rates, as demonstrated by the 21.1% increase in opens with a 100.0% level of statistical confidence.
This indicates that subscribers are more likely to open an email when they have a clear understanding of the information they will find inside.
Furthermore, the treatment email also resulted in a 678% increase in Clicks with a 100% level of confidence and a 386% increase in Clicks to Opens with a 95% level of confidence.
This shows that not only did the more direct subject line improve open rates, but it also drove more engagement and actions downstream from subscribers.
The treatment subject line also creates a more intense sense of urgency while also not giving everything away. It creates a sense of mystery — "What's the announcement?"
So, moving on from this experiment, we will continue testing ways to create more direct and urgent subject lines that clearly articulate the message in the email without giving it all away.

experiments.title: How ‘skipping the form’ affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing one-click access to a free resource will lift click rates among subscribers because removing the sign-up process will demonstrate respect for their time and leverage trust to deliver as much value as possible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/31/2024 - 07/31/2024
We always aim to improve user experience (UX) and deliver maximum value, especially to our subscribers. One way to create a better UX is to reduce friction, which is anything that makes taking the desired action more difficult.
But too often, we consider friction only from the viewpoint of how it impacts us and not our subscribers — what we should remember is that when someone abandons, they are also missing out on something of value.
So we wanted to know what would happen if we removed the sign-up process and maximized the value we delivered to our subscribers by respecting their time and providing one-click access to the resource we were sharing.
In the control, subscribers were sent to a landing page where they would complete a form to access the desired PDF. In the treatment, subscribers were sent directly to the desired PDF when they clicked the email link – no form fill required.
The only change in the email was a line that emphasized that the subscriber would not have to fill out a form to access the resource. Traffic was split ~50/50 between the treatment and control.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Linking directly to the resource and skipping the form fill produced a statistically valid 28.5% increase in click-through rates (CTR).
Plus, while all 300 subscribers who clicked the treatment successfully accessed the desired resource, only about 18% of those who clicked the control completed the registration process (93), meaning the treatment was able to get the resource into the hands of 222.6% more subscribers. While we can certainly work to improve the landing page's conversion rate, achieving a 100% conversion rate is unthinkable.
Our subscribers win because they don't have to complete another form (they're already subscribers, we don't need to collect their contact information again and again), and we win because the treatment allowed us to deliver far more value to our audience, helping us fulfill our mission and driving toward better business outcomes—win/win.
The treatment version streamlined the user experience (UX) by eliminating the need for users to fill out forms, resulting in a significantly higher CTR. This indicates that simplifying processes for users can lead to a more positive response.
We are currently testing this concept with cold audiences and hope to share our learnings soon.
One additional thing to consider is how we will handle this in terms of contact scoring. But that is a solvable 'back-end' problem that is worth working through to deliver as much value as possible.

experiments.title: Will reducing the size of the express checkout button on the homepage donation page increase donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by reducing the size of the Express Checkout button for visitors to our homepage donation page will achieve a decrease in donor conversion friction because of the decreased size of the Express Checkout button competing for the donor’s attention while completing the donation process. because of the decreased size of the Express Checkout button competing for the donor’s attention while completing the donation process. because of the decreased size of the Express Checkout button competing for the donor’s attention while completing the donation process. because of the decreased size of the Express Checkout button competing for the donor’s attention while completing the donation process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2024 - 03/05/2024
The team have been doing some testing on the Express Checkout opportunity on various groupings of their giving pages. The team had just finished a test on removing Express Checkout on the main homepage donation page and the findings were very flat, but we wondered what would happen if we reduced the size of the buttons instead.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment on reducing the size of the Express Checkout button on the homepage donation page for Focus on the Family include:

1. **Increased Transactions:** The treatment group experienced a significant 21.3% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control group. This indicates that the smaller size of the Express Checkout button helped reduce conversion friction and encourage more visitors to complete their donations.

2. **Significant Confidence Level:** The experiment achieved a high statistical level of confidence at 95.7%, meaning that the results are reliable and not likely due to random chance.

3. **Significant Impact on One-Time Transactions:** The treatment group also saw a remarkable 229% increase in One-Time Transactions with a 96% confidence level. This suggests that the smaller size of the button not only increased overall transactions but also influenced more one-time donations.

Based on these findings, future experimentation for Focus on the Family's donation pages could consider implementing smaller-sized Express Checkout buttons as a strategy to improve conversion rates and encourage more donations. Further testing could delve into the impact of button size on recurring donations, donor retention, and overall user experience to continuously optimize the donation process and enhance fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How does “opening a loop” in the subject line affect open rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an ‘open loop’ subject line will increase open rates by piquing people’s curiosity and creating a desire to ‘close the loop’ by finding out more about the update to which it alluded.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/20/2024
Since the primary job of a subject line is to win the open, we wanted to know if opening a loop of curiosity could improve open rates by giving less away. The control in this experiment directly stated what was inside the email, while the treatment merely mentioned an update.
The idea was that by piquing someone’s curiosity, or ‘opening a loop,’ the human desire to close that loop by finding out more about the update would prompt more people to open. Traffic was split roughly 50/50 between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Using an 'open loop' subject line produced a statistically valid 18.3% increase in open rates. By teasing an update without giving away specific information, people are more likely to be intrigued and feel the need to open the email to discover what the update is. This tactic taps into the human desire to close open loops and can be a powerful tool for increasing engagement.
The CTR of the treatment was lower (18.8%), but the sample size was insufficient to validate that outcome, so we will reserve drawing conclusions in that regard. In future experimentation, we should continue to test the effectiveness of curiosity-driven subject lines and test against other concepts. We will also continue monitoring the impact on click-through rates. We should also consider if the length of the subject line (the treatment being shorter) played a part in this result.
Finally, there is probably a more effective way to run this test while mitigating the risk of diminished click-through rates by teasing the subject matter of the email without giving the content away, I.e., "This will boost donations" etc.

experiments.title: How using a ‘reverse funnel’ design that places the form above the fold affects email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving an email acquisition form above the fold will increase sign-up rates by capitalizing on the momentum created by earlier steps in the funnel and giving the user the option to read more if they choose.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
If we’re being honest, we don’t think a prospect should have to read several paragraphs of copy to see the value in exchanging their email address for a content offer. It’s generally accepted that 8 out of 10 people will read your headline/subheadline and nothing else, which means your “above-the-fold” copy is, without a doubt, far and away, the most important copy on the page.
Knowing this and working forward from the assumption that someone had to land on our landing page from somewhere — presumably some form of marketing communication that has already, at least, teased the offer …
We wanted to test what would happen if we seized the existing funnel momentum to drive the conversion by placing the form above the fold (directly beneath the headline and subheadline) and moving the ‘explainer’ copy down the page where a user could scroll to read if they so desired.
The treatment kept the headline and subhead copy unchanged and simply moved the body copy ‘below the fold’ where people could continue reading if they wanted to know more about the offer.
**Important note: although they do not show up in the renders below, both the control and the treatment featured 3D renders of the asset being promoted in the upper righthand space of the page
experiments.keyLearnings:
Moving the form above the fold resulted in a statistically valid 56.3% lift in email acquisition rates.
By giving people the option to 'fast-track' the download process, we were able to see more conversions. Importantly, this test is in no way meant to suggest that 'less copy is better.' We did not remove the copy; we simply moved it down the page so that people could forego additional reading if they were already 'sold.'
What this test does show is that your headline and subhead should be strong enough to carry the conversion on their own and that placing the form above the fold may create a more frictionless experience for enough users to make a significant difference in conversion rates.
It also shows the importance of analyzing each asset on its own and as part of the broader funnel. Avoid repeating the same message in your ad, email, landing page, donation, page, etc. Consider the momentum your other awesome assets have already created and capitalize on that to drive the conversion!
In future experimentation, we will focus on testing the headline, subhead, button copy, and any other above-the-fold elements, as this test shows that 'above-the-fold optimization' likely has an outsized impact on conversion rates

experiments.title: How using simplified, invitational ad creative for a video series ad increased clicks to the landing page

rows.name: NA | Kingdom Mindset, normal ads - PO (March 2024)
NA | Kingdom Mindset, simple ads - PO (March 2024)

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a simplified style of ad creative for video series ads on Facebook will achieve an increase in clicks to the landing page because the ads are intriguing and make the reader want to visit the landing page to learn more. because  the ads are intriguing and make the reader want to visit the landing page to learn more.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2024 - 04/27/2024
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a large Christian ministry organization that creates and shares free resources like video series to encourage families. They promote these free video series on Facebook and wanted to see if using a simplified, “invitation” style of ad creative would lead to more clicks to the video series landing page.
For this experiment, we tested their standard style of ad creative against a version that had fewer design elements and was written to sound more like an invitation from the speaker in the video series, rather than an informal ad from an organization.
Our hypothesis is that the simple invitation ad design will increase clicks because it is more personal and engaging.
We ran this as an A/B campaigns test in Facebook with the ad budget split evenly. Both ad variants used the same text and CTA button, the only variable was the ad creative, and we ran this test for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this test for one month, we found that the simple, invitational ad creative increased in clicks by 46.2%, with a level of confidence (LOC) of 100%. This indicates that this result is valid and that we'd get a similar result if we tested this again.
Aside from clicks, the treatment ads also resulted in a 26% increase in email sign-ups for the video series, with a 99.9% LOC. This shows us that the simple, invitation style of ad creative didn't just motivate people to click on the ads. It also made them more motivated to sign up for the series!
Our key learnings from this experiment are:
The simplified style of ad creative, designed to sound like a personal invitation, led to a significant increase in clicks and email sign-ups. This indicates that the treatment ad was more effective in engaging and attracting viewers to take action.Testing this kind of simple invitation ad with Focus on the Family's other video series offers may lead to similar results, but each series offer is different and more testing is needed to have a more definitive answer.

experiments.title: How reducing the length of the primary ad text on a content offer Facebook ad impacts downstream donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the primary ad text for a free content offer Facebook ad will achieve an increase in clicks and downstream donations because it will reduce friction for the user.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/11/2024
Focus on the Family uses free video series content offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. These ads rely on compelling ad imagery and lengthy ad copy promoting the series and explaining its value.
We aimed to determine if using a compelling image with shorter ad copy would result in greater engagement, more clicks, and increased donations. The hypothesis was that shorter copy would reduce friction, allowing the video series landing page to effectively communicate the offer’s value and convert traffic.
We developed a new campaign for their “Cultural Deceptions” video series, using the same imagery as our control campaign but with abbreviated primary ad text. The revised ad included just a single sentence: “Check out best-selling author and apologist Alisa Childer’s new free video series, “God’s Truth Amidst Cultural Deceptions,” to uncover the lies behind some of today’s most popular social media slogans.”
We split the campaign traffic using Facebook’s AB testing feature and conducted the test over 12 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our key learning from this experiment was that shortening the length of the primary ad text on our free video series offer Facebook ads led to a significant decrease in donations for the "Cultural Deceptions" video series campaign.
The treatment ads with the shorter primary ad text saw a 53.6% decrease in donations compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 99.9% level of confidence (LOC).
In future experimentation, it's essential to consider the impact of ad text on donation behavior. Our hypothesis assumed that shortening the primary ad text would reduce friction for the user and thus increase engagement. However, our results in this test showed the opposite effect. Instead of adding friction, the longer ad text in the Control ads provided important context for social audiences. Not only did we validate a decrease in donations, but also a 52.7% decrease in clicks with a 100% LOC.
However, there are a few ________
The cost per click was $0.12 cheaper on the short ads vs the long ads. 61% diff in CPM $4.73 instead of $12.06The GCPD was $3 less for the short
The results of this experiment show that longer, more detailed ad copy may be necessary to effectively communicate the value of the video series offer and encourage donations. While reducing friction for the user is crucial, it should not come at the expense of providing important information that can drive conversions. In future ads and testing, we will look at how to balance brevity and informativeness to optimize campaign performance.
