experiments.title: How sending a webinar invite from the host affects email click rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending a webinar invite from the host will increase click rates by humanizing the email and creating a feeling of liking.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/25/2024
We all know and love our Audience Engagement Manager, Riley, and so do our subscribers. But for this test, we wanted to know what would happen if we sent a webinar invite from the webinar’s host (Nathan), rather than our usual sender, (Riley).
Our previous tests have shown that the more we can humanize our emails (I.e., make them look and feel as much like a personal 1:1 email as possible), the better our engagement rates.
So we wondered if sending the email from the host would make the email seem more like a personal invitation and less like a promotion — and how this would affect engagement rates.
For this test, we split the traffic 50/50 with roughly one-half of subscribers receiving the invite from Riley (with “we” language) and the other half receiving the invite from Nathan (with “I” language).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Sending the email from the webinar's host produced a 31.7% statistically valid lift in email click rates, smashing the control.

So this seems like a clear-cut learning -- by sending the email from the webinar's host, we humanized the interaction and earned a bunch more clicks, end of story, right? ... not so fast, my friend!

As we dug deeper into the data, we discovered an unexpected incongruency in the click rates and registration rates between the control and the treatment.

While the treatment (from the host) produced a significant 32% increase in clicks, it ultimately led to a 167% decrease in sign-up rates.

This one's a head-scratcher. Considering that the changes to the treatment were tightly aligned with our hypothesis, and no other changes were made (either to the email or the following landing page), we would not expect to see the treatment produce such a large lift in click rates but such a gigantic decrease in sign-up rates.

The only element we could isolate in our analysis is that the control email shares Nathan's title while the treatment shares it only in Nathan's signature, in gray font. Perhaps the demonstration of "authority" made a bigger impact in people's decision to register.

Clearly, humanizing the email improved click rates, which is what we set out to test ... what's less clear is why this tactic caused such a dramatic dip in registration rates, warranting closer analysis and a second test.

experiments.title: How using a ‘pattern interrupt’ in a paid ad affects email acquisition and CPA
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting everyday elements in uncommon contexts will produce higher acquisition rates and lower CPA by using incongruence to “jar the prospect to attention” and entice them to engage with the offer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/03/2023
For our content offers aimed at name acquisition, we’ve traditionally had a difficult time finding a treatment that outperformed a simple 3D render of the resource, especially in terms of CPA—despite the widely parroted best practice of putting value prop copy on the image.
Our hypothesis has always been that the simple presentation of a 3D render on a plain white background worked for 3 primary reasons:
The simple design was more authentic than a heavily-designed ad
The simple design, in its simplicity, stood out from the crowd of busier, more colorful ads and posts
The simple design did not distract from the value of the offer itself
But still, to be convinced of the value of the offer, the prospect had either to read the ad’s “primary text” or click through to the landing page.
And we continued to suspect that if there was a way to prominently feature the value of an offer on the ad image itself, where a user’s attention is naturally drawn, without sacrificing the authentic, human appeal of the 3D render, it may be able to snatch the prospect’s attention, in a unique way, and more effectively.
For this test we pitted our usual control —3D render of the offer as the ad image with value proposition copy in the “primary text” field—against a simple screen grab of a note we created using the Apple Note app on our phone, which communicated the value of the offer, along with common, everyday language used for the primary text.
Because we were testing a new concept, we changed both the ad image and primary text but made sure both fit into our overall hypothesis. We split traffic between the control and the treatment and let the test run until it reached statistical validity and acquired more than 50 conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that presenting everyday elements in uncommon contexts can significantly improve acquisition rates and lower CPA.
The treatment used unexpected juxtaposition to catch the prospect's attention, resulting in a 106.6% increase in email acquisition for all traffic compared to the control. But that's not all. Here are the highlights:
107% increase in email acquisition -- 39 v. 1786.49% increase in CTR -- 64.9% v. 34.8%87.3% increase in outbound clicks -- 103 v. 5556.4% decrease in CPA -- $9.68 v. $22.1946.5% decrease in cost-per-click -- $3.69 v. $6.90
This suggests that by using a creative approach to funnel design, one that subtly blends the ordinary with the unexpected, we can entice prospects to engage by creating an experience beyond just "showing them an ad."
The key to the success of this concept, we believe, is presenting everyday design elements and  language outside of their expected context, using day-to-day, non-promotional urgency, to stoke curiosity and build momentum through to the landing page
What's interesting about this test is that if you stop to think about the treatment logically, it doesn't make any sense. But that does not mean that its "illogical" construction was unintentional. The incongruity of the presentation, we hypothesize, plays a large part in piquing the prospect's interest and pulling them into the funnel.
In future experimentation, we should continue exploring the surprising juxtapositions of elements. It would be interesting to test this concept throughout the entire funnel.
And while we are intrigued and inspired to keep testing this concept, we are also aware of the potential for "fatigue" with the specific application we used in this test. Therefore, while we encourage the "note app" treatment for low-investment to test the concept, and will try it with a few different offers, we believe we will eventually need to test this concept using different tactics and applications.

experiments.title: How using an iPhone Notes App style of Facebook ad creative impacted clicks to a video series landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the ad creative image to mimic a Notes App style for Facebook traffic will achieve an increase in clicks and email signups because the ad creative looks more authentic and eye-catching. because  the ad creative looks more authentic and eye-catching. because  the ad creative looks more authentic and eye-catching. because  the ad creative looks more authentic and eye-catching.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2024 - 06/24/2024
Focus on the Family is a large Christian ministry organization that creates and shares free resources like video series to encourage families. They promoted these free video series on Facebook and wanted to see if we could try some radically different ad creatives to improve clicks and email signups from their ads.
For this experiment, we tested using an organic, non-branded ad that resembles a screenshot of someone’s iPhone Notes App. This ad creative featured an impact-focused headline and three bullet point statements below.
The control ads for this test used the same headline and bullet points, but they would look like the typical branded ads we usually run on Facebook.
Our hypothesis is that the Notes App ads, with their authentic and eye-catching design, will significantly boost clicks and email signups. This is due to their unique appearance, which we believe will resonate more with our target audience on Facebook.
We ran this as an A/B campaigns test in Facebook with the ad budget split evenly. The Control campaign had the standard ads for two video series, and the Treatment campaign had the Notes App version of the ad creative for those same two video series. All other variables remained the same, and the test ran for a total of 2.5 weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We expected the Notes App style of ad creative to perform better in this test, and we were surprised to see that our Control ads actually had the best results.
These are the results we saw:
The Treatment ads led to a -6.1% decrease in clicks to the video series landing page, with a 99.9% level of confidence (LOC).The Treatment ads also validated a -7.9% decrease in the number of emails acquired, with a 99.6% LOC.And the Treatment ads had a -24.0% decrease in donations, with a 98.8% LOC.The Control ads had a campaign ROI of 1.39, which was higher than the Treatment campaign's ROI of 0.94.
Our key learnings from this experiment are that the Notes App style ad creative did not perform as well as the standard branded ads in terms of clicks, email signups, and donations. Despite the hypothesis that the more authentic and eye-catching design would lead to an increase in engagement, the results showed a decrease in clicks to the landing page, emails acquired, and donations.
We have a couple of theories on why the Treatment ads performed worse in this test, and these are ideas that we can test into with future ads:
The Treatment ads lacked the Focus on the Family branding and logo, which could have decreased their credibilityThe Treatment ads did not incorporate any imagery to support the value or impact of the video series, but the Control ads used imagery to support their claims.Ex: For the "Reconnected" marriage video series ad, the image in the Control is a couple holding hands. This supports the message that the video series will help the reader reconnect with their spouse.The Control ads had more color to help them stand out on Facebook, whereas the white background of the Treatment ads caused them to almost "blend in" with the rest of the white space on the page.Ex: For the "Reconnected" marriage video series ad, the image in the Control is a couple holding hands. This supports the message that the video series will help the reader reconnect with their spouse.

experiments.title: How streamlining and simplifying email copy affects click rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Streamlining an email by removing additional content (even when that content is value-focused) will increase click rates by improving the clarity of the email and focusing on the value derived from the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2024 - 05/30/2024
A lot of brands in the b2b space send heavily-designed emails. We can’t help but wonder if they’ve ever tested this approach, because we have consistently found that the less ‘markety’ and the more human our emails, the better our response.
So far our testing has instructed us to remove all branding elements, use everyday language, left-justify our copy, and generally design our emails to look as similar to a personal email as possible.
But for this test, we wanted to know how applying the same principle of simplification to an email’s content would affect click rates.
In the past we’ve improved click rates by cutting any non-essential or repetitive language, eliminating “hooks”, and removing any “warm up” copy or overlong salutations.
But what would happen if we also removed additional VALUE from the email beyond the resource we’re sharing?
The control in this test included some value-driven copy that could help the user understand more about using the content offer we were sharing. In the treatment, we removed this copy and presented the offer straight.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Streamlining the email by removing additional content produced a significant 48% lift in click rates when compared to the control, reinforcing our hypothesis that clarity and simplicity trump most other tactics when it comes to improving email engagement.
This is one test in a long series of tests to optimize our email engagement. And from our learnings, we have been able to develop a 'templated control' for the emails we send to our subscribers, based on their preferences.
Present a problem or common misconception. Provide an example of what to do instead. Explain a 'unique mechanism' of this recommendationPosition the offer as the "how to" CTAReinforce main benefit
So it's interesting to see how such a seemingly simple message is the product of such extensive testing! And we'll continue to find ways to refine this control over time
Overall, the results of this experiment highlight how focusing on ONE core message in your email can improve click rates and engagement.

experiments.title: How video ad creative impacts engagement n compared to static ad creative
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that video ad creative for a direct appeal campaign will be more engaging than an organic image and lead to higher engagement by increasing the visual appeal and clarity of the donation CTA
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2024 - 06/10/2024
Bible League Canada ran a direct appeal campaign on Facebook, inviting users to donate to support children’s Biblical education worldwide. In a prior test, we validated that using “organic” looking ad creative, we could increase engagement and donations for direct appeal campaigns. However, for this campaign, we could use an inspirational short video that demonstrated the need for Christian education worldwide. We hypothesized that this moving video could attract more users on Facebook and lead to better results.
We built two campaigns with matching ad copy; the only change was the ad creative. Our control campaign featured the “organic” looking static image we’d tested in the past against the video ad creative. We ran the test for one week.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

1. Video outperformed static image: The experiment demonstrated that using a video ad creative for direct appeal campaigns led to a significant increase in engagement, as indicated by the 84.1% increase in Clicks for All Traffic. This result supports the hypothesis that video ads are more engaging than static images for this type of campaign. This in turn led to a 300% increase in donations at a 92% Level of confidence.

2. Visual appeal and clarity: The success of the video ad creative in this experiment may be attributed to its ability to convey the need for Christian education worldwide in a more compelling and emotionally impactful way compared to the static image. The moving visuals likely captured users' attention and prompted them to take action.

It is worth noting that our CPM more than doubled for the video campaign and our cost per click went up 8%. However, the people we were paying to place the ad in front of were far more interested in the content, and thus, our click-through rate and instant donation page conversion rate both increased significantly, leading to the 300% increase in donations. (Our instant conversion rate increased 117%).

We will continue testing the impact of inspirational videos like this in other parts of the funnel and on other campaigns to see their impact.

experiments.title: How adding additional value prop to a giving banner impacted conversion rates during year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the copy to include a specific goal on the homepage banner for the family audience will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because of the addition of a specific goal (exclusivity) and value prop will increase the motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2023 - 01/01/2024
During CYE Focus on the Family had a Giving Banner on the top of their homepage that had a very short CTA. We wanted to see if we tweaked the copy if we could increase the motivation to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the copy to include a specific goal on the homepage banner for the family audience significantly increased donor conversion rates and revenue. By adding a specific goal and value proposition to the banner, we were able to create a sense of exclusivity and increase motivation to give.

The treatment group saw a 109.6% increase in revenue for all traffic, demonstrating the effectiveness of the new copy. Furthermore, there was a significant increase in transactions, both one-time and overall, with a 1412% and 1372% increase respectively.

experiments.title: How Adding a Countdown Affects Donor Conversion on a Wikibanner
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown on the homepage wiki banner for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the countdown adds appeal and exclusivity to the value proposition as to why somebody should give a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2024 - 05/20/2024
Focus on the Family is running its SeeLife campaign.. which drives more traffic to the homepage than usual. With this increased traffic we wanted to see if we could get donors to give on the homepage by having a wiki banner on the top of the homepage.
We have tested this before with previous campaigns. However, with this one we wanted to see what was more appealing to their donor audience, a total campaign goal countdown or a daily goal countdown.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that adding a countdown on the homepage wiki banner for prospective donors significantly increased donor conversion rates. Specifically, the treatment group experienced a 35.4% increase in Transactions - One-Time for All Traffic with a high statistical level of confidence of 98.6%. This suggests that the countdown added appeal and exclusivity to the value proposition, motivating more donors to give.
Moreover, Transactions overall saw a substantial 287% increase with a statistical confidence level of 97%. This highlights the effectiveness of incorporating a countdown in driving donor engagement and ultimately increasing donations.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue leveraging the use of countdowns in fundraising campaigns to capitalize on the sense of urgency and exclusivity they create.
Ultimately, the results of this experiment provide valuable insights that can guide future fundraising strategies and help maximize donation opportunities for the Focus on the Family organization.
____
Additional Notes
The tactic of adding a campaign goal counter to the homepage giving widget was effective at increasing conversions—likely due to the increased visibility of the need for donations. It also provides a level of social proof as donors can see that others are giving together to achieve a shared goal.
The more effective treatment, however, was the daily donor goal. While this treatment also increased the visibility of the need and adds the social proof aspect, it also reframes the goal to be more tangible and achievable to the donor. A potential donor may feel that their donation is a drop in the bucket of a 39,000 donor goal. But being of 675 donors needed today may feel more accessible and attainable.

experiments.title: How Conversion-Centered Design Affects Click Rates On A Landing Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that redesigning the NextAfter Institute membership page using conversion-centered design will increase click-through rates by using proven persuasion levers to hold attention and compel action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/12/2024
Conversion-centered design (CCD) aims to apply multiple (a) persuasive design techniques and (b) psychological triggers to get more people to take a desired action. This tactic can be helpful when you feel a page “needs a lot of work” and/or when a page does not receive enough traffic to validate smaller changes.
As we began to drive more traffic to a landing page promoting the NextAfter Institute Membership, we wanted to test how using CCD to make multiple changes would impact conversion rates. For this test, we developed a treatment that tested:
Structuring page to improve “skim-ability” – We used color to create on-page contrast and make it easier for the user to find the information they needed; we also narrowed the copy width to make it more readable
Making social proof more prominent – We embedded a widget that made it easy for users to read and scroll through our many positive reviews, giving them a more prominent placement on the page and placing them in two locations: near the top and near the bottom of the page
Using an outcome-driven headline – The headline is the most important copy on the page, so we shifted from a feature-focused headline to one that reinforced what those features will allow the reader to achieve, reinforcing the “how” with a subhead
Using long-form copy to make the case – At the top of the page, we used outcome-driven copy in big bold font to prompt immediate action but we reinforced that messaging with an in-depth sales letter that uses compelling language to illustrate the value propsotion concisely yet thoroughly
Drawing attention to benefits and outcomes – We used color contrast, huge fonts, bold type, testimonials, highlighted text, short paragraphs, and crossheads to draw attention to the benefits and outcomes of membership
We split traffic between the control and the treatment and ran the A/B test for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing conversion-centered design techniques can significantly impact click-through rates.
By making multiple changes to improve the page design from the perspective of conversion rate optimization, we were able to increase click-through rates by 106.6% with a 100% statistical level of confidence.
In future experimentation, we should continue to utilize conversion-centered design principles to optimize our landing pages and drive conversions.
We can now improve on this design by experimenting with different creative combinations to refine our approach and improve our overall conversion rates. Additionally, we should consider applying these learnings to other areas of our website and marketing materials to enhance the effectiveness of our messaging and calls to action.

experiments.title: How removing an eBook from Facebook creative impacts complete registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesize that substituting the 3D eBook with a scientific image will lead to increased registration rates, as it is expected to captivate the attention of the targeted demographic more effectively.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/03/2024 - 04/28/2024
This experiment was conducted on behalf of a non-profit organization specializing in scientific research, education, and charitable initiatives. The test was carried out as a paid social media advertising campaign on Facebook, comparing the effectiveness of two different creative images. Our objective was to ascertain whether the target audience of this non-profit organization would respond more favorably to a 3D image of an eBook or a photograph. The purpose of this experiment was to determine whether adjusting the creative approach could enhance audience engagement, ultimately leading to a higher rate of completed registrations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that the treatment, which involved using a scientific image instead of a 3D eBook image, significantly increased registration rates across various segments of the target audience. The treatment resulted in a 247.9% increase in Complete Registrations for All Traffic with a 100.0% level of confidence.

In addition, the experiment also showed significant decreases in Cost per Click (78%) and Cost per Registration (243%) with high levels of confidence, indicating that the treatment not only attracted more registrations but did so more cost-effectively.

Moving forward, the learnings from this experiment suggest that utilizing scientific images may be more effective in capturing the attention and interest of the target audience for the SETI Institute. Future experimentation should focus on leveraging visually engaging scientific images to drive higher engagement and conversion rates. It is important to continue testing different visual creatives to identify the most effective approach for engaging the audience and achieving the organization's objectives.

experiments.title: How a micro yes on a sticky slide out on article pages impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition to the article pages sticky donate button for visitors reading the article pages will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the copy has incentive for somebody to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/30/2023
Focus on the Family is a well-known Christian ministry that receives tons of traffic to their article pages to guide people on a variety of different topics. For this test we wanted to see if we added value proposition to the sticky donate on the side of the page would urge people to give.
The copy we used was specifically to increase the urgency and get a website visitor to agree to a ‘micro-yes’.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding value proposition to the sticky donate button on the article pages had a negative impact on donor conversion rate. The treatment group, which saw the value proposition copy, had a 35.9% decrease in donations compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant with a confidence level of 98.3%.

This specific ask was for a visitor to join Friends of the Family, which is their monthly sustainer program. It's important to note that for recurring gifts the treatment had 9 donors, and the control had 10. While it was still the losing variant, it did perform very closely to the control, whereas one-time gifts were significantly less (63 to 38).

Based on these findings, it is clear that the copy used in the value proposition did not effectively motivate visitors to make a donation. It is important to reassess the messaging and value proposition used in future experiments to ensure that it resonates with the target audience and encourages them to take the desired action.

Future experimentation should focus on identifying and testing different value propositions that can effectively incentivize visitors to donate. This could involve conducting user research to better understand the motivations and needs of the target audience, and then crafting messaging that speaks directly to those motivations. It would also be beneficial to test different placements or formats for the donate button to optimize its visibility and impact. By continually testing and iterating on the value proposition and donation messaging, Focus on the Family can work towards achieving a higher donor conversion rate on their article pages.

experiments.title: How removing most of the primary ad text from a content offer Facebook ad impacts downstream donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing most of the the primary ad text from a free content offer Facebook Ad will remove friction and increase clicks and downstream donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2024 - 04/04/2024
Focus on the Family uses dozens of free video series content offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. These ads rely on compelling ad imagery and lengthy ad copy promoting the series and explaining its value. We hypothesized that removing this and instead using a compelling ad image with a short overlaid Call to Action would lead to greater engagement on the ads and, thus, more clicks and donations because it was removing friction from engaging with the ad and letting the video series landing page do the “heavy lifting” of explaining the offer value and converting traffic.
We created a new campaign for their Proof You Need video series that used the same ad imagery as our control campaign but without the extra primary ad text, leaving just a single ad sentence: ” Experience a bold confidence in the reliability of Scripture with these free videos!” We split the campaign traffic using Facebook’s AB testing feature.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that removing the primary ad text from a free content offer Facebook Ad led to a significant decrease in donations for the "Proof You Need" video series campaign. The treatment group, which had the primary ad text removed, saw a 55.6% decrease in donations compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 100.0% confidence level, indicating that the control performed better than the treatment.
In future experimentation, it's essential to consider the impact of ad text on donation behavior. While the hypothesis assumed that removing ad text would reduce friction and increase engagement, the results showed the opposite effect. Instead of adding friction, this ad text provided important context for social audiences. Not only did we validate a decrease in donations, but also a 95% decrease in clicks.

experiments.title: How Adding A Donor Testimony on a Donation Page Impacts Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimony on the donation page for the sustainer campaign will achieve a higher number of monthly donor transactions because of the added social proof to the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2024 - 03/22/2024
Focus on the Family runs a sustainer drive each year. In this campaign, there will be language all over their site that talks about the impact that a monthly donor makes and will have several CTA, including a countdown for the number of sustainers needed, wiki banners on article pages, etc.. All of those CTA are pointing to their main sustainer donation page for the campaign. We wanted to see if we could increase the number of conversions by adding donor testimony specifically from a monthly donor since that is who we are trying to acquire.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donor testimony on the donation page for the sustainer campaign significantly increased the number of monthly donor transactions. The 61.0% increase in Transaction - Recurring for All Traffic with a 97.0% statistical level of confidence indicates that the treatment was successful in achieving its goal of driving more conversions.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue leveraging social proof, such as donor testimonials, to reinforce the value of becoming a monthly donor. This provides potential donors with reassurance and validation, ultimately motivating them to take action. Additionally, incorporating more donor testimonials or varied forms of social proof across different touchpoints in the donation journey could further improve conversion rates.

experiments.title: How using a plain image in a content offer Facebook ad impacted clicks compared to a designed image using an overlaid CTA
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a plain image in a content offer Facebook ad will increase clicks by making the ad appear more authentic because it looks like a post from another Facebook user
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2024 - 03/20/2024
Focus on the Family uses free video series content offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. For one of their best-performing campaigns, they hypothesized that they could increase engagement by making the ad look like a post from another user. Users who share content don’t create “ads” with a custom photo with overlaid call to action text. Instead, they often post a plain image with some thoughts about it.
We created an ad treatment with no text or CTA overlaying the image. We used the ad copy itself to promote the free video series to users and invite them to sign up. We ran this against our control ad images that featured overlaid CTAs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using an organic-looking Facebook ad design, which resembles a post from another user, can increase clicks significantly compared to traditional ad designs with overlaid call-to-action text. This approach creates a more authentic and engaging experience for the audience, leading to higher engagement with the ad content.
Moving forward, we should leverage this learning in future experimentation by incorporating more organic-looking ad designs in our campaigns. By focusing on creating content that mimics user-generated posts rather than traditional ad formats, we can build a stronger connection with our audience and drive higher click-through rates. Additionally, we should continue to test and optimize different creative approaches to further enhance our advertising strategies and maximize results.
For a future offer, we will test no ad copy and instead use an image with a clear, short call to action on a plain-colored background.
Impact on other metrics:
The treatment decreased the gross cost per click by $0.48 ($1.42 vs $190)There was no statistically significant difference in emails acquired during the experiment.

experiments.title: How a fullwidth, plain-text email affects click rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a full-width text format for our weekly email send will achieve an increase in email clicks because the email will seem more organic, personal, and less like marketing.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/03/2023 - 11/04/2023
Our weekly email sends have been the same format for quite a while. We tested into using plain text format to create a more human-looking email a while ago and were curious if we could take it one step further to  seem even more organic. Typically our email text is centered so that when it’s opened it’s still quite obvious it’s a bulk send, not a one-off email.
The only thing change in the treatment of the email is the format. The copy, subject line, sender, etc. all stayed the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a full-width text format for our weekly email send can significantly increase the number of clicks. The treatment group, which received the full-width text format, experienced a 27.6% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control group.
This indicates that the full-width text format created the perception that the email was sent straight from Gmail rather than a bulk send. This likely caught the attention of recipients and encouraged them to click on the links within the email.
It is important to note that the treatment showed a significant 214% decrease in Clicks to Opens. This means that even though the treatment group had higher click-through rates, fewer people actually took the step to open the email. The treatment would have no impact on this though since the test was run on the email layout, not the content or email "envelope."
To use this learning in future experimentation, we might want to explore different formats that combine the organic feel of the full-width text with elements that entice recipients to open the email.
For example, we could experiment with subject lines that highlight the content or benefits of the email, as well as using personalization techniques to make the email feel even more tailored to each recipient.
Overall, this experiment has shown that the format of our weekly email can have a significant impact on recipient engagement. By continuing to test and iterate on different formats, we can improve our email performance and ultimately achieve our goal of increasing click-through rates while maintaining a high open rate.

experiments.title: How Removing the Store Page and Cart Page from an ecommerce funnel impacts the number of visitors starting the checkout process
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the store product page and cart page from an e-commerce funnel will increase the number of visitors starting the checkout process by removing friction
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2023 - 01/12/2024
Focus on the Family was promoting an exclusive 4-season streaming series, RVL: Discipleship the Study, as a product in their store. As a product, there were multiple steps in the e-commerce checkout process that we hypothesized could add friction to the sales process. We decided to test a new funnel that bypassed the store product page and cart page, so visitors would be able to go straight from the product landing page to the checkout pages.
We split the traffic for all sources evenly and ran the experiment for
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the store product page and cart page from an e-commerce funnel can significantly increase the number of visitors starting the checkout process. The treatment version, which bypassed these pages, led to a 53.0% increase in those starting the checkout process for all traffic compared to the control version.

We were unable to validate the increase we saw in final purchases due to tracking issues in BigCommerce. We will continue testing this new, shorter funnel to see how we can further increase clarity and the perceived value of the offer so that more users purchase RVL. We also are optimizing the checkout pages so that these are easier to complete.

experiments.title: How an ultra-personal, interruptive ad format affects email acquisition and CPA
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting an ultra-personal format in an unfamiliar context will produce higher acquisition rates and a lower CPA by using incongruence to “jar the prospect to attention” and entice them to engage with the offer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/03/2023
For our content offers aimed at name acquisition, we’ve traditionally had a difficult time finding a treatment that outperformed a simple 3D render of the resource, especially in terms of CPA — despite the widely parroted best practice of putting value prop copy on the image.
Our hypothesis has always been that the simple presentation of a 3D render on a plain white background worked for 2 primary reasons:
The simple design was more authentic than a heavily-designed ad
The simple design, in its simplicity, stood out from the crowd of busier, more colorful ads and posts
But still, to be convinced of the value of the offer, the prospect had either to read the ad’s “primary text” or click through to the landing page.
And we continued to suspect that if there was a way to prominently feature the value of an offer on the ad image itself, where a user’s attention is naturally drawn, without sacrificing the authentic, human appeal of the 3D render, it may be able to grab the prospect’s attention, in a unique way, and more effectively.
For this test, we pitted our usual control — a 3D render of the offer as the ad image with value proposition copy in the “primary text” field — against a simple screen grab of a note we created using the Apple Note app on our phone, which communicated the value of the offer, along with common, everyday language used for the primary text.
Because we were testing a new concept, we changed both the ad image and primary text but made sure both fit into our overall hypothesis. We split traffic between the control and the treatment and let the test run until it reached statistical validity and acquired more than 50 conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that presenting everyday elements in uncommon contexts can significantly improve acquisition rates and lower CPA.

The treatment used unexpected juxtaposition to catch the prospect's attention, resulting in a 106.6% increase in email acquisition for all traffic compared to the control. But that's not all. Here are the highlights:
107% increase in email acquisition -- 39 v. 17
 	86.49% increase in CTR -- 64.9% v. 34.8%
 	87.3% increase in outbound clicks -- 103 v. 55
 	56.4% decrease in CPA -- $9.68 v. $22.19
 	46.5% decrease in cost-per-click -- $3.69 v. $6.90

experiments.title: How an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar with a small donation CTA and opt-in for an email reminder impacts donor conversion during a Calendar Year-End Campaign

rows.name: Dear reader urgency with Scrolling Sticky Bar

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar reminding visitors of the value they’ve received from this organization’s articles will increase donor conversion by increasing the appeal and exclusivity of the offer
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2022 - 12/25/2022
Historically, this organization used a combination of sticky bars and slide-outs with messaging around the urgent need of helping youth experiencing homelessness or housing insecurity  and have tied this urgency back to either a donor goal or a revenue goal. During their calendar year-end campaign, they often use a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown to convert visitors to their article pages into donors.
They wanted to test this against a design inspired by one of Wikipedia’s tests where there is an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar when scrolling. Instead of emphasizing the matching challenge in effect and the donor goal, the in-line appeal would ask the reader to consider the value they’ve received during the past year from their articles and tell them that less than 1% of readers donate to make these articles possible. Once the reader scrolled to continue reading the article, they would be prompted with the following, “If we have given you $5 worth of truth about housing insecurity this year, take a minute to donate.” They would then be asked for a gift of as little as $5 to “keep these exclusive articles possible to provide guidance to transform lives.”
If the visitor clicked the button to give, they would go to the donation page with the amount they selected. They could also click, “maybe later” which loaded an embedded HubSpot form that allowed them to opt-in for an email reminder to give. Those who opted in would receive an email reminder before the end of the campaign to make their gift.
Our hypothesis was that the conversational and value-driven copy on the in-line design and sticky bar, coupled with the option to opt-in for an email reminder would increase donor conversion by increasing the appeal and exclusivity of the offer. We would also be tapping into the donor’s desire to not lose access to these articles by highlighting how few people give to make them possible.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new messaging and design crushed the control sticky bar with a daily donor goal. What is remarkable is that the avg gift was $29 despite the fact that the CTA was to make a $5 gift. This showed that readers felt that they were receiving much more value from the articles and content and wanted to make sure that it would still be available to them in the new year.

It's also worth noting that the dear reader/slide-up treatment brought in a $10,000 gift. We've pulled this out in the recording of this experiment so as not to skew the average gift or the revenue to give a more accurate picture of the overall results.

111 people opted in to receive follow-up emails to remind them about giving opportunities to this organization. We will be monitoring this segment to see downstream conversion as well as sending specific appeals to them reminding them that they asked to know more about ways to give at a later date.

We will continue to refine the messaging of this bar for future urgent campaigns as well as test into leveraging statistics of how few visitors/pledge signers/readers, etc donate to see if this messaging increases donor conversion on acquisition offer donation pages as well.

We also want to test the opening line, "we humbly ask you, don't scroll away" in other places to see if that conversational approach can improve conversion by keeping people on the page longer. For example, on an instant donation page after someone has requested an eBook or signed a pledge. Will that headline keep someone on the page long enough to read the donation appeal?

experiments.title: How a video-based landing page affects email signup rates for a new YouTube fundraising show
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing a text-based landing page with a video-based landing page will increase registrations by “providing a sample” of the content’s value and showing rather than telling why people should want to tune in.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/10/2023
We recently decided to pursue a new way to share what works in fundraising by launching a YouTube show called “Decoding Generosity”.
And to promote this new ‘edutainment’ experience, we wanted to know how using a video on a landing page would affect sign-up rates for a prescreening of the show.
This test was run in the context of a broader promotional strategy testing the concept of creating a unified in-funnel experience using video as a means of “showing” rather than telling about the show.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Using a video-based landing page instead of a text-based landing page produced a significant 57% increase in the registration rate.

We believe there are several reasons that the video-based treatment produced these results:
Show, don't tell. The video-based landing page matched the medium of the content we were promoting, allowing us to preview clips from the show to stoke their desire to tune in and "show" that the claim presented in our offer was genuine
 	Front and center. By reducing the supporting copy, the value-driven headline and subhead were given more visual prominence
 	Quality matters. The high production quality of the video likely played a role in the overall effectiveness of the treatment
 	Context matters. Traditionally, we have seen that text-based landing pages far outperform video-based landing pages. But it's important to consider context. This promotion was run in the context of a B2B promotion (rather than a fundraising context) and the landing page was part of a broader video-based promotional experience meant to "keep people in the story" without feeling like they were in a funnel**

experiments.title: Will shortening a promotional email increase click-throughs?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening a promotional email for our general subscribers will achieve an increase in click-throughs because the email is more direct, human, and will create a higher sense of urgency by focusing exclusively on the upcoming ticket price increase.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/21/2023 - 07/24/2023
At NIO Summit, there’s a lot going on. Beyond the in-depth sessions that dig deep into the most recent data and strategies in digital fundraising, there’s a whole theme that we lean into—heavily.
Each month when there’s a ticket deadline it’s often challenging to pick and choose which unique value of NIO to drive forward to the audience that will make them want to come. And often this leads to long emails with a lot of details.
After seeing a very parsed-down email from Sticker Mule about a deal on t-shirts that they were running, we wondered how that might apply to an upcoming ticket deadline promo we were planning to send to our subscribers.
We wanted to see if communicating all the many benefits of attending NIO Summit in a single email was creating mental friction. And whether a simpler, more direct email, focused on a single message might eliminate that friction.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While both emails had about a 20% open rate, the treatment email resulted in a 138% increase in clicks. Beyond an increase in clicks, it’s important to note that 3 people who received the treatment email bought tickets to NIO Summit, while no tickets were bought by people who received the control email. 

This supports our hypothesis that our typical value-heavy promotional emails may be seen as cluttered and create mental friction. And that the shortened promotional email may increase click-throughs by creating more clarity and a higher sense of urgency.

We plan to test this same hypothesis in other contexts. Our next iteration will be to test this with an email releasing a new online fundraising resource. 

 

Winston's Analysis:

The key learning from this experiment is that shortening a promotional email to focus on a single message can lead to a significant increase in click-through rates. The treatment email, which was more direct and focused exclusively on the upcoming ticket price increase, resulted in a 138.4% increase in clicks for all traffic.

This suggests that providing a clear and urgent call-to-action, without overwhelming the audience with too much information, can be more effective in motivating subscribers to take action.

Furthermore, the experiment also revealed a 591% decrease in Clicks to Opens. This indicates that the shorter email may have created a higher sense of urgency, resulting in subscribers clicking on the email without spending much time engaging with its content.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring the impact of shorter, more focused emails on click-through rates. This can be applied not only to promotions for ticket deadlines but also to other types of email communications.

Additionally, it would be valuable to analyze the conversion rates and overall engagement of subscribers who clicked on the shorter email versus the longer email. This will help determine the quality of the clicks and the effectiveness of the treatment in driving desired actions.

Overall, the key takeaway is that simplicity and directness in email messaging can lead to higher engagement and click-through rates. Communicating a single clear message that creates a sense of urgency can be a powerful strategy in future email promotions.

experiments.title: How a popup inviting donors to convert their onetime donation to a monthly gift at 60% of the value impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a popup inviting donors to convert their onetime donation to a monthly gift at 60% of the value of their donation will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2022 - 12/16/2022
An organization was looking for ways to increase their monthly donor acquisition on their website and wanted to test a popup that would fire the moment someone clicked the button to complete their one-time gift and offer them the opportunity to save even more lives by giving a monthly donation of 60% of the value of their one-time gift selection.
We hypothesized that this would attract donors because the gift amount would be less initially than what they’d already decided to donate, and this would offer this conversion to a monthly gift when donor motivation was the highest.
The traffic was evenly split, and this ran for approximately 10 days.
We believe that pop-ups inviting donors to convert their one-time donation to a monthly gift at 60% of the value of their donation will increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Contrary to our expectations, the popup decreased one-time donations and revenue (43.7% revenue reduction). It also failed to increase recurring giving which was relatively flat between variations. In reviewing the experiment, additional value proposition for why donors should become monthly donors was needed.

So, an additional 1-2 sentences in the popup explaining the benefit to the donor for making that switch. Also, in the experience, selecting the option to switch to a monthly gift would update the donor form with the new gift amount and select the checkbox for recurring, but the donor would still need to resubmit the donation. Having a checkbox may have added additional friction to the process.

 

Since we were testing this during year-end, when donors have high motivation, that friction alone could have stalled response on the page. We want to retest this popup in the new year outside of a high-urgency campaign to see if we can increase donor conversion by adding a stronger value proposition for converting a one-time gift to a recurring one.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to the monthly donation tab on the main donation page impacts donor conversion

rows.name: Value proposition on the monthly donate tab

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition to the monthly donation tab on the main donation page will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2022 - 11/04/2022
An organization that uses online content and breaking-news stories to engage with visitors wanted to test ways of acquiring more monthly donors on their main donation page.
They decided to test the impact of adding a callout above the “monthly donation tab” on the form with an additional value proposition for making a monthly gift.
They tested this approach on the main donation page (this experiment) and also on a campaign donation page they used in the October recurring donor campaign. The results of that experiment are recorded separately.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After one month, they achieved an 11% increase in donor conversion with an 88% Level of Confidence. They were able to validate a 57% increase in donor conversion for returning visitors to the site. Other key segments with only the exception of direct traffic all showed directional lifts in conversion.

Our hypothesis is that for most visitors to the page, the extra value propositional copy increases the perceived value of becoming a donor and thus increases donor conversion. Interestingly enough, we were not able to validate an impact on monthly giving from the callout. It seems that saying that a monthly gift "saves lives 365 days a year" reminded visitors that a onetime gift saves lives as well.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
All Traffic visitors had an 11.0% increase in donations with an 88% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 48.6% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Referral visitors had a 1,025.0% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Direct visitors had a 21.1% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.
 	Paid Search visitors had a 100.0% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: Will a HTML designed email achieve a higher click rate than a plain-text email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that plain-text emails will achieve a higher click rate than a html design (newsletter style) email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/09/2023 - 03/15/2023
In effort to test new and valuable content to our active subscribers, we decided to send an email digest of recent experiments we’ve tested to our house file. Since we have over 4,800+ experiments in our library, sometimes it’s easy to lose track of the current experiments.
Since this was a new concept, we decided to test two variants of emails.
Each email contained the same content but one was a html design and the other was a plain-text design. To add context, we normally send plain-text emails to our house file.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. Plain-text emails performed better in terms of clicks compared to html design newsletters.
2. Plain-text emails also had a higher open rate and click-to-open rate.
3. The experiment results had a high level of statistical confidence.
Moving forward, we should consider sending more plain-text emails to our house file as it seems to be a more effective approach for engaging our subscribers. We should also continue to test different email formats to see if there are any other changes we can make that will improve our email engagement metrics. Ultimately, we need to prioritize the preferences of our audience and find the best ways to communicate with them effectively.

experiments.title: How your Facebook (Meta) Advertising performance goal impacts video campaign reach
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the “Ad recall lift” goal in Facebook (Meta) Advertising for our awareness campaign will achieve a more cost-effective reach  because the algorithm is showing it to whoever it believes will likely “remember” seeing the ad.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2023 - 03/15/2023
As a part of NextAfter’s “Fundraising Genius” branding ad campaign, our team wanted to experiment with the video awareness campaign goals to monitor what performance goal would achieve the greatest “reach” (which is defined as: how many people in the target audience received at least one ad impression) with the campaign.
So, our team decided to try the “Maximize number of impressions” goal (as the control) vs. a new awareness building campaign goal called “Maximize ad recall lift” (as the treatment).
Otherwise, the two campaigns were identical, targeting the same audience.
Our research questions really were:
Which campaign setting would generate the greatest reach, and to what amount are we trading off “frequency” of ad placements (eg how many times anyone in the audience sees an ad per day)? And,
Which of the two campaign types is more cost effective for the investment?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using the "Maximize ad recall lift" goal in Facebook advertising for awareness campaigns is more effective than using the "Maximize number of impressions" goal because it reached nearly 2x the number of people on just 12.8% of the spending of the control audience.
This was confirmed by a 91.3% increase in "Reach" (how many unique people in our target audience received at least one impression) with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence.
To put that another way — the cost to reach 1,000 people in the control ad setting was $34.84, while the cost to reach 1,000 people with the treatment ad setting was just $2.68 (which is is a 92.3% decrease in cost to reach 1,000 people).
Having said all of that — it's worth noting that our frequency has plummeted, dropping from 12.4 in the control campaign to just 1.3 in the treatment.
Conclusion:
If you're looking to reach far more people in your target audience with at least one ad impression, prioritized to whomever the platform believes is most likely to consume the ad and remember it, you should use the "Awareness > Maximize ad recall lift" campaign setting.If you just want to overpower your competitors and ensure high ad frequency for a smaller number of your target audience, then you should use the "Awareness > Maximize the number of impressions" campaign setting.

experiments.title: How more focused offers increase your lead quality in acquisition campaigns

rows.name: Facebook Lead Ads w/Facebook Ad Template Offer
Facebook Lead Ads w/better qualifying offers
LinkedIn Lead Ads w/better qualifying offers

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using offers that do a better job of prequalifying my audience for acquisition campaigns will achieve a higher number of “qualified leads” (and a lower cost per qualified lead) because we believe the offer itself plays an important role in pre-qualifying the audience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/01/2023 - 01/31/2023
As a part of recent experimentation with using Facebook Lead Ad forms in our own acquisition efforts, we observed a substantial decrease in the cost per lead, without decreasing the disengagement and unsubscribe rates of the leads generated.
But, with this much higher volume, we received a substantial number of personal email addresses, which made it difficult to understand whether or not we were actually generating leads from our prime audience (fundraising professionals and/or marketers at nonprofit organizations that raise a certain number of dollars per year).
Our hypothesis was that the offer used in December of 2022 and previous experiments could have more “generic appeal” to any marketer (not just those who work at nonprofit organizations).
We also wondered whether Facebook advertising is the best platform for acquiring leads in general, as well as qualified leads tied to qualified nonprofit organizations.
Therefore, we ran a month-long experiment testing two offers that are more specific to nonprofit organizations across both Facebook (using the lead ads conversion type), while also trying those same (more specific) offers on LinkedIn advertising (using their lead ad equivalent).
Our goal was to compare the results acquired during this experiment time period against the results collected during the previous month’s experiment with Facebook Lead Ads with an offer that would (theoretically) have more generic appeal.
We had four metrics in mind to determine success of this treatment type, which are:
Cost per lead (of any type)
% of leads generated that have a “generic domain” (something like: “@gmail.com” as opposed to a work email address)
% of leads generated that are tied to a “qualified nonprofit organization”
Cost per qualified lead generated
experiments.keyLearnings:
As it turns out, the offers used to better qualify the audience indeed increased the number of qualified leads generated through our acquisition efforts during the duration of this experiment.
In fact, both platforms substantially increased the number of qualified leads generated for our advertising dollar spent. But, the question really is: "By how much?"
We observed that the "better quality offer" treatments across both platforms decreased the cost per qualified lead in the following way:
Facebook: -82.9%LinkedIn: -19.8%
Key Takeaway: So, not only did we increased the volume of qualified leads, we did so by dropping the cost per qualified lead by significant amounts.
Cost per lead trends compared in this way:
Facebook: -48.4%Linked: +1,182%
Key Takeaway: While running the "better quality offers" on Facebook, we actually decreased our cost per lead overall, but the cost per lead (of any type) generated on LinkedIn increased substantially. Therefore, at least in the way the LinkedIn ad campaigns were set up during this experiment period of time, we would not suggest shifting advertising budgets toward LinkedIn to replace Facebook lead generation campaigns.
Finally, our last measurement was to review the % of leads generated with a "generic email domain" — here is how the treatments performed against the control campaigns:
Facebook: -23.9%LinkedIn: -12.9%
Key Takeaway: The "better quality offers" indeed decreased the volume of leads generated with a "generic email domain," as well.
Summary of Findings:
There are actually three key takeaways that we have from this experiment, which are:
The relevance of your offer to your ideal customer/audience is a key factor for increasing both your campaign's response rates overall, but also can attract better quality leads, as well. If your campaigns aren't specific enough to your ideal audience, consider developing new, more relevant offers that can prequalify the audience at the ad impression level.Facebook Lead Ads continues to perform well, as we observed improvements in all success metrics using that tactic. LinkedIn Ads cost (at least in the campaign settings we used) are tremendously more expensive when compared to Facebook Lead Ads, and although we observed higher rates in which that platform produced "better qualified leads," the cost to produce any lead at all on that platform leads us to conclude that we should not shift large amounts of our advertising budget to that platform for net-new lead acquisition efforts.

experiments.title: How using an identity resolution service impacts your first-party record match rates within Facebook (Meta)
experiments.researchQuestion: Would we match our first-party contact list at a higher rate when using LiveRamp’s “identity resolution” technology once it was pushed into Facebook (Meta) Audiences?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/26/2023
LiveRamp is a data enablement platform, that claims to take first-party contact data points provided to them and match those contacts within their platform using a process called “identity resolution” to match your contacts within advertising platforms in a more reliable and consistent way, such that the “match rate” increases dramatically.
Since iOS 14.5, advertising platforms like Facebook (Meta) have had an more difficult time matching uploaded contact records, as well as less visibility into online behavioral tracking data, and also online purchase behavior.
With lower match rates and less information flowing back to the algorithm to learn and refine lookalike models, some clients have experienced less return on ad spend (ROAS).
With all of this in mind, we decided to experiment with what the match rates on Facebook (Meta) would be by uploading contacts via the HubSpot CRM integration, when compared to uploading them to Facebook (Meta) via LiveRamp.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a confidence level of 100%, we observed that the LiveRamp "identity resolution" upload process into Facebook (Meta) Ads Manager significantly improved our first-party contact match rate within the advertising platform — matching +94.9% more of the list that we uploaded.
Now that we know that the LiveRamp "identity resolution" service does indeed match your first-party contacts in a much better way, the real question is:
Do the lookalike audiences modeled from this LiveRamp list produce a more reliable lookalike modeled audience to use for acquisition campaigns?
It stands to reason that this could be the case. Why?
Because if our source list for lookalike modeling uses nearly 2x more contacts, then it would be reasonable to assume that the model it produces is far richer and the prospecting audience it produces is using twice as much data to model prospecting audiences to market to with your acquisition campaigns.
Further experimentation is required, specifically as it pertains to nonprofit organizations who are uploading donor data.
In fact, we're in the process of launching a handful of experiments on this very topic, and will share our findings when they are available.

experiments.title: How Facebook Lead Ads impacted email signup rates and lead quality

rows.name: Control - Ad Click to a Hosted Landing Page/Form

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the Facebook Lead Ad campaign type for lead campaigns targeting net-new prospect audiences will achieve an increase lead volume at a lower cost.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/06/2022
Since the iOS 14.5 software update (among other things) we have noticed a continuous increase in the cost to acquire a new subscriber within our acquisition campaigns here at NextAfter from Facebook advertising.
In an effort to continuously test ways to decrease the cost per lead metric for our own advertising and marketing campaigns, we wanted to experiment with trying the Facebook Lead Ad campaign type.
In previous tests (many years ago), we observed that the quality of the leads generated was far inferior to those collected from the more “recommended” experience of promoting ad clicks to a hosted landing page, making the case as to why someone should download the offered resource.
As a part of this experiment, we used the same resource as the offer, locking the creative used within the ad, targeting the same audience, but the treatment used a “Facebook Lead Ad” conversion point, as opposed to taking ad clicks from Facebook itself to our hosted landing page to secure the email address.
We were curious as to whether this treatment could not only lower the cost per new subscriber but also whether or not the “lead quality” would be negatively impacted, as well.
It’s worth noting that (at least for us) a quality lead is someone who we can tell (either through the information provided directly, or through a quick Google search for those leads) whether or not they were confirmed to work for a nonprofit organization, at least in the marketing or fundraising teams.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99% level of confidence, we observed an increase in email signup rates of +131.5% through the Facebook Lead Ad treatment campaign experience.
We also observed:
A valid increase in ad clickthrough rate by +24.3% (LoC: 99.1%), andThat the CPM (cost per one thousand impressions) also decreased by -35.8%, as well.
Overall, this treatment experience decreased our cost per lead by a whopping -72.3%!
But, the real question is: Did we just create a bunch of "junk leads" by using the Facebook Lead Ad campaign?
To determine this, we researched the lead using the data provided on the form submission to see whether or not the person requesting the resource was working at a charitable organization within the marketing or development/fundraising groups, or as a decision-maker within the organization's executive team.
Of the leads generated by experience type, here is what we found:
61.6% of the Control experience leads were determined to be "qualified."46.2% of the Treatment experience leads were determined to be "qualified."
So, the claim that the Facebook Lead Ads would produce a lower quality is true — but, from a certain point of view.
The truth of the matter is that although we generated a lower percentage of "qualified leads" from the overall amount of leads generated, the cost per "qualified lead" was actually decreased by -63% when compared to the control experience.
In fact, the cost per qualified lead from the Treatment experience was even -40% less expensive when compared to the total cost per lead from the Control experience.
There are two more observations that we made, as well...
When someone completes the Facebook Lead Ad form, the form itself offers marketers with the opportunity to offer one more thing to the person who just submitted the form. It's worth noting that when we promoted a site-hosted webinar recording that was relevant to the offer used within the experiment, we observed that 64% of the leads generated in the Treatment experience also chose to click the button (to go to our website/watch the webinar offered). And,Facebook Ads Manager's web interface is notoriously bad at properly tracking/reporting accurately under the Control experience type of campaign, however, we noted that the Treatment version of the campaign had 100% accuracy in reporting leads generated, cost per lead, etc.
When it's all said and done — here is what we found:
Facebook Lead Ads will very likely decrease your cost per lead/subscriber.But, as you scale up the number of leads, you'll lose a bit on the percentage of those leads being of "high quality."Regardless of that fact, it's worth using this approach because you get so many more leads (and an overall higher volume of "qualified leads" as a part of the higher total of leads overall) with this approach.
So, what comes next? We'll plan to circle back to the leads generated in this study and look at two more data points downstream, which are:
The percentage of leads who opt-out by the end of the new subscriber welcome series. And also,The percentage of leads who opt-out, are disengaged, and/or who have never opened a single email 60-days after securing the lead.
More on those studies soon, but for now — it's advised that you consider using Facebook Lead Ads in this post-iOS 14.5 world of continuously decreasing performance in Facebook Advertising to drive net-new subscriber growth for your organization and marketing campaigns.

experiments.title: How lookalike modeling for offer downloaders impacted email signup rate in a prospecting campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that narrowing our lookalike models to just the audience that downloaded the offer being promoted and expanding the number of lookalike models used for our Facebook prospecting campaign will achieve a higher email signup rate, because it’s more narrowly modeled after audiences that converted on the offer in question.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/09/2022
As a part of our attempts to drive down the cost to generate a new email subscriber on Facebook acquisition and paid prospecting campaigns, we wanted to experiment with going deeper and wider into the lookalike audience modeling capabilities for Facebook.
Although we were using some lookalike modeling in our control audiences, we stopped at the most “narrow” matching (looking at roughly the top 1.2MM lookalikes, that were matched at the 99-100% level for all emails on our existing subscription file).
We decided to experiment with building a wider set of lookalike audiences from just subscribers that converted historically on the same offer — and see if it would increase our signup rates, and/or decrease our cost per new email signed up from the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.6% confidence level, we observed an increase in email signup rates of +45.1% by focusing our lookalike modeling on offer-specific lookalike audiences.
In fact, this experiment also validated an increase in ad clickthrough rate of +56.3% (with a 100% level of confidence, even though the ad used across the experiment was identical between the two audiences).
The end result is that our cost per new email subscriber actually dropped by -12.1% (which is a great thing)!
So, right off of the bat, our ad became more attractive to the more acutely focused, offer-specific lookalike audience models — even though the "similarity" level of this treatment audience was decreased from just a 99% match down to a 90%+ match on this specific offer's downloaders historically.
Further experimentation may be required, but as it stands now it looks like at least for our own marketing activities, that we should create 90-100% match audiences, but make the source of those lookalike modeled audiences specific to offers used in our paid advertising campaign to generate net-new subscribers moving forward.

experiments.title: How different audience motivations affect click rate in a Facebook ad (Part 3)
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad variation will the housefile audience respond to more?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2016 - 08/04/2016
While setting up the advertising and promotions for an upcoming live broadcast, we wanted to determine if our various audience groups for our Facebook Ads would respond differently to various ad treatments. We have 3 main audience groups that we ran this test with: our housefile, housefile lookalikes, and nonprofit professionals.
Our original live broadcast Facebook Ad focused on the speaker and the title, but we thought people may be more motivated by different aspects of the focus topic. For the treatments, we developed an ad that focused on “Growing Your Donor File” and one that focused on “Empathy.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
Treatment 1 had a lift of 146.9% compared to the control. This confirms our hypothesis that this audience responds stronger to the learning and results rather than the speaker. Although, it is possible that the results could be different if the speaker was someone they were more familiar with.

Comparing this result to the result of our previous test on the housefile lookalike audience reveals a drastic difference in the motivations of two audiences that are supposedly similar. The lookalikes were 110% more likely to click on the empathy treatment, while the housefile was 26% less likely to click on the empathy treatment.

experiments.title: How a survey question in a Facebook Ad impacted email signup rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Our hypothesis is that by using a survey-style ad (with a “Yes/No” question included in the ad image) will increase clicks on the ad so much, that we can generate more emails at a more efficient cost.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/22/2022
As a part of our own marketing and prospecting via Facebook, we decided to try a new survey-style ad image to see if we could generate a much higher volume of ad clicks, and subsequently from that extra traffic, convert more people on the subsequent landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a confidence level of 100%, we observed a decrease in email signups by -57.8%.

Our hypothesis completely failed with our audiences on Facebook Advertising, because this decrease in email signups was actually driven by fewer clicks overall (we saw that this treatment ad decreased ad clicks by -53.6%, also with a confidence level of 100%).

Ultimately, we'd like to re-try this experiment, but next time with a different question in the treatment ad. With evidence that these style of ads work well in other cases, the thought here is that perhaps the question is too vague. Furthermore, could the length of the question also be impacting ad clickthrough rates?

Would asking a more simple question (like: "Do you want to get more from your Facebook advertising?") get straight to the point, and resonate with a larger portion of the audience? It's worth testing, so we will soon.

experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/22/2022
As a part of our own marketing and prospecting via Facebook, we decided to try a new survey-style ad image to see if we could generate a much higher volume of ad clicks, and subsequently from that extra traffic, convert more people on the subsequent landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a confidence level of 100%, we observed a decrease in email signups by -57.8%.

Our hypothesis completely failed with our audiences on Facebook Advertising, because this decrease in email signups was actually driven by fewer clicks overall (we saw that this treatment ad decreased ad clicks by -53.6%, also with a confidence level of 100%).

Ultimately, we'd like to re-try this experiment, but next time with a different question in the treatment ad. With evidence that these style of ads work well in other cases, the thought here is that perhaps the question is too vague. Furthermore, could the length of the question also be impacting ad clickthrough rates?

Would asking a more simple question (like: "Do you want to get more from your Facebook advertising?") get straight to the point, and resonate with a larger portion of the audience? It's worth testing, so we will soon.

0% increase in traffic
				× 57.8% decrease in conversion rate

experiments.title: How Imagery Affects Conversion in a Facebook Ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding additional clarity to the Facebook ad image increase course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/13/2017 - 10/18/2017
When running a Facebook ad campaign for a new online course, we wondered if we needed to add more clarity to the Facebook ad image. In order to more clearly depict the title and purpose of the course, Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we added the Facebook logo leading towards a dollar icon on the ad image.
This experiment was conducted with the Network for Good audience ad set.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new Facebook ad image led to a 68% decrease in course registration, despite appearing to ad more clarity. Since the control ad had previously been running, it’s possible that the additional social proof from likes and shares contributed to it’s better performance; although, likes and share don’t always make that significant of difference.

It could also be that the new imagery, although more accurate and clear, made the course look similar to other marketing courses out there that appear gimmicky or too good to be true.
