experiments.title: Which email sign up headline produces the highest conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: What email value proposition will produce the most email sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2015 - 08/28/2015
Every once in a while we get around to running tests on our own website to make sure NextAfter is practicing what we preach. So we set up two different email signup pages encouraging visitors to get the This vs. That weekly email which showcases one of our latest test results. The goal was to see which email sign up value proposition would drive the most conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test is another example of how clarity trumps persuasion. Clearly stating what a person is signing up for, getting a test each week, is a better description than talking about how a person will benefit. The surprising aspect to us was that even with the low traffic to the page the test validated.

The copy used to promote offers is the most important element in getting someone to say yes. Just throwing something up on the page and not testing it would have seriously affected the number of email sign-ups the page generated.

experiments.title: How Bullet Points in a Facebook Ad Affect the Clarity of the Value Proposition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of bullet points in a Facebook ad make the value of the course clearer, and lead to more course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 10/09/2017
When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below reflect are from the ad set targeting the Network for Good Facebook interest.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this audience, the result was a resounding “No.” The Network for Good audience had a 75% decrease in course registrations when shown the bullet points ad.

Each post had a comparable amount of clicks to the landing page, implying that people who viewed the control were more primed to complete their registration. It’s possible that by adding these bullet points, it made it easier for someone to skim through the post and not experience the full value proposition of the offer.

This learning should not be taken out of context and applied to every audience. Other ad sets targeting different audience segments are approaching validity with the Bullet Point ad as the winning treatment.

When we first launched the new NextAfter.com website, we had some internal debate about the best offer to use in the footer of the site. Historically, we had offered a subscription to our weekly newsletter, This vs. That, which allowed subscribers to receive one experiment per week from the Research Library.
Since this was our main website, we hypothesized that maybe the more general offer of “Staying Informed” would work better for the visitors. We decided to set up a test that would allow us to evaluate these two different offers.
experiments.keyLearnings: We discovered that the “Stay Informed” offer led to an infinitely better number of emails captured (none versus some is by definition infinitely better). While the This vs. That newsletter is still one of our more popular offers, a more general contact form was a better fit given the motivation of the people visiting the website.

experiments.title: How adding a brand name in the sender line affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a brand name to the sender line increase open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2016 - 03/07/2016
NextAfter sent an email promoting their new eBook “Turning Likes Into Donors” to a rented email list of nonprofit development professionals. The email was sent from Tim Kachuriak, and meant to look like a personal email. However, we wondered — if one of the recipients of the email wasn’t familiar with Tim, would they be likely to open and click the email? We developed a test that added “NextAfter” to the sender line to give the recipient better clarity in regards to the sender and launched an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings: The added brand name reduced clickthrough rate by 20.7%. This shows that the presence of a brand name actually decreases the likelihood that the user will engage. This may be because the brand is as unknown as the name. This experiment also may suggest that people are more willing to engage with a name they don’t know, but not if it’s evident that they are being marketed to.

But we wanted to see if the send time had a direct affect on click through rate as well.
experiments.keyLearnings: With a valid result of a 38.8% relative increase in click through rate, we discovered that the people on our house file are more ready to engage with a live broadcast offer in the mornings rather than the evenings.

experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad variation will the nonprofit professionals audience respond to more?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2016 - 08/01/2016
While setting up the advertising and promotions for an upcoming live broadcast, we wanted to determine if our various audience groups for our Facebook Ads would respond differently to various ad treatments. We have 3 main audience groups that we ran this test with: our housefile, housefile lookalikes, and nonprofit professionals.
Our original live broadcast Facebook Ad focused on the speaker and the title, but we thought people may be more motivated by different aspects of the focus topic. For the treatments, we developed an ad that focused on “Growing Your Donor File” and one that focused on “Empathy.”
experiments.keyLearnings: The “Growing Your Donor File” treatment greatly out performed both the control and treatment 2, lifting click rate by 277% with the nonprofit professionals audience. This learning will allow us to increase our total leads for this specific live broadcast. For future live broadcasts, it shows us that it will be more effective to focus our advertising on the learning and results that someone will get out of it, rather than the speaker and their qualifications.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/15/2016 - 07/15/2016
NextAfter has a research blog we run Facebook advertising on to reach new audiences with our nonprofit fundraising optimization content. We wondered if simply altering the call to action on one of these ads would affect the click rate.
The control call to action was simply the name of the blog, while the treatment turned the call to action into an actual action saying “Learn How to…”
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned that altering the call to action to be focused on ‘learning’ creates a 74% drop in click rate. It’s possible that the call to action of “Learn How to Humanize Your Email Fundraising” did not provide enough clarity about the nature of the content. In contrast, the control of “5 Ways to Humanize Your Email Appeals” provided a clear description of the type of content, how long it was, and exactly what aspect of fundraising it applied to.

experiments.title: Will previewing the content of a blog post in an email lead to more clicks through to the post?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that previewing the content of a blog for those in our NIO Cultivation series will achieve an increase in clicks to the full post.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/19/2021
For our 2021 NIO Cultivation series, we were curious whether it would be more effective to preview the content we were sharing in the email itself, or simply just share the link and let the recipient do the work to view the content. In this particular email we were sharing a blog post. The control of the email previewed one of the findings from the blog post and shared images of an experiment, where the treatment was a short message simply just passing along the link to the post.
experiments.keyLearnings:
It's difficult to isolate the exact variable that led to the decrease in traffic on the treatment version of the email. The location of the link in the depth of the email could have played a factor. Because the treatment version did not link to the article until the very end, people may have abandoned the email by that point. Another factor could be that the shorter version created mystery about the content, encouraging recipients to click in and learn more.

 

Moving forward in this cultivation series, we will follow the shorter email structure. But we will definitely need to test this again and better the isolate variables.

experiments.title: How Creating Mystery in the Subject Line can Influence Conversion Rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will creating more mystery in the subject line result in greater opens?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2017 - 10/09/2017
When setting up this email to promote an upcoming year-end webinar, we wondered if placing the percent difference between “#GivingTuesday” and the “last week of the calendar year” in the subject line was giving away too much too soon.
By moving the percentage to the body of the email, rather than giving away the result of our analysis in the subject line, we thought we could create more mystery and drive a greater desire to open the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Not only did we see a 10% lift in open rate, but this led to a 35% increase in the amount of recipients registering for the webinar.

By creating more tension and mystery in the subject line, we gave recipients a reason to open the email and engage with the content.

experiments.title: How Bullet Points in a Facebook Ad Affect the Clarity of the Value Proposition [Housefile]
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of bullet points in a Facebook ad make the value of the course clearer, and lead to more course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 10/10/2017
When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below are from the ad set targeting our housefile.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this ad set, we saw a 184% lift in conversion rate from the ads that used bullet points. Additionally, we say a 32% decrease in cost-per-lead.

Interestingly enough, we saw the opposite result with an ad set targeting people unfamiliar with NextAfter. I would hypothesize that since we have already established a sense of trust with our housefile, they do not require as deep of an understanding of the value proposition of our offers.

By bullet pointing the key aspects of the course, someone who already trusts us is able to see how the course is relevant to them and decide to register. The ads without bullet points have the key aspects of the course buried in paragraph form, making it harder for someone to quickly see whether or not this is a resource they’re interested in.

We launched this test across each target audience. The results below are from the ad set targeting our housefile lookalikes.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The use of bullet points with the housefile lookalikes audience saw an 81% decrease in course registrations. This result, combined with the result of the Network for Good audience test, continues to confirm our hypothesis that the use of bullet points is less effective with an audience that is not familiar with NextAfter.

They need to see the full value proposition of the offer in order to understand its credibility and depth, whereas our housefile already trusts our work and just needs to quickly see whether the offer is relevant to them.

experiments.title: How making the phone number optional affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the phone number field optional for visitors to the Global Online Fundraising Scorecard page will achieve more report downloads.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2021 - 02/19/2021
In this experiment, we had launched The Global Online Fundraising Scorecard in partnership with Salesforce.org. Salesforce often requires a phone number in order to follow-up with new contacts, but we wondered if we could actually get more total report downloads by making the phone number field optional.
The control used a required phone number field. The treatment used an optional phone number field.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The optional phone number field increases conversions by 20.6% at a 98% level of confidence. This is further evidence that requiring information that is "non-essential" to the visitor introduces friction that can keep someone from saying "yes" to your offer.
However, the caveat is that we ended up with fewer phone numbers from the treatment page.
The control version (required phone number) captured phone numbers for 30% of page traffic. The treatment (optional phone number) acquired the phone number for 16.3% of traffic.
A next step from this experiment would be to evaluate the quality of the phone numbers and follow-up calls from the "Required" version versus the "Optional." Intuition would say that phone numbers given willingly would be more receptive to a phone call. But phone numbers given when it was required would be less receptive. However, more analysis will be needed to determine this.

experiments.title: How a unique, more mysterious subject line impacts engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a very unique, mysterious subject line for email subscribers will achieve a greater open rate..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/15/2020
For our bi-weekly digest email where we send through a few different links and resources related to online fundraising, we wanted to see how we could increase engagement (opens) and also, hopefully, increase the number of clicks. We’ve tested a number of different subject lines like personalization but, generally speaking, we default to something that is very clear as to the utility someone would get from opening the email. So we wanted to see if a pretty unique, creative subject line would stand out more.
In this case, we used a treatment that made it seem like we forgot to create the subject line so it was like a note to ourselves to insert a great subject line. We used some square brackets [like this] to call it out further.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With almost a 100% LoC (99.9%) the unique, mystery subject line led to an increase in opens which more than anything underscores how changing up the type of subject lines used can help keep people engaged. In this case, the [square brackets] may have helped it stand out but our theory is the more humourous and unique element of the subject line helped lead to more opens.

Interestingly, with a true 100% LoC (100%) we saw a 93.3% increase in click rate and a 63.8% increase in click to open rate (99.9% LoC) with the unique, mysterious subject line. This underscores both the relationship between the open rate and click rate and how the subject line can influence a reader's motivation before they even get into the body/content of the email itself.

experiments.title: How a different offer on a confirmation page affects engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Which confirmation page offer will lead to more click-throughs?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/13/2017 - 12/01/2016
When people completed their registration for a NextAfter live broadcast, we wanted to give them an opportunity to engage with other resources that we offer. Our original control prompted registrants to take our free FCORM assessment, but we hypothesized that this might not be the right next step for most people.
For the treatment, we created a page that presented multiple eBook offers that addressed a variety of topics. Our primary goal with this experiment was simply to get people to click through to explore more of our content.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our treatment saw a 156.7% increase in clicks, confirming our initial hypothesis that the FCORM offer was not the right next step. Our general audience is more motivated to learn practical insights that inform their marketing and fundraising.

Additionally, Google Analytics data tells us that those who clicked through the FCORM offer abandoned the site within 1 or 2 steps. Of those who clicked through the treatment page, 51% went on to download at least one other offer, and 41% viewed at least 5 more pages before exiting the site.

experiments.title: How the right offer affects emails acquired
experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer in the footer of the NextAfter website will lead to the greatest number of emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/28/2015 - 11/10/2015
When we first launched the new NextAfter.com website, we had some internal debate about the best offer to use in the footer of the site. Historically, we had offered a subscription to our weekly newsletter, This vs. That, which allowed subscribers to receive one experiment per week from the Research Library.
Since this was our main website, we hypothesized that maybe the more general offer of “Staying Informed” would work better for the visitors. We decided to set up a test that would allow us to evaluate these two different offers.
experiments.keyLearnings: We discovered that the “Stay Informed” offer led to an infinitely better number of emails captured (none versus some is by definition infinitely better). While the This vs. That newsletter is still one of our more popular offers, a more general contact form was a better fit given the motivation of the people visiting the website.

experiments.title: How using an email forward can affect response rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will retargeting a “non-opens” audience segment with an email forward result in a higher response rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/25/2016 - 10/27/2016
When promoting an upcoming NextAfter live broadcast, we wanted to retarget those who did not open the initial email inviting them to the event.
We wondered if creating an email that had the appearance of an email forward could help make the resend email feel even more like a personal follow-up, and prompt more people sign up for the live broadcast.
experiments.keyLearnings: The email forward saw a 64.9% drop in response rate. Our hypotheses is that the email forward gave the appearance of being spam, thus resulting in a lower response rate.

experiments.title: How a different value proposition affects click rate in a Facebook Ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will drive the most clicks from a Facebook Ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2016 - 08/12/2016
We released a research study on nonprofit value proposition and advertised it through Facebook Ads to people affiliated with NextAfter including emails on file and website visitors.
There are many different aspects of the value proposition that we could focus on for the promotion of the study, so we wanted to test 3 primary value propositions to see what resonated the most with our audience.
The control focused on the depth of the study. Treatment 1 focused on the 4 key components of a value proposition. And treatment 2 focused on the end result of an effective value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For the audience that has already engaged with us in some way, it was 254% more effective to focus on the results of more names, donors, and dollars. This will inform the way the we promote and communicate this study to our website traffic and engaged contacts.

This also points to an underlying motivation of our engaged contacts: these people are looking for results in their fundraising.

experiments.title: How clarity in the design of a Facebook ad can affect conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Which Facebook ad design will acquire more email addresses?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2016 - 09/21/2016
When promoting the Why Should I Give to You? research study on nonprofit value proposition, we came to the realization that our Facebook ads were a little bit hard to read. The yellow text against the light gray background seemed to get lost when scrolling through a Facebook feed.
This led us to the hypothesis that a clearer design would yield at least a greater conversion rate. As a result, we developed a treatment version of the ad that presented the same information, but with a clearer design that would stand out more in the Facebook newsfeed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment saw a 56.7% decrease in conversion rate. Initially, this was very confusing. How could a design that appeared so much stronger be so ineffective by comparison? We almost didn’t even run a test on this new design because we were so certain it would outperform the control.

Taking a second look, we realized that the slight change of phrasing in order to improve the design aesthetic may have decreased the clarity of the content of the offer.

There are two main learnings we can glean from this test:
There are no marketing experts, design experts, advertising experts, etc. There is only experience. And the only way to know what is more effective is to test
 	Language and copy that makes sense to you doesn’t always make sense to your audience. In this case, the copy in the treatment design made complete sense to us, yet it appears to not have been quite as clear to the Facebook audience.

When NextAfter site visitors downloaded the eBook Why Should I Give to You?, we wanted to drive them to the Nonprofit Innovation & Optimization Summit website.
We wondered if visitors would be more motivated to check out the conference by an overall summary of what they would take away, or through specifically featuring some of the top slated speakers.

experiments.title: How adding preview links to a digest email increases click throughs
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding preview links for digest email recipients will achieve more click throughs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/23/2022 - 03/23/2022
After getting some inspiration from the Marketing Brew’s daily emails, I wondered if the same idea of adding preview links above the full content of a digest email would provide more click throughs.
The goal was to create ease for those who don’t want to read the full email but get straight to the content, but also provide more context about the content for those who want it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was a 30% increase in click throughs on the version of the email with the preview links. Further, looking at the heat map on version b of the email, the highest percentage of link clicks were on the preview links. We can gather that most subscribers prefer to just click direct to the content and do not need more context.
Next, we should test preview links with no further context vs. preview links with context.

experiments.title: How a simplified eBook ad image impacted email signup rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the eBook image in our ad for Facebook prospects will achieve increase email signup rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/03/2022
As a part of our experimentation with new subscriber acquisition offers, we theorized that the amount of text and call-outs on the control ad image used to promote our Facebook Ad Template offer created anxiety — which is defined as the “psychological resistance to take action” — by looking so busy.
Therefore, we decided to simplify the eBook image cover and use it in an ad to see if we could generate more clicks and increase the number of people signing up after they clicked the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, the treatment produced 53.2% fewer email signups.

Our hypothesis that simplifying the eBook image cover actually worked against us in this case. Now the question becomes: Why?

There are some other data points that are worth considering, as well. They are:
We observed that the treatment ad variant had a 1.35x higher CPM — meaning that Facebook charged us more to reach 1,000 people when compared to the control audience.
 	When the above note is combined with the fact that we saw a 2.5x higher cost per click — the treatment never really had a chance, but it's worth noting also ...
 	Even once we generated a click from the treatment ad variant, we saw 12% fewer people signing up for the offer once they had arrived on the landing page.
less is not more

experiments.title: How the “lowest cost per click” conversion event impacted full video promotional ad views on Facebook Ads Manager
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the optimizing our video ad campaign for lowest cost per click for prospective and known audiences we were targeting in our ad campaign will achieve only increase clicks, but not increase full video completion rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/10/2022
As a part of the NIO Summit 2022 promotional paid advertising campaign to sell event tickets, we wanted to experiment with what kind of video conversion event would produce the best balance between impressions, reach, clicks, and full promotional video views.
Our natural thought was to promote the video ads via “reach” campaign types, but our desire as direct response marketers was to test that natural inclination against a more “direct response” like conversion event of “lowest cost per click.”
How would the video ad start and finish rates play out while balanced against clicks and reach?
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 100% level of confidence, we observed that full video views on the "lowest cost per click" campaign surprisingly produced a staggering increase of full video views in the amount of +851.9% over the "reach" optimized campaigns.
It's worth noting that we did reach 85,286 with the "reach" optimized campaign when compared to just 10,287 prospects in the "lowest cost per click" optimized campaign.
Alternatively, we did see 9.5x more clicks (+7805% increase in ad link clicks, with a 100% level of confidence) in the "lowest cost per click" optimized campaign, though.
So, to summarize — although we reached 8.2x more people with the "reach" campaign, we saw 9.5x more link clicks with the "lowest cpc" campaign (and 1.14x more "full video views" as noted in the data above) over the "reach" optimized campaign.
Ultimately, it comes down to what you value and your ultimate goal ... if you're looking to just get your video ads in front of the largest number of people — you should optimize for the "reach" event type. However, if you're mostly interested in driving site visitors or full video views based upon who you reached, through your video campaigns — it makes sense to optimize for the "lowest cpc" event type.

experiments.title: How optimizing for video reach in a Facebook Ads campaign impacted video consumption
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that optimizing for reach for prospective NIO Summit ticket purchasing audiences will achieve increase the number of overall full video views.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/02/2022
As a part of promotional efforts for NextAfter’s 2022 NIO Summit event, we wanted to experiment with which event we should optimize for as it pertains to video consumption and reaching prospective ticket purchasers.
We set up an experiment on Facebook where one campaign would optimize for “ad recall lift,” while the other campaign was optimized for “reach.”
We were not just looking for reach, though — ultimately we wanted to see how many full video views either campaign would produce.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 100% level of confidence, we increased the reach of our videos to our prospective NIO Summit customers by +12.3%.
Remember, though, we were looking at which campaign event produced the most full video views — and with regards to that metric, we saw inconclusive data, achieving only a +10.2% increase with the "Reach" campaign, but with only an 18% level of confidence.
Both campaigns generated the same number of website clicks, which left us needing to make a decision on which way to go.
Therefore, we decided that rolling out the "Reach" campaign against a new "Lowest CPC" campaign would be our next step in the optimization of this campaign.

experiments.title: How focusing on the outcomes you can achieve with an offer impacted email signup rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on the outcomes of getting an offer for our target audience will achieve an increase in email signup rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/16/2022 - 08/05/2022
As a part of our own optimization efforts for an existing offer to fundraisers, we decided to look critically at one of our most popular offer landing pages, and come up with a new approach to see if it would improve email signup rates.
In reviewing the “control” version of the landing page — the message was focused on “what you will get.” We theorized that our target audience would only want to get this offer to achieve something. That led us to think through what it was that the target audience would want to achieve with this offer — and that led us to derive that they would like to raise more money online, and produce better results.
Therefore, we wrote a treatment to our landing page focused on “outcome-oriented” language, as opposed to what you get right now.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 97.2% level of confidence, we increased email signup rates by +15.4%.
This is an exciting study because it proves that focusing on what motivates your target audience to take action and download your offer should truly be the center of the messaging as to WHY you want them to have it.
There can be other motivation types beyond "raising more money" or "increasing performance" that may motivate this audience to download at a higher rate, so further experimentation may be required, however, these results are promising and should be considered when developing new offers, or trying to optimize old ones.

experiments.title: How a more direct, urgent and exclusive positioning of an offer impacted email signup rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being more direct, urgent, and exclusive in our invitation for subscribers and members at NextAfter  will achieve increase email signups.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/03/2022
As a part of promoting a new course, the team at NextAfter was going to send a special invitation email to subscribers to invite them to participate in an online training cohort through its NextAfter Institute.
In reviewing the control email invitation — the team focused on how a “radical redesign” treatment may increase demand and ultimately drive a higher number of cohort signups.
In reviewing the control, we identified a number of variables that could serve as a testing treatment — but with limited time to get the email out on time, we decided to hit on all variables that we isolated.
Specifically, we identified 6 variables that could be tested, and the treatment addressed every one, which are:
Anxiety — defined as “psychological resistance” for the audience to take the presented action. In our control, we identified that putting too much information (the training schedule) could lead less people to sign up.
Brevity/Directness —the length of the email was long, and much of the messaging wasn’t necessary for the reader to determine whether or not they wanted to signup for the cohort. So, we theorized that shortening the message and being more direct could drive a higher signup rate.
Anticipating Objections — We thought through why someone would decline to participate in the training cohort. Our top reason is that the training schedule didn’t permit them from participating in the live training sessions (on-time), so we wanted to address that in the email message.
Urgency — Both treatments talked about the upcoming start date for the training cohort that we were asking them to sign up for, but we wanted to increase the urgency that someone had to take action right now.
Exclusivity — We wanted the reader to feel special that they were being personally invited, and a part of group that would have first access to the new course/training cohort.
Intrigue — We wanted to pique the reader’s curiosity and leave some of the control email (the schedule) behind the click. By giving away too much in the inbox, we could prematurely force our audience to make a rash decision against signing up for the course. Therefore, we wanted to tease out more clicks and allow the strength of the landing page (and the cognitive momentum of them clicking to “learn more” about the course) to carry them through the registration process for the training cohort.
As noted, because there were too many variables to isolate and too little time to reasonably test successively each concept, we decided to develop a treatment to drive all of these elements into the treatment message shown below.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, we increased email signups for the new course (and training cohort) by +96.4%.
What's interesting is that we saw far more clicks (+76%, with a confidence level of 100%) on the treatment email, as well. There have been cases where if you pull back some information (through the "intrigue" concept), you can drive more clicks, but with a lower post-click conversion rate on the subsequent landing page.
This was not the case for the treatment email. In fact, it drove more clicks, and the landing page signup rate was actually validated (after the click) to increase email signups on the same landing page by +28.5% (with a confidence score of 99.6%).
This means that we didn't drive "exploratory clicks" from the treatment email, but rather that the clicks we drove from the treatment were more motivated to signup for the course than those clicking from the control email were.
Each of these variables should be isolated in subsequent testing to determine the efficacy of increasing motivation for each component used within the treatment message, but suffice it to say that you should consider each of these variables when attempting to drive a motivated click that is more likely to respond to your message in the way that you want.

experiments.title: Does a personal iPhone video on a Facebook Ad increase resource downloads?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an iPhone Facebook ad video for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in resource downloads as compared to our typical model.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2022 - 05/19/2022
For a couple months we were seeing our typical Facebook ad model with our tools and templates underperforming. It was time to shake things up and we wanted to take a more “human” approach to the creative rather than our typical image of the resource.
After creating a brand new New Donor Welcome Series Timeline tool using content from a previous NIO Summit session, we wondered if using a personal iPhone video created by the presenter would increase resource downloads as opposed to our typical Facebook ad model.
You’ll see in the screenshots that we tailored the copy in the treatment ad version to align more with the video itself.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Surprisingly, there was a 57% decrease in resource downloads from the video ad. However, it is important to note here that the control ad version had just 492 link clicks, where the treatment video ad version had 695 link clicks. These results show that the treatment version of the ad was much more successful at getting traffic to the landing page, but the landing page did not convert visitors.
Based on this observation, I can infer that the offer may not be the right match for this kind of ad. Moving forward we should test this same hypothesis on a different kind of offer.

experiments.title: How topic-focused versus Persona-focused calls-to-action affect email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that topic-driven CTAs for homepage traffic will achieve more site conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/11/2022 - 06/06/2022
When initially launching NextAfter.com, we had implemented specific calls-to-action on the homepage targeting 3 key personas: nonprofit executives, fundraising directors, and fundraising practitioners.
We wondered if a more topic-based approach could actually lead to more conversions from the homepage. The thinking was that many people are coming to our website looking for specific solutions – and giving clear categories related to common problems would lead to a more relevant  engagement.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The result was that a more topic-driven CTAs on the homepage were leaning towards a 9% decrease in overall conversions - but only at 72% level of confidence. Most interestingly – the topical CTAs led to a valid 25% decrease in conversion for returning visitors, but new site traffic saw a 28% increase in conversion at an 82% level of confidence.
This shows us that the traffic coming to our homepage is not of a singular motivation. There appears to be variance of motivation and intention of new VS returning traffic.
This will require more analysis of new VS visiting behavior to determine how we might customize a website experience for differing motivations.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning visitors had a 25.9% decrease in all downloads and registrations with a 100% level of confidence.New visitors had a 28% increase in all downloads and registrations with an 82% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How sending a personal follow-up affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the personal tone of the subject line and preview text in a resend affect open rate?
experiments.summary:

We wanted to re-send a live broadcast invitation to email recipients who got the original invitation, but never opened it. This is a fairly common practice – filter for non-opens and schedule your email for a different time, often with a different subject line that hits on a different aspect of urgency or importance.
We wondered if instead of running the resend in the typical marketing fashion, we could lift open rates by writing a personal follow-up email that you might get it if you didn’t respond to an email from a colleague.
experiments.keyLearnings: With an increase of 109.8% in open rate, we’ve learned that our email recipients are much more likely to engage with a re-send email that feels like a follow-up email a colleague or friend might send.

experiments.title: How a different messaging approach affects conversion rate on an offer page
experiments.researchQuestion: Which version of the value proposition for the email appeal template would lead to more downloads?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/19/2022
In this experiment, we were launching a new email appeal resource and came up with two strong ideas for positioning the appeal of the offer. One approach was to assure the reader that they’d have more confidence in the email appeals they would be sending out by following this template. The other approach was to assure them that this template would make their life easer by helping them have a simple and clear approach to writing their appeals.
We created a treatment to address the each version of the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The copy treatment that focused on making life easier for the fundraiser with a simple and clear approach led to a 26.3% increase in conversions compared to the treatment focused in having confidence in results.
This is an interesting insight into the mindset of the fundraiser. For those seeking out a tool like this, they appear to be less motivated by improving the outcomes and more motivated by tools that free up time and allow them to work faster. This is an approach we can continue to test and see if it holds up for other offers outside of this email appeal template.

experiments.title: Will using smart content to send a resource in a digest email lead to more resource downloads?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that targeting digest content for our general subscriber list will achieve more conversions than sending a general list of content.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2022 - 02/14/2022
For our monthly digest email we wanted to see if sending only one resource that we know a contact has not downloaded yet would be more effective than sending multiple resources, regardless of if they downloaded or not.
To do this we set up our control email which is the typical digest design sharing 4 resources with no smart content. This was sent to half of our general subscribers list.
For the experiment group, we set up a series of 4 emails each only sharing one resource and recognizing that we know they’ve previously downloaded something. We used a workflow in Hubspot to filter contacts by form submission so that each contact would only receive a resource that they haven’t downloaded before. If a contact had downloaded all resources they received a blog post. This was sent to the other half of our general subscribers list.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment led to a 51% increase in conversions with a 99% confidence. This tells us that our contacts are most engaged when there is less clutter and the content is specific to them. Moving forward, we will use smart content to provide a better experience and more tailored content to our subscribers.

experiments.title: How an embedded form in a dear-reader ad affects course signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that embedding the registration for in the ad for blog post readers will achieve more course registrations.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/20/2021
In this experiment, we launched a dear-reader ad on our blog posts to promote free year-end fundraising course. We wanted to know if it would be more effective to include a call-to-action button that drives people to the landing page to learn more and activate their course, or to include the full course registration form in the dead-reader ad itself.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Despite the length of the registration form, it was more effective to embed the full course registration form in the dear-reader ad then to link readers to the landing page to learn more and register. Embedding the form led to a 94.8% increase in course registrations.
As we ramp up year-end content promotion and traffic generation campaigns, this optimized dear-reader ad should help us get the most from blog traffic generation.
It's noteworthy that this embedded form was even more impactful for desktop users as well as new visitors. It was approaching validity for mobile users (80% level of confidence). For returning visitors, it made minimal difference – the thought here being that returning users have a higher level of trust in our content and therefore their motivation is high enough to overcome the friction of an additional step in the process.

experiments.title: How justifying why you sent the email right away affects email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that justifying why we’re sending the email sooner in the copy for our email housefile will achieve more ebook downloads.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2020 - 12/14/2020
We had previously run an a/b test where we saw that telling a longer personal story was more effective at generating clicks than a brief personal story. But I wondered if actually getting to the point of why we’re emailing right off the bat might be even more effective – while still utilizing a personal tone and format.
Version A led with a personal note about the holiday season and how our family was getting in the Christmas spirit. Version B jumped right to the primary reason I was emailing – to give them a free ebook to increase their year-end revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Getting straight to the point and justifying why we are emailing right away led to a 36% increase in people downloading the ebook. In fact, it also led to a valid increase in every key metric: opens, clicks, and conversions (see below).
While we have clearly seen the power of being human and personal in email marketing, this experiment shows us that our subscribers are looking at their inbox and engaging with emails that they consider to be important, helpful, and valuable. As a result, our email envelope and copy need to clearly convey the value of the content we're sharing.

experiments.title: How getting straight the point in your email copy leads to an increase in conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that justifying why we’re sending the email sooner in the copy for our email housefile will achieve more ebook downloads.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2020 - 12/14/2020
We had previously run an a/b test where we saw that telling a longer personal story was more effective at generating clicks than a brief personal story. But I wondered if actually getting to the point of why we’re emailing right off the bat might be even more effective – while still utilizing a personal tone and format.
Version A led with a personal note about the holiday season and how our family was getting in the Christmas spirit. Version B jumped right to the primary reason I was emailing – to give them a free ebook to increase their year-end revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Getting straight to the point and justifying why we are emailing right away led to a 36% increase in people downloading the ebook. In fact, it also led to a valid increase in every key metric: opens, clicks, and conversions (see below).

experiments.title: Will emphasizing a sale deadline in an email subject line increase email opens?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting the discount deadline for a NIO discounted ticket deadline reminder email will achieve an increase in email opens.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/16/2021
Approaching a NIO Summit discounted ticket deadline we sent out a reminder email that the discount was ending on Friday of that week. We wondered if highlighting the deadline in the subject line would create urgency and increase email opens.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was an increase of about 9% in the open rate of our treatment email which aligns with our hypothesis. Notably, while the more urgent subject line increased the open rate of the email, the click-through rate to the ticket checkout page was about the same for both versions.
After reviewing these results, we cannot definitively say that the reason for the increase is the highlight of the deadline in the treatment subject line because it is not an isolated variable. The control subject line mentions NIO as "in-person" which we did not mention in the treatment version.
This hypothesis requires further testing with more isolated variables.

experiments.title: How a unique, more mysterious subject line impacts engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a very unique, mysterious subject line for email subscribers will achieve a greater open rate..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/15/2020
For our bi-weekly digest email where we send through a few different links and resources related to online fundraising we wanted to see how we could increase engagement (opens) and also, hopefully, increase the number of clicks. We’ve tested a number of different subject lines like personalization but, generally speaking, we default to something that is very clear as to the utility someone would get from opening the email. So we wanted to see if a pretty unique, creative subject line would stand out more.
In this case, we used a treatment that made it seem like we forgot to create the subject line so it was like a note to ourselves to insert a great subject line. We used some square brackets [like this] to call it out further.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With almost a 100% LoC (99.9%) the unique, mystery subject line led to an increase in opens which more than anything underscores how changing up the type of subject lines used can help keep people engaged. In this case, the [square brackets] may have helped it stand out but our theory is the more humourous and unique element of the subject line helped lead to more opens.

Interestingly, with a true 100% LoC (100%) we saw a 93.3% increase in click rate and a 63.8% increase in click to open rate (99.9% LoC) with the unique, mysterious subject line. This underscores both the relationship between the open rate and click rate and how the subject line can influence a reader's motivation before they even get into the body/content of the email itself.

experiments.title: How visually changing the “Give Monthly” button impacts traffic to the donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Would subtle animation on the Give Monthly donate button increase traffic to the monthly giving page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2020 - 06/12/2020
On this organization’s website, they present two options for people to give, “Donate” and “Give Monthly”. We hypothesized that we could increase traffic to the monthly donation page by visually setting off the “Give Monthly” button. To do this, we created a subtle animation on the “Give Monthly” button that used a heart icon that pulsed once and the effect would then ripple across the rest of the button. We split the traffic 50/50 and ran this experiment across the website’s entire navigation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The subtle animation on the "Give Monthly" donate button increase traffic to the monthly giving page by 19%! We weren't able to validate this experiment off of monthly donations because the conversion rate on the page is too low to validate.

The subtle animation on the treatment caught people's eye causing them to click through to the donation page. We hypothesize that this tactic may be a novelty. We will continue to monitor it's effectiveness and retest the approach again in two months to see if the novelty has worn off, or if the subtle animation really is a longer term tactic to increase clicks to the donation page.

experiments.title: Will a shorter, more direct email lead to more clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will as shorter, more direct email lead to more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/17/2020
NextAfter’s annual conference — the Nonprofit Innovation & Optimization Summit — moved to a virtual event due to COVID-19 but the change also further delayed the launch and promotion. For the first launch email, we wondered if a shorter, more to the point email may actually do better in driving clicks, engagement, and registrations (free and paid) as a lot of people on our list we (assume) know the event, know it’s quality, and may even be expecting the announcement so we didn’t need to spend as much time in the email explaining the event and sharing its value.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The shorter, more direct email significantly increased the number of clicks and people who went on to visit the landing/registration page. The goal of the email, of course, wasn't just to get clicks but to get registrations (both free and paid) so we wanted to look at that as well and here's what we found for free and paid registrations:
The treatment (shorter version) helped increase free registrations 46.6% (99.9% LoC - experiment here)The treatment (shorter version) saw a decrease in paid registrations of 60% (74.3% LoC - experiment here)
The shorter email not only sent much more traffic to the registration page but those that visited the page converted at almost the exact same rate (31.7% vs. 32.7%) which seems to validate that those who were interested in attending with a free ticket didn't need the extra copy at all to help convince them to register. We did see a lift, but not significant, for paid ticket registrations with the longer copy and a directional lift in paid registration conversion rate for those who clicked from the longer version so there may be a relationship with longer copy and paid registration but in this case it both wasn't clear and not worth the extra copy/time for overall performance.

In this experiment, we were running a pre-sign up offer for a year-end fundraising training course. There was quite a lot of copy on the landing page to communicate the value and various components of the training. And we wondered if using design elements could help users understand the copy better and lead to more conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings: The more designed landing page was the clear winner with a 40% increase in conversion at a 99% level of confidence. Going forward, we can be sure that design elements on a long form landing page like this can be used to make the page more scannable, easier to understand, and higher converting.

experiments.title: How more content options in an update email affects downstream engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will offering more content options lead to fewer bounces and greater website engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2018 - 12/10/2018
In this experiment, we had tested a different type of research update email using more content options, with less value proposition copy. The goal was to see if a more newsletter-like format could maintain or increase traffic. If so, this would give us a better way to share more of our content with our house file. The control was written in a long, personal format like normal using 3 curated links. The treatment used less copy, but provided 8 different links in more of digest email style.
When looking at clicks, there was no statistical difference. But we wanted to see if these more options affected website engagement since our subscribers had more types of content to choose something that was most relevant to them. To measure this, we looked at the amount of people that clicked through to a second page (the inverse of bounce rate).
experiments.keyLearnings: The newsletter email increased conversion to a second click by 63.7%. This indicates that, although changing the format does not increase traffic, the traffic is more engaged since the content they engage with is more relevant to their interests.

experiments.title: How an “Email Pause Button” affects unsubscribes
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding an option to pause email decrease unsubscribe rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2020 - 09/14/2020
We often have people request to take a break from our marketing emails for a time. This is normally because they are in the midst of a busy season or they just accessed a resource that gave them tons of ideas and want to focus on those for a bit.
So we created an “Email Pause Button” to allow users to pause emails for a time instead of unsubscribe.
Version A was a just a normal email. Version B included the pause link near the unsubscribe link.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Interestingly enough, the option to pause email actually increased unsubscribes by 90% – the opposite of what we had intended. It's possible that this is because it drew more attention to the unsubscribe option and asked people to make a decision – where normally they may not have even been considering unsubscribing.

However, I don't think this rules out the pausing emails as an option. It may be more effective to weave it into the unsubscribe process as an alternative option to opting-out.

experiments.title: How a more human subject line affects conversion rates

rows.name: A free copywriting training class for you

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more human sounding subject line lead to more registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/10/2020 - 08/11/2020
In this experiment, we were launching a free training course to help nonprofits improve their copywriting. The initial copy included a subject line that utilized sales-style tactics to try and win the open. But we wondered if a more humanized version of the subject line could lead to more opens – and possibly even more registrations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new subject line led to a statistically valid increase in a) Open, b) Clicks, and c) Course Registrations. In fact, the subject line made the most significant impact on registrations, leading to a 41.9% increase in people registering for the free training.
This experiment is another solid indication that making your subject line sound more believable, human, and empathetic impacts the perception of the entire email - as well as the landing page.

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a different type of offer lead to better email conversion rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2020 - 06/03/2020
In this experiment, we had an idea to promote 2 different offers related to donation pages. The first – an in-depth research study on donation pages – is the “higher quality” offer is there’s more research, data, examples, and tactics. But we wondered if our general donation page template might acquire more emails since it is simple, actionable, and requires less work to digest.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The general donation page template was the overwhelming winner, despite an even sample size because of Facebook's ad prioritization. It not only led to a 70% increase in conversion rate, but it was also 50% cheaper to acquire an email address.
A brief analysis of the quality of contacts indicated the general donation page emails may even be higher quality than the leads coming from the study.

experiments.title: How a “help” note on a form can increase phone field completion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a note next to the phone field lead to more phone field completions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/17/2020 - 03/24/2020
In this experiment, we had 2 primary questions we wanted to answer. First, we wondered if adding a note next to the “phone” field on a webinar registration explaining why we wanted their phone number would increase overall registrations. Second, we wondered if this same note would lead to more phone field completions (the phone number is an optional field).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The note about using the phone field to send a text about live webinars did not affect webinar registration (3% increase at approximately 30% level of confidence).
But adding the note next to the phone field did lead to greater field completions – 51.5% increase in completions at a 99% level of confidence. This means that we can confidently build a SMS opt-in audience via webinar registrations without compromising on webinar registrations.

experiments.title: Will an even more personal tone and approach increase engagement?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an even more personal tone and approach increase engagement?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/31/2020
Twice a month we send a ‘digest’ email that shares a variety of fundraising resources and research we’ve produced lately. Our emails are typically written in first person and quite ‘human’ and personal. In the week leading up to this digest, I shared a video of me shooting on a LittleTikes hoop in my backyard on LinkedIn that got a TON of engagement even though that’s not what I post and it’s not really the ‘personal’ social channel (assumption) and wondered what an even more ‘me’ focused email in terms of approach and personal tone would do with our audience.
In this case, we added a few short paragraphs talking about my personal life and even linked to my son, my dog, and myself shooting hoops in the backyard. Things that don’t really have any utility or value for the reader in terms of fundraising resources or ideas.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The even more personal tone led to an increase in clicks of 53.2% and indicates that people are fine with even more personal communication. Now some of the links had no 'business value' in terms of driving course signups or email series subscriptions and we did see a slight decline in conversion (11.2%) but it was not significant (47.9%) so it appears the more personal approach had no real difference on conversion although that is something to look at it future extra personal tests.
In addition to the increased engagement, we saw a slight (but also not significant) decline in unsubscribe rate which again hints that people don't hate more personal emails. We also saw 186.5% increase in replies which was interesting and a great way to engage with our subscribers and followers (one person even offered me a Daddy Saddle toy for my son...). This shows quite clearly that when we communicate as a human in more personal way it can lead to more engagement and conversations which is part of the goal of email.
Overall, this approach requires more testing as the increased clicks and replies is great but ideally we could also see a lift in actions and conversions.

Both the NIO Attendee list and the Interest Based Targeting list had significantly lower conversion rates. But, it was significantly cheaper to get acquire new traffic from the interest targeting list. In fact, the interest targeting was 28% cheaper per landing page view.

experiments.title: How the aspect ratio of a Facebook video ad affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a square cropped video ad increase webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/31/2020
In this experiment, we had tested into using a talking-head style video ad for some audiences in our webinar promotions. But we wondered if we could actually get more registrations by using a square cropped video, which is Facebook’s recommended video aspect ratio.
The control was the full aspect ratio video ad. The treatment was the square aspect ratio. Both videos were exactly the same in every other regard, including the subtitles.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The square cropped video ad led to a 78% decrease in webinar registrations.
In analyzing the results, I wondered if the square cropped video may have gotten more engagement, but fewer conversions purely based on how much the call-to-action gets pushed down when using the square cropping. Interestingly enough, the CPM cost we nearly identical and the engagement metrics for the square video were actually lower than the full aspect ratio video.
The square cropping seemed to fall short on every metric.
One caveat that might affect the results is simply that this particular webinar promotion performed below our typical performance. But with such a significant decrease at such a high level of confidence, I'm not inclined to test this strategy any further.

experiments.title: Which is more effective in a subject line: “Mystery + Value” or “Mystery + Hyper-Personalization”

rows.name: A new fundraising webinar you’ll want to mark your calendar for
Matt, I’ve been working on this for you

experiments.researchQuestion: Which combination of subject line elements will lead to greater opens?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/04/2020 - 03/18/2020
In this experiment, I was using NextAfter’s 5-step subject line creation tool to craft a subject line for a new webinar. I ended up with 2 different, equally viable subject lines. The first emphasized “utility” or “value” while not giving away the exact topic of the webinar. The second was equally “mysterious” while laying heavily into personalization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The heavily personalized version led to a 25.5% increase in opens at a 99.9% level of confidence. But interestingly enough, there was no statistically significant change in the number of people who signed up for the webinar.
This is yet another example of how the subject line plays a crucial role in setting up how someone will receive and perceive the rest of the email. In this case, it's likely that people opened the treatment because of the additional personalization and mystery, but ultimately weren't inclined to join a webinar. While the opens generated by the control were already primed to engage with a webinar - leading to a higher conversion rate from those that did open the email.
In a case like this where the net signups are a wash - I will always err on the side of the email with greater opens. At the most basic level, getting more opens can lead to better deliverability, and lead to stronger relationships with the folks on our email file.

experiments.title: How a talking head video in a Facebook ad affects webinar registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a talking head style facebook ad lead to more webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/07/2020 - 01/20/2020
I see video-based Facebook ads all the time for different courses and trainings, and they always drive me crazy. But if they’re being used all the time, I wondered if they might just be more effective than static images. In this experiment, we tested using a static webinar title image (which has been our typical ad design for many months) against a “talking head” style video.
The video is of me in our webinar studio giving a high-level description of the webinar and what viewers can expect. It contained subtitles so that it can easily be understood with the sound off.
experiments.keyLearnings: Looking specifically at our “All Site Traffic” retargeting audience, we saw a significant, 136.8% increase in webinar registrations. On top of that, our cost per registrant was 54% lower. We will continue to test more video-based ads that use the personal and organic approach to see if the results hold true and apply in other contexts.

experiments.title: How a different messaging angle affects conversion rate from a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition angle will lead to more report downloads?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/01/2020 - 01/08/2020
At the start of the year, we launched a new Facebook ad campaign promoting the new State of Nonprofit Email Cultivation research report. As this is the first time to push the report in a focused way, we want to test a couple different messaging angles that each seemed to be a viable approach.
The control copy focused in on known stats about donor retention in order to a make the argument that something is broken with our cultivation strategies.
The treatment copy focused on the donor’s experience, illustrating how frustrated and jaded donors can feel if they are not cultivated well.
Each version ended up making the same call-to-action, using the same headline, image, etc. The primary change was the first 2-3 paragraphs of copy to set up the call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
From this experiment, we learned that the "frustrated and jaded donors" approach is a more effective messaging approach – to the tune of a 168% increase in conversion over the control. It is interesting to see that data alone does not always move people to action. But using copy to make a more personal and human connection – and helping fundraisers to empathize with donors – can be a stronger copywriting method.
We'll need to put this broader learning to the test in other scenarios to see if it holds true.

experiments.title: How a different value proposition affects webinar registrations from a Facebook ad

rows.name: Are You Ready for Giving Tuesday?

experiments.researchQuestion: Will addressing a common misconception about Giving Tuesday in the Facebook ad lead to greater registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2019 - 11/15/2019
In this experiment, we were running Facebook ads to drive registrants for a webinar on Giving Tuesday strategies. I wrote the control version of the ads to be similar to the copy being used in the invitation email and the landing page. But after sending the initial invite, I received some unexpected responses from people telling my why they weren’t actually participating in Giving Tuesday this year.
After seeing this feedback, I wrote a treatment version of the ad playing off a common theme I heard in the email responses. Many people had the misconception that you need a large social media following in order to have any level of success on Giving Tuesday.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment version led to a 57% increase in webinar registrations. This experiment gives us a couple key takeaways...
We now have a better starting place for marketing Giving Tuesday content in the future.It shows the impact of utilizing customer/donor feedback to influence your marketing/fundraising copy. By listening to our subscribers and addressing common concerns in the copy, we were able to align the value proposition of our product with a deeper need.

experiments.title: How the headline on a blog post Facebook ad affects landing page views

rows.name: Headline Using Post Title and Basic CTA

experiments.researchQuestion: Which style of Facebook ad headline will drive more traffic to the blog post?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/05/2019 - 11/08/2019
In this experiment, we were launching Facebook ads to drive traffic to a newly updated year-end fundraising blog. In developing the ads, we wondered what type of headline would be most effective at driving visits to the blog post. We have seen strong call-to-actions be effective for conversion focused ads, but wondered if that would hold up in an ad purely designed to drive traffic.
Version A used simply the post title as it might appear if someone shared the post organically. Version B used a headline that packed the specific value of the blog post into an action statement. Version C took a hybrid approach of putting the blog title first, followed by a simple call-to-action separated by a pipe.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The hybrid approach was the overwhelming winner, increasing landing page views (people that made it to the page and loaded it) by 190%. This shows us a few things...
A simple blog post title is not enough to call a Facebook viewer to take an action, even though this is the more natural appearance of an organic post.The value heavy CTA, although we have seen it be effective for driving someone to convert on a specific offer, is not nearly as powerful for driving pure traffic. In this case, our goal is to drive traffic to build an audience, not get a specific download or opt-in instantly.The hybrid approach clearly communicates the topic, and it clearly communicates the medium. The more action oriented headline could cause confusion about what the actual content is on the other end. But the hybrid approach makes it abundantly clear what is expected of the Facebook viewer: "Read the post".

In this experiment, we were sending in invitation email out to our house file about an upcoming webinar previewing a brand new study on donor cultivation. We wondered which type of subject line would perform better – one that asks a question, or one that directly states the content.
experiments.keyLearnings: The more direct and concise call to action led to a 57% increase in webinar registrations at a 99% level of confidence. Interestingly enough, this was not solely a result of an increase in opens. Opens increased by about 9%, and clicks increased by 39%. This is a great example of how a subject line can have major downstream impact and set an expectation in the recipients mind of what content is to follow.

Ended On: 07/22/2019
When we redesigned the NextAfter blog, one of the goals was to increase subscribers/email signups through both the placement and message of the offer. One new sign up placement was a  a large call to action area above the blog and we wanted to see which message would encourage people to sign up. For the control, we kept our old/standard value proposition about what you will get if you sign up but for the treatment, we added in the number of people subscribed and who they were — fundraisers and nonprofit marketers — to add social proof and clarity.
experiments.keyLearnings: By using social proof, number of others subscribing the blog, it helped increase conversion as it helps build credibility (if that many other people read it can't be bad) and possibly even a bit of 'loss aversion' (I don't want to miss out on what they are getting). The added clarity of saying who else is subscribing and who the blog is for — fundraisers and nonprofit marketers — also could've played a role.

experiments.title: How location based imagery affects click-through rate of a Facebook Ad campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Will location specific imagery lead to greater landing page click-throughs in a geo-targeted ad campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2019 - 06/18/2019
In this experiment, we were advertising online fundraising workshops hosted by NextAfter Institute. Each specific location had it’s own geo-targeted Facebook Ad campaign. The intention was to keep the workshop in their mind during the course of the sales period and drive them back to the landing page to consider attending.
We knew our sample size would be too small to optimized for ticket sales, but we hoped to run an experiment to optimize our landing page click-throughs.
The control ads used a picture of a course instructor teaching in a room full of attendees. Common advertising recommendations are to use people in imagery to make it relatable. But we wondered if using images specific to someone’s city would actually be more a powerful driver to show that the training was more specific and local to them.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment ad set resulted in a 64% increase in click-throughs to the landing page at a 99% level of confidence. From this, we can learn that local-recognition is a more significant motivating factor than showing imagery of what the workshops actually look like. This will be our new default ad-set to start testing on to try and drive even more traffic to these workshops.
It's worth noting that these results are an aggregate of 6 different geo-targeted campaigns including Chicago, Dallas, Boston, Toronto, Vancouver, and London. Each campaign ran for 2-3 months between March 2019 and June 2019.

This shows us that focusing the ad headline on the value proposition with this audience helps create additional clarity about the offer, and increases the liklihood that someone will register.

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a value proposition focused headline result in greater webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/30/2017 - 11/10/2017
When launching a Facebook ad campaign for an upcoming webinar, we wondered if the traditional “Learn More” headline was strong enough of a headline to prompt someone to click and register. We thought it was possible that packing more value proposition into the headline could create more clarity up front, and result in greater conversion rates.
We created a treatment using “Optimize Your Donation Pages with These 21 Elements” as the new headline, and ran the test. The results below are from the as set targeting the NextAfter Housefile Lookalikes audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The control received zero webinar registrations while the treatment had a conversion rate of 1.41%. Since this experiment reached a statistical confidence and a 99% statistical power, we can safely conclude that our Facebook audience of Housefile Lookalikes needed the additional clarity of the value proposition in order to fully understand why this webinar is valuable to them.

This result runs contrary to what we saw with the “Nonprofit News Sources” audience which yielded the opposite result. It’s possible that this value proposition is more relevant to and easily understood by our lookalikes. But those following major nonprofit publications may not have as clear of an understanding of “optimization” or a desire to learn the practicalities of creating a donation page.

When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below are from the ad set targeting our housefile lookalikes.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The use of bullet points with the housefile lookalikes audience saw an 81% decrease in course registrations. This result, combined with the result of the Network for Good audience test, continues to confirm our hypothesis that the use of bullet points is less effective with an audience that is not familiar with NextAfter.
They need to see the full value proposition of the offer in order to understand its credibility and depth, whereas our housefile already trusts our work and just needs to quickly see whether the offer is relevant to them.

experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of bullet points in a Facebook ad make the value of the course clearer, and lead to more course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 10/10/2017
When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below are from the ad set targeting our housefile.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this ad set, we saw a 184% lift in conversion rate from the ads that used bullet points. Additionally, we say a 32% decrease in cost-per-lead.
Interestingly enough, we saw the opposite result with an ad set targeting people unfamiliar with NextAfter. I would hypothesize that since we have already established a sense of trust with our housefile, they do not require as deep of an understanding of the value proposition of our offers.
By bullet pointing the key aspects of the course, someone who already trusts us is able to see how the course is relevant to them and decide to register. The ads without bullet points have the key aspects of the course buried in paragraph form, making it harder for someone to quickly see whether or not this is a resource they’re interested in.

experiments.title: How a broader targeting strategy with a lifetime budget affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will broader audience targeting with a lifetime budget acquire more course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 10/10/2017
When launching Facebook ads for our new online course, we had been encouraged by a partner to target a broader audience and allow Facebook to do to the work of optimizing the audience based on conversions.
We set up our targeting in the traditional way, but we also set up a broader ad set targeting people in the United States within our target age range. The budget was set for a lifetime spend over the course of a month.
We wanted to see if this targeting recommendation would outperform our traditional targeting both in conversions and in cost per lead.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, this targeting methodology did not work for this offer. We saw a 75% decrease in registrations, and a 208% increase in cost-per-lead after running it for 8 days.
It’s possible that spending more up front could lead to enough conversions for Facebook to learn which users are most likely to convert – causing the cost-per-lead to level off over time. But even in this is the case, the cost-per-lead would have to come down enough to off-set the initial investment.
