Not only did we see a 10% lift in open rate, but this led to a 35% increase in the amount of recipients registering for the webinar.
By creating more tension and mystery in the subject line, we gave recipients a reason to open the email and engage with the content.

When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.

For this audience, the result was a resounding “No.” The Network for Good audience had a 75% decrease in course registrations when shown the bullet points ad.
Each post had a comparable amount of clicks to the landing page, implying that people who viewed the control were more primed to complete their registration. It’s possible that by adding these bullet points, it made it easier for someone to skim through the post and not experience the full value proposition of the offer.
This learning should not be taken out of context and applied to every audience. Other ad sets targeting different audience segments are approaching validity with the Bullet Point ad as the winning treatment.

experiments.title: How “personalized” ad copy affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will personalized ad copy increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2015 - 10/06/2015
The Texas State Historical Association launched a free eBook offer about Texas’ involvement in the Civil War. They wanted to test whether personalizing ad copy would increase clickthrough rate, so they made two variations of the same ad, with a slight but significant word change. The first set gave viewers the offer to get “the” free eBook, while the second personalized the offer with get “your” free eBook. We hypothesized that personalizing the offer would increase clickthrough rate and result in more email addresses acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment ad copy decreased conversion by nearly 25% — which showed us that viewers of the ads didn’t respond nearly as well to a personalized offer. This indicated that viewers of these ads didn’t feel that the book was already “theirs”, and that creating distance between the user and the offer enticed more of them to claim it. There are plenty of tests that show the opposite result, which means that it is always beneficial to test against “best practices”.

Timeframe: 02/01/2017 - 02/06/2017
When launching the promotion for a new NextAfter webinar, we wanted to ensure that we were choosing an effective title.
We had observed that many nonprofits are focused on acquisition during the first quarter or the year. This led us to hypothesize a title focused on “online donor acquisition” could be an effective way of getting the attention of nonprofit fundraisers.
But we also knew that many people we talk to specifically mention that they don’t know how to get value out of Facebook. Many have given up on it as a viable channel for fundraising. That led us to think that focusing our title around “Facebook fundraising” could also be effective.
The only way to know which would be most effective was to test.
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned that the treatment title “Cracking the Code of Facebook Fundraising” resulted in 197% more webinar registrations. By testing this through our Facebook advertising channels before rolling it out to our email file, we were able to capitalize on this learning in order to drive higher conversion throughout the promotional cycle.

For the treatment, we created a page that presented multiple eBook offers that addressed a variety of topics. Our primary goal with this experiment was simply to get people to click through to explore more of our content.

Our treatment saw a 156.7% increase in clicks, confirming our initial hypothesis that the FCORM offer was not the right next step. Our general audience is more motivated to learn practical insights that inform their marketing and fundraising.
Additionally, Google Analytics data tells us that those who clicked through the FCORM offer abandoned the site within 1 or 2 steps. Of those who clicked through the treatment page, 51% went on to download at least one other offer, and 41% viewed at least 5 more pages before exiting the site.

experiments.title: How clarity in the design of a Facebook ad can affect conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Which Facebook ad design will acquire more email addresses?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2016 - 09/21/2016
When promoting the Why Should I Give to You? research study on nonprofit value proposition, we came to the realization that our Facebook ads were a little bit hard to read. The yellow text against the light gray background seemed to get lost when scrolling through a Facebook feed.
This led us to the hypothesis that a clearer design would yield at least a greater conversion rate. As a result, we developed a treatment version of the ad that presented the same information, but with a clearer design that would stand out more in the Facebook newsfeed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment saw a 56.7% decrease in conversion rate. Initially, this was very confusing. How could a design that appeared so much stronger be so ineffective by comparison? We almost didn’t even run a test on this new design because we were so certain it would outperform the control.
Taking a second look, we realized that the slight change of phrasing in order to improve the design aesthetic may have decreased the clarity of the content of the offer.
There are two main learnings we can glean from this test:
There are no marketing experts, design experts, advertising experts, etc. There is only experience. And the only way to know what is more effective is to test.
Language and copy that makes sense to you doesn’t always make sense to your audience. In this case, the copy in the treatment design made complete sense to us, yet it appears to not have been quite as clear to the Facebook audience.

The control focused on the depth of the study. Treatment 1 focused on the 4 key components of a value proposition. And treatment 2 focused on the end result of an effective value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For the audience that has already engaged with us in some way, it was 254% more effective to focus on the results of more names, donors, and dollars. This will inform the way the we promote and communicate this study to our website traffic and engaged contacts.
This also points to an underlying motivation of our engaged contacts: these people are looking for results in their fundraising.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2016 - 08/10/2016
At the launch of our value proposition research study, Why Should I Give to You?, we ran Facebook across our typical Facebook audiences. We wanted to determine what components of the value proposition resonated most with different audience groups early on.
The control focused on the depth of the study, treatment 1 focused on the 4 key components of a value proposition, and treatment 2 focused on the result of an effective value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned that with our NPO Professionals target audience, the control that focused on the depth of the study resonated the most and drove the most clicks. Future tests will look at different variations of smaller variables within the control ad to perpetually optimize the click rate.

experiments.title: How different audience motivations affect click rate in a Facebook ad (Part 3)
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad variation will the housefile audience respond to more?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2016 - 08/04/2016
While setting up the advertising and promotions for an upcoming live broadcast, we wanted to determine if our various audience groups for our Facebook Ads would respond differently to various ad treatments. We have 3 main audience groups that we ran this test with: our housefile, housefile lookalikes, and nonprofit professionals.
Our original live broadcast Facebook Ad focused on the speaker and the title, but we thought people may be more motivated by different aspects of the focus topic. For the treatments, we developed an ad that focused on “Growing Your Donor File” and one that focused on “Empathy.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
Treatment 1 had a lift of 146.9% compared to the control. This confirms our hypothesis that this audience responds stronger to the learning and results rather than the speaker. Although, it is possible that the results could be different if the speaker was someone they were more familiar with.
Comparing this result to the result of our previous test on the housefile lookalike audience reveals a drastic difference in the motivations of two audiences that are supposedly similar. The lookalikes were 110% more likely to click on the empathy treatment, while the housefile was 26% less likely to click on the empathy treatment.

experiments.title: How a reinforcing the value proposition can affect conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page treatment will have the greatest affect on live broadcast registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/02/2016 - 08/02/2016
The landing page for an upcoming NextAfter live broadcast had a decent conversion rate already, but we wanted to see if we could lift it higher and get more people to sign-up to attend.
We hypothesized, as a result of a learning from a Facebook Ad test, that we could grow conversion rates by focusing less on the speaker, and more on the learning that results that an attendee would get out of the live broadcast. This involved both copy tweaks and visual that reinforced the idea of “growing your donor file.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that the treatment that placed a greater emphasis on “growing your donor file” and reinforced the value proposition with imagery gave a 53.4% lift in conversion rate. This confirmed our hypothesis that people are more motivated by the learning than the speaker.
This also showed us how a learning earlier in the conversion process can be tested downstream to grow conversion rates.

experiments.title: How the call to action of a Facebook ad affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Which Facebook ad call to action will generate more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/15/2016 - 07/15/2016
NextAfter has a research blog we run Facebook advertising on to reach new audiences with our nonprofit fundraising optimization content. We wondered if simply altering the call to action on one of these ads would affect the click rate.
The control call to action was simply the name of the blog, while the treatment turned the call to action into an actual action saying “Learn How to…”
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned that altering the call to action to be focused on ‘learning’ creates a 74% drop in click rate. It’s possible that the call to action of “Learn How to Humanize Your Email Fundraising” did not provide enough clarity about the nature of the content. In contrast, the control of “5 Ways to Humanize Your Email Appeals” provided a clear description of the type of content, how long it was, and exactly what aspect of fundraising it applied to.

With this test on the housefile lookalikes audience, we found that the “Empathy” treatment outperformed the control and treatment 1, lifting click rate by 110%. This further confirms our learning from our test with the nonprofit professionals audience that it is more effective to focus our ads on the topic and results that will come from the live broadcast rather than the speaker.
This also shows us that even similar audience groups can have varying motivations: the “Empathy” treatment was the winner with this audience, but the “Donor File” treatment was the winner with the nonprofit professionals audience.

experiments.title: How sending a personal follow-up affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the personal tone of the subject line and preview text in a resend affect open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/20/2016 - 07/21/2016
We wanted to re-send a live broadcast invitation to email recipients who got the original invitation, but never opened it. This is a fairly common practice – filter for non-opens and schedule your email for a different time, often with a different subject line that hits on a different aspect of urgency or importance.
We wondered if instead of running the resend in the typical marketing fashion, we could lift open rates by writing a personal follow-up email that you might get it if you didn’t respond to an email from a colleague.
experiments.keyLearnings: With an increase of 109.8% in open rate, we’ve learned that our email recipients are much more likely to engage with a re-send email that feels like a follow-up email a colleague or friend might send.

Timeframe: 05/26/2016 - 05/27/2016
During a promotion for a live broadcast, we wanted to test what affect send time would have on email engagement. We had measured the affect of send time on open rate before, and it had caused us to think that sending in the evening would create more engagement. But we wanted to see if the send time had a direct affect on click through rate as well.
experiments.keyLearnings: With a valid result of a 38.8% relative increase in click through rate, we discovered that the people on our house file are more ready to engage with a live broadcast offer in the mornings rather than the evenings.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/10/2015 - 10/02/2015
Not only did conversion increase significantly, there was a dropoff in the amount of negative feedback relating to this course’s enrollment. Making it clear that the visitor was enrolled was pivotal in reducing cognitive friction, which had a direct impact on the conversion rate. This copy was rolled out across all of Hillsdale’s course donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings: Hillsdale College had run a battery of tests on the donation page for their Constitution 101 course. Conversion had increased steadily, but there were still a lot of complaints coming in to the support team that indicated that new enrollees thought that they HAD to give a gift to take the course. It became obvious that a lack of clarity was causing cognitive friction and reducing donations, so they created a treatment in an attempt to plainly state that the visitor was now enrolled.

NextAfter was promoting their Nonprofit Innovation and Optimization Summit, a conference for nonprofit marketers. They were sending an in-depth story telling how the conference came together, and wanted to see what time of day got the best open rate. Were the members of their list earlybird or night owls?
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that our readers, at least in this one case, are night owls. This may indicate that they have less time during the day to read emails, and are more likely to consume this content at night. Even though the test was statistically valid, it warrants more testing.

experiments.researchQuestion: Will using donor-centric in the tribute widget increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2015 - 06/26/2015
Increasing personalization reduced clickthrough rate by 17.2% — which tells us that the additional language made the value proposition of the tribute widget less clear. Clarity trumps persuasion, which is proven yet again.
experiments.keyLearnings: CaringBridge wanted to run a test on their tribute widget to see if increased donor-centric language would improve clickthrough rate, which leads to a donation opportunity. They designed a treatment that addressed the donor (rather than making a “demand”) and told them what their donation would do — making it relevant to the page they were on. This was a two-layer test, as they were willing to sacrifice clickthrough rate if it led to more donations and revenue overall.

experiments.title: How selling the final call-to-action too early affects names acquired
experiments.researchQuestion: Does promoting member benefits on the initial email acquisition page increase the number of names and donors acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2015 - 10/01/2015
For the Texas State Historical Association, the primary source of new members is through a subsequent membership offer once a visitor downloads one of their free eBooks. This two step process consists of two landing pages; the first uses language that sells the value of the eBook with the subsequent page promoting the benefits of membership.
We had a hypothesis that the visitors to the eBook landing page might be motivated enough that they didn’t need as much language to “sell” of the eBook (it is given free in exchange for their email address). Instead, we could use the headline and subsequent paragraph to begin talking about the benefits of membership which would then make the subsequent offer on the second page more attractive.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By promoting a TSHA membership before first “selling” the initial name acquisition offer (the eBook), we ended up decreasing the number of email addresses acquired by 15.8%. Additionally, it led to a drop in the number of members acquired by 37% since we were driving less traffic to the membership page and they were not as interested to learn more about the associated benefits.
After further analysis, we realized that we skipped several steps in the donor funnel by promoting the membership before the initial offer. The visitors need to be taken through a mental conversation that ultimately leads to member conversion but each step along the way is important. We must first address their reason for visiting before we sell them on any other offer.

Historically, we had offered a subscription to our weekly newsletter, This vs. That, which allowed subscribers to receive one experiment per week from the Research Library.
Since this was our main website, we hypothesized that maybe the more general offer of “Staying Informed” would work better for the visitors. We decided to set up a test that would allow us to evaluate these two different offers.
experiments.keyLearnings: We discovered that the “Stay Informed” offer led to an infinitely better number of emails captured (none versus some is by definition infinitely better). While the This vs. That newsletter is still one of our more popular offers, a more general contact form was a better fit given the motivation of the people visiting the website.

experiments.title: Which email sign up headline produces the highest conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: What email value proposition will produce the most email sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2015 - 08/28/2015
Every once in a while we get around to running tests on our own website to make sure NextAfter is practicing what we preach. So we set up two different email signup pages encouraging visitors to get the This vs. That weekly email which showcases one of our latest test results. The goal was to see which email sign up value proposition would drive the most conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test is another example of how clarity trumps persuasion. Clearly stating what a person is signing up for, getting a test each week, is a better description than talking about how a person will benefit. The surprising aspect to us was that even with the low traffic to the page the test validated.
The copy used to promote offers is the most important element in getting someone to say yes. Just throwing something up on the page and not testing it would have seriously affected the number of email sign-ups the page generated.

experiments.researchQuestion: Which method of linking resources in a digest email will lead to more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/22/2019 - 02/28/2019
In this experiment, we were continuing to test the format of our digest emails to optimize for more clicks. When setting up this email, we had 2 ideas of how we could link up the various articles, blogs, podcasts, etc. On the one hand, we could link up the description text so that the links are tied directly to the value copy. On the other hand, we could simply list out several links in a block below the description text.
experiments.keyLearnings: The list of links saw an 81% increase in clicks. It's possible that having the linked up text in the description paragraphs made the email harder to read, or obscured all the different resources. Putting them in a list could make it more skimmable and easier to digest.

experiments.title: How email design affects open rate in a newsletter email
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a designed email newsletter affect open rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2019 - 03/18/2019
Email appeal experiments had given us a predisposition to think that email with button, images, and minor design elements are ineffective. But we had really only discovered that to be true in the specific context of a donation appeal.
In this experiment, we wondered if basic design elements could actually lead to greater click-throughs. The control was a plain-text style email in the format we had tested into, and the treatment added section images and clickable html buttons.
But we also wanted to keep an eye on open-rates. We thought it was possible for these design elements to hurt deliverability and inbox placement, and hurt open rates as a result.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This result is a little bit baffling. We expected we might see a decrease in opens, or in the best case scenario, see no change at all. Instead, we saw an 8.1% increase in open rates at a 98% level of confidence.
We thought it was possible that the preview text appeared differently, but that proved to not be the case. Subject lines and senders were also exactly the same. Now, the 98% confidence means theres a 1 in 50 chance that this result may not hold in when tested again. The odds of that are very slim, but seeing that we have no real explanation for why this result occurred, we will test again to be sure.

experiments.title: How a two-step form on a pop-up offer affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using a two-step form instead of a “Learn More” link lead to more conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2019 - 03/05/2019
In this experiment, we had launched popups on key pages on our website in order to try and get a few more people to enroll in the online fundraising benchmark study. To enroll in the study, you needed to provide 3 key metrics – monthly web traffic, monthly donations, and average gift size.
The original pop-up had a brief description of the report, and then had a button link to the landing page. But we wondered if we could increase conversion by asking someone right away what their monthly web traffic was. Upon entering their web traffic, they’d advance to the landing page to finish provide their other 2 metrics and their contact info. (The landing page pre-filled their web traffic info so it didn’t have to be answered again)
experiments.keyLearnings: While the volume of people converting on this offer is small (this was expected), the treatment saw an infinite increase in conversions. Although it required more work to get to the landing page, having someone complete an easy first step made them more committed and likely to convert on the following page. Clicking "Learn More" is a much easier action, but it doesn't result in as much motivation and commitment to the final offer.

experiments.title: How a different value proposition affected enrollment in the benchmark report
experiments.researchQuestion: Will value-first messaging lead to greater conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2019 - 03/05/2019
In this experiment, we wondered if our copy wasn’t communicating enough about why the benchmark report is valuable. The control seemed to place a stronger emphasis on what is required to enroll in the report. So we created a treatment that was more focused on the value of a benchmark report to the marketer/fundraiser.
experiments.keyLearnings: Our gut instinct that we needed more value-heavy copy didn't pan out. We saw a 37% decrease in conversion.Reviewing the treatment in light of the result, it's possible that we muddied the waters about what the final product was. In the control, it's clear that we're gathering data for a new benchmark study and inviting people to be a part of it. In the treatment, the heavy emphasis on the value could imply that that they're going to get the report right away. But when you get down to the form, you learn that the study isn't ready for you to download right away.For the future, we'll want to test a treatment that makes two things abundantly clear: we're gathering data for a new study, and the report is going to be immensely valuable.

experiments.title: How the length of a story in an email affects webinar registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will spending less time on the story and more time on the content affect webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2019 - 02/20/2019
In this experiment, our original email was trying to communicate the importance of a/b testing by telling a personal story. This is typical of many emails that we write – we often look for a personal story to make help the recipient see that we’re humans (not just marketers) and use that connection to lead into a content offer of some type.
But we wondered if it might be more effective to tell this same story in a much shorter format, and spend more time talking about the specifics of what someone will learn in the webinar, rather than solely relying on the landing page to cover those details.
experiments.keyLearnings: Our treatment saw a 48.4% increase in webinar registrations. This is a very interesting result as it can make a significant impact on how we structure of emails related to free content offers in the future. It's often hard for us to validate an experiment like this based on our scale, let alone see an increase this large. So a 98% level of confidence gives us great reassurance that this strategy will be on to continue test and iterate on moving forward.

experiments.title: How more links in a digest style email affects clickthrough
experiments.researchQuestion: Will more featuring more links in a digest style email affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/09/2019 - 01/15/2019
In an effort to share more of our content, we had previously run this same experiment in order to see if we could craft a digest style email with more links, while maintaining our click-through rate. The first test saw no change in clicks whatsoever, but we wanted to run it again to determine if that was a fluke result, or if there was more going on than that test result showed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment led to a 30% decrease in clicks with a 99% level of confidence. This significant shift in results from our original experiment was a bit confusing as our initial experiment had no significant change in clicks. In fact, the original experiment had a slight increase in clicks over the control, even though it wasn't significant.
Upon reviewing the original experiment, there is one key difference between the formatting of the variants. The original experiment's control utilized section headers that made it feel more like a digest style email, even though the copy was written very personally and in a long-form style. This experiment's control did not use section headers, but was one long email - more like a friend or colleague would write.
My hypothesis is that this addition of headers into the control in the original experiment took away the human factor and nullified the intent of the experiment. This new experiment is a more accurate "humanized email" vs "digest email" experiment – and possibly the more reliable result.
A future test could be to run the humanized email with headers against the humanized email without headers to determine if that alone is the significant factor in affecting clicks.

experiments.title: How streamlined copy affects conversion on a benchmark survey page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will streamlining the landing page copy lead to greater enrollments in benchmark report?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/04/2019
In this experiment, we were trying to get more people to share their 3 key online fundraising metrics for us to create a new online fundraising benchmark report. Our original page had an in-depth description of the 3 key metrics, their role in the fundraising process, and a breakdown of how they each can affect revenue.
We wondered if we could streamline the copy by removing this section explaining the metrics in detail. The hypothesis was that it made the page feel more like a blog, and might be distracting from the final call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment saw a 110% increase in people enrolling in the benchmark study. This will lead to significant increases in enrollment when we do a much more direct marketing push later on.

It also may be an indication that there is such thing as too much information. Explaining the full details of why we chose these 3 key metrics could be causing people to forget about the purpose of the page, what brought them there, and ultimately cause them to disengage. The additional value of this explanation is out-weighed by the additional friction it creates.

experiments.title: How an additional, clarifying form field affects webinar registrations

rows.name: Do you work for a nonprofit organization? (Required)
Do you work for a nonprofit organization? (Not Required)

experiments.researchQuestion: Will asking registrants if they work for a nonprofit decrease webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2019 - 01/25/2019
In this experiment, we wanted to see if adding an additional field to understand whether or not someone worked for a nonprofit would make a significant impact on conversion. If we could add this field without hurting registrations, we’d be able to have cleaner data and do additional segmentation in our marketing to try to engage “for-profit” people on our email file differently.
So we set up a 3 variant experiment – the control had our normal form fields, variant 1 had an additional required field asking if someone worked for a nonprofit, and variant 2 had an un-required field asking the same question.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The result of this experiment was actually shocking. The best case scenario we hoped for was seeing no difference in conversion rate. Instead, we saw a 24% increase with the required field, and a 32% increase with the un-required field. Both increases reached 95% or higher statistical confidence. Variant 2 reached 99.9% confidence.
While I can't say I'm 100% confident as to why this increase occurred, my hypothesis is that it is very similar to this experiment with Jews for Jesus. Additional form fields often lead to an increase in friction - which results in lower conversion rates. But in this case, the additional question may have served as a qualifying question that gave registrants more confidence that the webinar is specifically geared for them. By sharing that they work for a nonprofit or for-profit, the implication is that the webinar will be more relevant and valuable.
The increase in perceived value outweighed the additional cost created by the form field, leading to greater conversion.

experiments.keyLearnings: When we focused the headline of the ad on the value to the visitor instead of simply addressing what they would see, we saw a 275% increase in the number of emails acquired. This is another example of ensuring every step of the acquisition funnel communicates the benefits to the visitor and not the work they will have to do or simply what the offer is.

experiments.title: How a more specific subject line affects opens, clicks, and conversions

rows.name: How are your year-end plans coming?
A new year-end fundraising webinar for you

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more specific subject line lead to greater email performance?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/16/2018 - 10/24/2018
In this email experiment, the original subject line posed a question that would be completely normal to receive from a colleague at this time of year…”How are your year-end plans coming?” It felt personal and genuine, but we wondered if it wasn’t doing enough to prime recipients for the upcoming call-to-action. So for the treatment, we created an subject like that maintained the personal nature (using the word “you”) and increased the specificity and clarity around what the content of the email was ultimately leading towards. For the treatment, we said “A new year-end fundraising webinar for you”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As we've seen in the past, even when the subject line doesn't affect the open rate, it can still have a dramatic effect on downstream metrics. In this case, the open rate remained nearly identical – 36.14% vs 36.16%. But the treatment version increased clicks to the webinar registration page by 42.7% at a 99% level of confidence.
The treatment also increased webinar registrations by 18.8% and was approaching statistical validity at an 89% level of confidence. This shows us 2 important learnings:

1 - The subject line has a powerful effect on how recipients view and respond to the entire email, even if it makes no difference in the open rate.
2 - A subject line that makes a clear tie to the ultimate call-to-action has a greater chance of getting a click and a conversion.

experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment saw a 242.5% increase in conversion rate. This tells us that it can be very effective to leverage significant findings in the imagery itself, rather that solely relying in the copy to communicate the substance of the offer.

Timeframe: 04/18/2018 - 05/15/2018
We had launched an exit-intent pop-up on the NIO Summit website offering a free sample session in order to get a taste of what the NIO Summit is like. The original offer directed traffic to a landing page with the video, and a call-to-action form to get access to all the videos from the 2017 summit.
We wondered if we could increase conversion by putting a form on the exit-intent pop-up that a visitor would have to fill out in order to see the free session. After completing the form, the visitor was redirected to the same landing page as the control where they could also get access to the 2017 videos.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment exit-intent pop-up increased email acquisition by 215%. We thought it could be possible that our traffic may not be motivated enough to give us their email address without seeing the quality of the content. But this shows the NIO Summit traffic has a high motivation for this type of content.

The treatment also saw an increase in the amount of people that signed up to get all of the 2017 videos, indicating that this small step of signing up to get the one video amplified their motivation to learn more about the summit.

experiments.title: How a reminder series affects online course completion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will getting reminders to stay on track with the course schedule increase course completion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/27/2018 - 05/07/2018
In this experiment, we launched a new online course on email fundraising. Along with the course, we built an optimal schedule in order to complete the course in a timely fashion. Course registrants had the opportunity to opt-in to get reminders outlining this optimal schedule from the course instructor.
While we didn’t launch a perfectly clean A/B test, we wanted to compare the difference in course completion between those that did not opt-in versus those that that did.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw a 388% increase in course completion from those that opted in to reminders. Now, this simply be a result of those that are more naturally motivated were more likely to opt-in anyway. But the drastic lift here indicates that this type of optimal schedule and reminders is effective at helping course takers make it to the end.

We'll develop this type of schedule for future courses, and we can do more testing to try and drive more registrants to opt-in to this reminder series.

rows.name: Jane, I have a special, spicy, and movie themed update for you

experiments.researchQuestion: Will specific preview text increase opens?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/27/2018 - 05/01/2018
In this experiment, we were sending a weekly update on new research and learnings. We typically let the preview text pull in the opening lines of the email since our emails are always personally written. We wondered if specifically crafting preview text to emphasize the value of the content in the email could increase opens and clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that this specifically crafted preview text actually decreased opens by 8.8%. Our hypothesis here is that preview text that sounds and looks like marketing is a dead giveaway that this is a marketing email. We've removed an aspect of the personal touch, and this is sets off a flag in the recipients mind that this email is inauthentic.

When looking at clicks, we saw no significant change. So even though we saw a slightly higher click-to-open rate from the more value heavy preview text, the net effect on clicks was a wash.

This email was a retargeted send to those who had yet to register for the upcoming webinar. We had previously tested into an email that simulated an email forward (but in a non-spammy way). It was personal, checked in to see if they had received the previous email, and included the previous email below.
We wondered if we could build on this approach. Instead of of simply saying “Did you get the email? Here’s the link to register,” we decided to apply a little empathy, communicate that it was no problem if they couldn’t attend the webinar, and specifically offer a free recording when they register – even if they can’t make the webinar live.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment saw a 52% increase in webinar registrations. This is major increase and will lead to many more people viewing what we consider to be one of our most valuable touch-points. This is further indication of the need to empathize with our constituents, rather than use organizational-centric logic.

experiments.title: How an additional call-to-action in a Facebook ad headline affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding an additional call-to-action in the Facebook ad headline increase webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2018 - 04/16/2018
Prior to this experiment, we had validated two related Facebook ad experiments. In one experiment, we learned that a headline focused on the content of the webinar is more effective than a direct call-to-action to reserve your spot. In another, we learned that a more tactics focused headline is more effective than a headline focused on broader fundraising growth.
For this experiment, we wondered if a combination of the two approaches could lead to greater webinar registrations. So for the control, we used the tactics focused headline. For the treatment, we added “Reserve Your Spot »” call to action at the end of the headline.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This additional call-to-action, paired with the tactics focused headline, increased webinar registrations by 164.9%. This additional clarity showed that there was an action to be taken, rather than simply reading a blog post or article.

Interestingly, there was no significant change in click-through-rate from the ad to the landing page. The control had a 2.3% click-through rate, and the treatment had a 2.5% click-through rate. This implies that by simply priming the user for what they could expect on the next page, we were able to drive more valuable traffic.

In a previous experiment with an on-site pop-up, we learned that the traffic on NextAfter.com was more inclined to download one of our free offers if we focused our message and call-to-action on learning specific tactics, rather than focusing on growth metrics.
We wondered if the same learning would hold up when using Facebook advertising to re-target our web traffic with a free webinar offer. The call-to-action headline of the control put the emphasis on the growth metric of “acquire more emails.” The treatment ad’s call-to-action headline put the emphasis on the “7 proven methods.”
We intentionally kept both messages in the call-to-action header, and just flipped their placement so that we could specifically isolate the motivation of the Facebook user.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The result of this experiment was a resounding "Yes." A tactics focused call-to-action headline will indeed increase webinar registrations. The treatment saw a 770% increase in conversion rate, compared to the control. 

This gives us further confidence moving forward that our marketing copy, at least for existing traffic to NextAfter.com, should put a stronger emphasis on the tactics rather than the growth.

0% increase in traffic
				× 19.7% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How a more tactical and specific headline affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more tactical and specific headline increase conversions?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/30/2018
In this experiment, we had recently updated our landing page template and wanted to launch new slide-out offers on our site. For the control, we used a a headline that was broadly focused on growing your email acquisition. For the treatment, we used a headline that was a lot more tactical and specific.
experiments.keyLearnings: By writing a headline that was more focused on the specific content and was more tactical in nature, we saw a 103.7% increase in emails acquired. This will help shape how we write headlines for offers in the future - by focusing on the specific and practical ways to make changes, rather than growth in general.

				gift).

experiments.title: How Imagery Affects Conversion in a Facebook Ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding additional clarity to the Facebook ad image increase course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/13/2017 - 10/18/2017
When running a Facebook ad campaign for a new online course, we wondered if we needed to add more clarity to the Facebook ad image. In order to more clearly depict the title and purpose of the course, Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we added the Facebook logo leading towards a dollar icon on the ad image.
This experiment was conducted with the Network for Good audience ad set.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new Facebook ad image led to a 68% decrease in course registration, despite appearing to ad more clarity. Since the control ad had previously been running, it’s possible that the additional social proof from likes and shares contributed to it’s better performance; although, likes and share don’t always make that significant of difference.
It could also be that the new imagery, although more accurate and clear, made the course look similar to other marketing courses out there that appear gimmicky or too good to be true.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 68.6% decrease in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: Will Defaulting to $50 monthly ask improve conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that preselecting the $50 gift amount in the gift array for the monthly donation page will achieve more conversions because by having it preselected there will be one less step for the user to do in the donation process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/29/2025
In the past, we that having this organization break out the episodic and monthly giving pages perform better by having a ‘donate’ and ‘donate monthly’ button in the navigation bar.
For this test, we wanted to see if we could improve conversion and increase average gift on the monthly donation page by preselecting $50 in the gift array.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that preselecting the $50 gift amount in the gift array for the monthly donation page did not result in more conversions. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 44.9% decrease in donations compared to the control group. While this difference is not statistically significant at a 93.7% confidence level, it suggests that further testing is necessary to determine the true impact of preselecting the gift amount.

When digging into the results, we noticed that the average gift amount is $35 for this page. Coming in at $50 could have added more friction to this test because the amount was too high. In our next test, we are going to see if preselecting $25 will increase conversion.

experiments.title: Will adding a heart to the monthly tab increase monthly transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a heart on the monthly tab for the sustainer donation page giving form will achieve a higher number of recurring transactions because the heart will the users eyes to the monthly tab and lead them to giving a recurring gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2025 - 02/18/2025
In the past we have tested adding different hearts to elements to catch the users eye and get them to take the action that we are wanting.
In this case, we wanted to see if we could add a heart on the monthly tab on the donation form would increase the number of monthly transactions.
We ran this test on the main sustainer donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the addition of a heart on the monthly tab for the sustainer donation page giving form resulted in a significant decrease in transactions for all traffic. The treatment group saw a 69.4% decrease in recurring transactions with a 92.4% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control group performed better in this case.

While the results are not statistically significant at the usual 95% confidence level, they are approaching validity, suggesting that further testing would be necessary to confirm the impact of the heart icon on user behavior.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of visual elements like hearts on donation forms, as they may not always lead to the desired outcome. It would be beneficial to conduct additional tests with different visual cues or design elements to see which ones are most effective in increasing recurring transactions. Additionally, it is crucial to analyze user feedback and behavior to understand the reasons behind the results and make informed decisions for future optimization efforts.

experiments.title: Is a graphic CTA better than a dear reader for non campaign season asks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing the graphic cta with a dear reader for the content pages cta will achieve more transactions because the dear reader is clearer to understand what the cta is and offers additional value proposition that can not be replaced by a graphic.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/24/2025 - 02/18/2025
For campaign seasons we’ve found that including a dear reader on the content pages significantly increases transactions and revenue. We wondered if we could use this same tactic during noncampaign seasons.
For this test, we replaced a content offer graphic with a dear reader to see if we could increase overall transactions and revenue. Below is what we found
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that replacing a graphic call-to-action (CTA) with a dear reader on content pages resulted in a 21.1% increase in Transactions for All Traffic, with an 88.3% level of statistical confidence. This indicates that the dear reader CTA was clearer and offered additional value proposition that resonated with users.

Moreover, there was a significant 319% increase in Transactions - One-Time, with a high level of confidence at 96%. This suggests that the dear reader CTA was particularly effective in driving one-time transactions.

Moving forward, it is important to note that while the results are promising, further testing is needed to validate the findings. The learnings from this experiment can be used in future experimentation by continuing to test the effectiveness of different CTAs, including dear reader messages, to optimize conversion rates and revenue. By iterating on these tests and analyzing the results, we can continue to refine our strategies and improve overall performance.

experiments.title: Will a guided reading experience on the donation page increase donations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the copy to have a guided reading experience for the main episodic donation page will achieve more transactions because the copy will be laid out in an easy to understand manner that puts the donor as the solution.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2025 - 01/29/2025
In the past, we have tested adding a guided reading experience to donation pages during a campaign.. We have found that it can be helpful to help guide readers down the page in an easy-to-understand way.
We wondered if we could find this same result during a non campaign season. For this test we adjusted the donation page copy to lay out the problem, solution, and how the individual donor can make a difference. We ran this test on the main episodic donation page. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the guided reading experience on the main episodic donation page did not lead to an increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment actually saw a decrease in transactions by 14.0% compared to the control. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 81.8%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

Therefore, these results should not be considered valid for future decision making. In the future, it may be beneficial to continue testing different approaches to optimize the donation page, keeping in mind the importance of clear and easy-to-understand messaging for donors. It is also advisable to ensure that experiments are run for a longer duration to gather more reliable and conclusive results.

experiments.title: Does adding a donor testimonial work on an evergreen donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonal for the goaa/soaa donation page during CYE will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because having the donor testimonal right next to the point of decision will reinforce the ‘need’ and the ‘why”. because  having the donor testimonal right next to the point of decision will reinforce the ‘need’ and the ‘why”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/05/2025
For CYE this year, we wanted to see if adding a right rail donor testimonial on the donation pages would make an impact on transactions. We wanted to test this on all three major donation pages for Focus, the main campaign episodic giving page, the main campaign recurring giving page, and the GOAA/SOAA broadcast pages that generate a ton of traffic each day.
For this test, we wanted to see how adding the testimonal on the the GOAA/SOAA donation pages would impact conversion. We thought this would be neat to see more of an ‘evergreen’ type of donation page even during a high urgency campaign
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor testimonial to the GOAA/SOAA donation page during CYE did not result in a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a 15.9% decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 82.8%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is adequate to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be helpful to test different types of donor testimonials or placement of the testimonials on the page to see if there are any variations that could lead to increased conversions. It is also important to take into consideration the timing of the experiment and the specific campaign or context in which it is being conducted to ensure that the results are relevant and applicable.

experiments.title: How adding a donor goal sticky bar to the donation page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar to the sohl donation page for the CYE campagin will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the sticky bar will reinforce the need as well as add exclusivity to the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2024 - 12/28/2024
For this test we wanted to see if adding a sticky bar that contains a donor goal would increase the appeal to give as well as convey the message that we’re wanting to send to the users. By adding a sticky bar on the family donation page we found that it will increase conversions. Below is what we found for the SOHL audience
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a sticky bar containing a donor goal to the sohl donation page for the CYE campaign resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, which means the results cannot be considered valid for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is at least 95% before drawing any conclusions or making decisions based on the results. It may be beneficial to conduct further tests or experiments to confirm the effectiveness of adding a sticky bar to the donation page. Additionally, it could be helpful to explore other strategies or elements that could potentially increase donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: Will adding a sticky bar to the top of a donation page reinforce the need for users to give?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding in a sticky bar that has specific donor goal language for the monthly family campaign giving page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the sticky bar will reinforce the need as well as add exclusivity to the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2024 - 12/28/2024
For this test we wanted to see if adding a sticky bar to the campaign donation page that contains donor goal language would increase the donor conversion rate on the page. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Adding a sticky bar with specific donor goal language to the monthly family campaign donation page resulted in a significant increase in Revenue for All Traffic by 397.1% with a 91.0% level of confidence.

2. There was also a 48% increase in Transactions - One-Time with a 96% level of confidence.

3. While the results were positive, the statistical confidence level of 91.0% for Revenue for All Traffic falls slightly below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for validity.

4. Further testing is recommended to confirm the effectiveness of the sticky bar in increasing donor conversion rates.

5. Future experimentation should focus on refining the donor goal language in the sticky bar, testing different designs or placements, and running the experiment for a longer duration to ensure reliable results.

6. It is important to continue testing and refining strategies to optimize donor conversion rates and maximize fundraising efforts for the nonprofit "Focus on the Family".

experiments.title: What message resonates more “give hope” or “deliver hope”
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the copy from “giving hope” to “deliver hope” for the homepage wiki header will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because while the language is slightly different the tangibility between deliver and give is significant.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/04/2024 - 11/21/2024
Each high-urgency campaign FOTF pushes a wiki header on their homepage. When the campaign first started we wanted to make sure that the language that we used in it was the most optimized that it could be going into our biggest testing season. For this we wanted to see if “give hope” or “deliver hope” resonates more with their audience. While the actual message itself is very similar, we were able to see a massive difference when it comes to making a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the copy from "giving hope" to "deliver hope" for the homepage wiki header resulted in a significant increase in Revenue for All Traffic, specifically a 134.6% increase. However, the statistical confidence level of 87.8% falls slightly below the typical threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.
In future experimentation, it would be important to continue testing different language variations to validate the initial positive outcome. Additionally, considering the notable increase in revenue seen with the change from "giving hope" to "deliver hope," it might be beneficial to explore further tests that focus on emphasizing the tangible impact and action-oriented language to drive donor conversion rates. Ultimately, this experiment highlights the importance of continuously testing and refining messaging to maximize effectiveness in engaging with the audience and achieving campaign goals.

experiments.title: How lowering the gift array options on a membership donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that decreasing the amounts on the gift array for people who are activating their membership will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the lower suggested gift amounts will make giving a gift feel more attainable to a larger amount of people.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/09/2024 - 08/07/2024
An organization has a donation page where people can make a donation of $25 or above and activate their membership. In an attempt to increase the donor conversion rate on this page, they wanted to test whether lowering the gift array amount options would lower the barrier to donating and in turn increase the amount of donors.
The control’s gift array was $50, $100, and $250.
The treatment’s gift array was $25, $50, and $100.
The experiment ran for one month and the control and treatment each received %50 of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment is that decreasing the gift array amounts did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate: Contrary to the hypothesis, the treatment group, which had lower gift options of $25, $50, and $100, experienced a 23.7% decrease in total transactions for all traffic compared to the control group, which had gift options of $50, $100, and $250. This decrease was at 83.8% which, while not valid, is directional.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to analyze the impact of the language used in the gift array on donor behavior. It is possible that the specific wording and formatting of the gift array may have influenced donor decision-making in ways that were not captured by this experiment.

Overall, it is essential to carefully design and execute experiments, take into account all relevant factors, and analyze results with a critical eye before making any decisions based on the findings.

experiments.title: Will aligning the value proposition from the donate button and the episodic giving page increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the value propostion from the donate button to the doantion page for main episodic donation page will achieve a higher number of transactions because the congruency between the button and the donation page will decrease friction and reinforce motivation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2024 - 11/01/2024
In this series of testing we have found that using Biblical Resources on the main donate button increases the number of clicks to the donation page.
We saw that aligning the value proposition with the main donation button on the monthly page increased transactions by 154%.
We wondered if we could duplicate those results for the episodic donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that aligning the value proposition from the donate button to the donation page for the main episodic donation page led to a 13.7% increase in transactions for all traffic. This indicates that having congruency between the button and the donation page can potentially decrease friction and reinforce motivation for users to make donations.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring the impact of aligning value propositions on donation pages to see if this trend holds true across different scenarios. Conducting additional tests with larger sample sizes and varied approaches to alignment could help validate these findings further and provide more conclusive evidence. It is important to note that while the results of this experiment are encouraging, they do not meet the standard threshold of 95% statistical confidence, so further testing would be necessary to confirm the effectiveness of this strategy.

experiments.title: How a cleaner weekly email design impacts the click rate of readers
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that redesigning Winston’s Weekly for subscribers will achieve a higher email click rate because there will be more information and resources available to them. because there will be more information and resources available to them.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/11/2024
We’ve been running Winston’s Weekly for a little more than a year now. The weekly email shares a case study from our experiment library and breaks it down to provide fundraisers with a deeper look at fundraising tests to inspire their own testing and optimization.
Looking at the design, it felt partially designed and partially plain text. We thought that using more design elements might create a cleaner, more readable way to present the information.
Pulling some inspiration from the newsletter Case Studied, we broke each header section into its own blocks in the design. We added a resources section at the bottom that links to the week’s experiment and other relevant resources. We also added a section about our services with a button to learn more.
Unlike the control version, this treatment version provided more opportunities and more reason to click out of the email to our website.
The treatment resulted in an email that was almost twice the length of our typical email but presented the content in a much cleaner way.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The redesigned version of Winston's Weekly did not perform as well as the original version, with a 27.5% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic. The statistical level of confidence for this experiment was 92.4%, meaning the results are approaching validity but cannot be considered completely reliable. The additional information and resources added to the treatment version did not lead to a higher click rate as hypothesized.

I'm interested in doing another email redesign in the control format with just the addition of the resources section and the section about NextAfter's services to see how that would impact clicks.

It could also be interesting to do another email redesign in a plain text format, but removing the gray background and using clearer headings like in this treatment to see if that gets more people to stick around till the end and click.

Future Experimentation:

1. Further testing is necessary to determine the impact of the redesigned layout on subscriber engagement.

2. Consider conducting additional A/B tests with variations in design elements to identify which specific changes lead to improved performance.

3. Use the learnings from this experiment to inform future iterations of Winston's Weekly, taking into account subscriber preferences and engagement metrics to guide design decisions.

experiments.title: How adding visual clarity to a marriage video series landing page image impacted email signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more visual clarity to the imagery on a video series landing page will achieve an increase in email acquisition because visitors will understand what’s being offered and why they should want it.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/15/2024 - 05/16/2024
This organization uses free video series content offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. Their video series landing pages include a “screenshot” image from one of the series videos to showcase the guest speaker for the series.
We have a theory that some of the visitors coming to the landing page may not be familiar with what a video series is or how they would access it, so we decided to test whether we could use the imagery on the page to add some visual clarity.
We designed this as a 3-way test with the following variants:
Control – No change, uses the plain image of the speaker
“Devices” Treatment – Shows the video series on a phone and laptop to show how it can be watched
“Devices & Value Flag” Treatment – Shows the video series on a phone and laptop, and also adds a value impact statement above it
Our hypothesis was that the treatment images would drive higher email acquisition rates because visitors would understand what the video series offer is and why they should sign up for it.
We split the campaign traffic using Facebook’s AB testing feature and conducted the test over 1 month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which included adding more visual clarity to the imagery on the video series landing page, resulted in a 2.3% decrease in Email Acquisition for All Traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 93.9%. This indicates that the control, which featured the plain image of the speaker, performed better.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider that while the treatment did not perform as well in this instance, there may still be value in exploring ways to improve visual clarity on landing pages. Further testing with different variations of imagery or messaging could help to identify what resonates best with the target audience. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test other elements of the landing page, such as the copy or call-to-action, to optimize for email acquisition.

Overall, the learnings from this experiment should be used to inform future testing strategies and to continue to refine the approach to optimizing video series landing pages for email acquisition.

experiments.title: How Does adding Consistent Value Language on the CTA Button Affect Conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the call-to-action value claims for the main navigation donate buttons will achieve a higher conversion rate because there is less friction for the donor. They will no longer have to choose which initiative they would want to support.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/03/2024 - 07/30/2024
Our first test in this series was seeing if aligning the CTA on the duel donation buttons would make an impact. You can read that test writeup here: https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/8277.
For this test, we wanted to see if aligning the value proposition on the buttons could increase transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted on the FOTF homepage showed that aligning the call-to-action value claims for the main navigation donate buttons resulted in a 61.3% increase in Revenue for All Traffic. While this result is statistically significant, the confidence level of 84.0% falls slightly below the threshold of 95% for validity.
The key learning from this experiment is that aligning the call-to-action value claims can potentially reduce friction for donors and lead to an increase in conversions and revenue. With the directional lift in transactions and revenue by using Biblical resources this variant will become the control for the next iteration of testing.

experiments.title: Will adding urgency to an in-line banner increase donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown timer on the Wikipedia banner will increase donor conversion because the countdown to the deadline would increase urgency.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/02/2024
As the campaign deadline draws near, we believe adding a countdown to the Wikipedia banner may insight more donor participation by visually showing time is running out and the time to act — time to save America’s families and make an impact — was coming to a close. Would adding a countdown timer increase donor perception that they have a deadline to make a donation and make a difference?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a countdown timer to the Wikipedia banner may increase donor conversion by creating a sense of urgency. The treatment group saw a 23.9% increase in donations for all traffic, indicating that the countdown timer potentially motivated more donors to take action before the deadline. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 81.0%, falling short of the typically accepted threshold of 95%.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of introducing a countdown timer or similar elements that create a sense of urgency. While this experiment provided promising results, the validity is not strong enough to definitively conclude that the countdown timer was the sole reason for the increase in donations. Therefore, further testing with a larger sample size or different variations of the countdown timer may be necessary to draw more reliable conclusions. Additionally, it would be beneficial to explore other strategies to increase donor conversion and engagement to complement the potential effectiveness of a countdown timer.

experiments.title: How adding an impact callout above the gift array impacted average gift and donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an impact callout to the gift array for visitors on the confirmation donation pages will achieve an increase in average gift because the callout will incentivizing and highlighting a larger gift amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2024 - 05/23/2024
Focus on the Family is a large Christian ministry organization that creates and shares free resources like video series to encourage families. After someone signs up for a free video series, they’re brought to a confirmation page that asks if they will make a gift to share the series with others. These confirmation pages also promote a physical book premium offer as a thank-you for donating.
In reviewing these confirmation pages, we realized that the body copy on the page was asking for a specific gift amount — usually $30 or $50 — so that the reader can get the premium book offer. However, the gift array did not have this specific amount highlighted in any way.
We decided to run an experiment to test whether adding a callout that highlights a desired gift amount and reminds the reader of their gift’s impact would increase average gift amounts and overall donor conversion.
Our theory was that the impact callout would increase average gift by highlighting a higher default gift amount in the array. We also theorized that the impact statement would increase donor conversion by reminding the reader of the impact their gift would have on other families looking for resources like the video series they just signed up for.
We built this test in VWO and ran it as a strict A/B test across four video series confirmation pages. The Control did not have any changes to its design. The Treatment version highlighted the second value in the gift array and also added the impact callout.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an impact callout to the gift array on confirmation donation pages could potentially increase the average gift amount and revenue. The Control had an average gift of $38.26, while the Treatment had an average gift of $41.70. This increase in average gift translates to a 9.0% lift, but it only achieved a 90.0% level of confidence (LOC).

However, while the average gift saw a positive trend, the impact callout Treatment led to significant decreases in donor conversion. We saw that the Treatment led to a -17.7% decrease in overall donor conversion with a 98.6% LOC. This trend was primarily driven by new visitors on mobile devices; returning visitors and desktop users did not see any changes in donor conversion, but they made up a minority of the test traffic.

Based on these results, we can't consider the impact callout as a validated strategy for increasing average gift. But the 90.0% LOC on lifting the average gift is promising and can be tested into further. It would be worth testing the impact callout separately from the highlighted amount in the gift array to see which element of the Treatment design led to the increase in average gift and decrease in donor conversion.

experiments.title: Homepage “Regular” Wiki vs Autonomy Wiki v2
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a decision point to make an impact for people who land on the homepage during a HUC will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the donor has complete autonomy to click “yes” and participate or “no” and close out of the ask because the donor has complete autonomy to click “yes” and participate or “no” and close out of the ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2024 - 03/21/2024
For our first test in this series we wondered if adding a question and a simple decision to make an impact increase donor conversion on the homepage campaign wiki banner? By adding a level of autonomy to the donor experience and giving them “control” to say “Yes, I’d like to make an impact” or “No, I’m not interested today,” would we invite more participation in the campaign and insight additional donor conversions over our “regular” campaign messaging with a gift array? You can see the results of the first test here.
For this test, the second in the series, we wanted to see if we created the autonomy treatment that’s more closely aligned with the control, will more donors say “yes” to help give families the resources they need?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. The treatment, which involved a decision point for donors to make an impact, resulted in a 64.6% increase in Revenue for All Traffic.
2. The statistical level of confidence for this increase was 84.5%, which is below the desired threshold of 95% for validity.
3. While the results suggest that the treatment was more effective in driving donor conversions, the lack of statistical significance means that these results should not be used for future decision making

experiments.title: How using mystery in the subject line affects email open rate on a cohort invite
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating mystery around the learning opportunity for our general subscribers will achieve more email opens than explicitly saying what the opportunity is.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2022 - 03/02/2022
It had been a while since we’d run a test using mystery vs. clarity in an email subject line. In this test, we wanted to see whether being clear that the email was an invitation to the next certification cohort or creating a mystery around a “new learning opportunity” would be more effective.
The control subject line read “An email fundraising cohort for you” and the treatment read “A new learning opportunity for you.”
Both emails were exactly the same otherwise.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not validate. There was hardly a change between the control and treatment results. Requires more testing.

experiments.title: How a stronger value proposition in the website footer affects email acquisition

rows.name: Value Prop Angle – Tested and Proven Strateiges

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and specificity to the website footer opt-in value proposition for website visitors will achieve greater email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/09/2021 - 02/10/2022
In this experiment, we wondered if users were really understanding the value that they would get from signing up for NextAfter emails. We hypothesized that a clearer and more specific value proposition would lead to more people signing up.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The clearer and more specific value proposition in the footer led to a 33% increase in email signups over a 90 day period - reaching a 94% level of confidence. And it appeared to have the most significant impact on new visitors who would be learning about NextAfter for the first time.
This is a great experiment to further confirm the need for clear articulation of a value proposition - especially when every other company, business, and organization has a "newsletter" as well. There is a lot of noise out there about best practices and training for nonprofit marketers and fundraiser. We must always articulate why our offerings are both different and better.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 28.5% increase in footer subscribe with an 88% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 42.3% increase in footer subscribe with a 94% level of confidence.45-54 visitors had a 194.4% increase in footer subscribe with a 98% level of confidence.

experiments.title: Will offering a free membership trial leads to more membership activations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a free 1-month trial for landing page visitors will achieve more membership activations.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/01/2021
As we reconfigured what membership looked like, we were trying to figure out a way to get more people to activate a membership to the NextAfter Institute. So, we thought if we offered folks a free 1-month trial, it would allow them the chance to learn what membership is about and be able to access our resources right away. We ran this experiment on our membership landing page with a 50/50 split in traffic between signing up for the typical $49/month membership vs. the 1-month free trial.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Something important to note is that metric tracking between all systems in this experiment was messy. We did our best to isolate variables, but they are not perfect.

Although the experiment did not validate, results point in the direction that the free trial is most effective in achieving more membership activations. However, something worth considering is if those folks will be retained as well as those who pay for the membership right away—more testing and analysis needs to be done to figure that out.

experiments.title: How a different value proposition affects email acquisition on a homepage ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a value proposition that conveys more urgency and focuses on the revenue at stake for homepage visitors will achieve more ebook downloads.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2020 - 12/30/2020
