In this experiment, we were promoting a new ebook focused on fundraising ideas that can help increase revenue during the last week of the year. We had two main ideas as to how to message the advertisement.
In version A, we used the homepage ad itself to illustrate how fundraisers can use a similar homepage ad to promote their year-end campaign.
In version B, the messaging emphasis focused on the urgency around the biggest giving week of the year being right around the corner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We didn't quite hit our desired 95% level of confidence, but the result was a strong indicator that it was more relevant to emphasize the urgency around the last week of the year – leading to a 50% increase in conversions. As noted below, it was especially effective for website visitors that already had some affinity towards us and our content.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning visitors had a 77.7% increase in all form submissions with an 89% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 213.3% increase in all form submissions with a 96% level of confidence.
 	Paid Social visitors had a 66.7% increase in all form submissions with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How more succinct copy affects total revenue on a homepage takeover
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing more succinct value proposition copy for homepage takeover visitors will achieve more sales and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2020 - 12/31/2020
During the last week of the year, we ran a homepage takeover to promote our last week of the year sale: 50% off any course, membership, or NIO Summit ticket.
After creating the control, we wondered if shortening the copy to be more succinct (without removing any of the core reasons to purchase) could actually lead to more sales and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although on the surface it appears that the treatment saw a big lift in revenue, the number of transactions are the same and only a single transaction made the difference in revenue. Pairing that with a small sample size, we can't really say that shorter copy was better performing with any confidence.

experiments.title: How increased urgency using a countdown clock affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown clock near the call-to-action for landing page visitors will achieve more ebook downloads.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2020 - 01/06/2021
In a previous experiment, we used a sticky bar to try and create a heightened sense of urgency around our ebook outlining strategies to increase giving during the last week of the year. But the sticky bar made no significant difference in conversions.
As a next step, we wondered if using a countdown clock lower on the page ticking down to the last week of the year would be a better means of conveying urgency and lead to more downloads.
experiments.keyLearnings: The countdown clock led to a 10.6% increase in conversion, although it didn't quite reach our desired level of confidence. However, it's a strong indicator that a countdown clock is an effective motivator to increase downloads in this context. We'll continue to use countdown clock on acquisition pages when there is a relevant deadline - in particularly around year-end and the last week of the year.

experiments.title: How increased urgency using a countdown clock affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown clock near the call-to-action for landing page visitors will achieve more ebook downloads.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2020 - 01/06/2021
In a previous experiment, we used a sticky bar to try and create a heightened sense of urgency around our ebook outlining strategies to increase giving during the last week of the year. But the sticky bar made no significant difference in conversions.
As a next step, we wondered if using a countdown clock lower on the page ticking down to the last week of the year would be a better means of conveying urgency and lead to more downloads.
experiments.keyLearnings: The countdown clock led to a 10.6% increase in conversion, although it didn't quite reach our desired level of confidence. However, it's a strong indicator that a countdown clock is an effective motivator to increase downloads in this context. We'll continue to use countdown clock on acquisition pages when there is a relevant deadline - in particularly around year-end and the last week of the year.

experiments.title: How paragraph formatting in an email affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will utilizing shorter paragraphs to increase readability affect clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/27/2020 - 11/03/2020
In this experiment, the initial email copy draft utilized long-form paragraphs. And many copy drafts arrive in this type of format before they get put on a landing page or in an email. I normally edit them to have more breaks and spacing in an effort to improve readability. But I wanted to see if editing the formatting to utilize shorter paragraphs actually made a difference.
My hypothesis was that this type of formatting would improve the clarity and understanding of the offer – and ultimately lead to more clicks and conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Breaking the copy up into smaller paragraphs led to a 19.7% increase in clicks at a 94.4% level of confidence. This is high enough for me to be confident in making these types of copy edits for emails moving forward.

The operating hypothesis here was that smaller chunks of copy can actually make it easier to read – which enhances the clarity of what’s being communicated in the email. And if it’s clearer what we’re asking people to do, we’ll have more click throughs – and ideally more conversions – as a result.

More testing will be needed to verify the downstream impact on conversion, as well as how this affects clarity on a landing page.

experiments.title: How adding additional clarity through the design affects registrations

rows.name: Event Details Box with Added Clarity

experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding additional clarity to the webinar details lead to more registrations?
experiments.summary:
Ended On:
Prior to this experiment, we had tested into using a time/date bar on the webinar registration page. It hadn’t impacted conversion significantly, but led to less inquires and confusion about the actual start time of the webinar. In this experiment, we wondered if we could use design to bring additional clarity to not just the time and date of the webinar, but to some of the additional perks of registering including live Q&A time and access to a recording afterwards.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new design led to a 6.4% increase in registrations, although it only reached a 90.9% level of confidence. As the overall conversion rate of this page is very high, it’s increasingly difficult to valid experiments on this page. That said, reaching a 90% level of confidence on a result that’s indicating positive growth gives me the confidence to roll out Version B as the winner.
Even if it makes no difference in conversion, there is now additional clarity on the page that all registrants will get a recording, which is one of the most common questions that registrants ask after the fact.

experiments.title: How adding a “Get a Recording” option affects webinar registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a “Get a Recording” option to the registration page lead to greater signups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/04/2020 - 02/11/2020
We have many folks who sign up for our webinars and then immediately let us know they can’t actually make it to the live event. We always send out a recording, so there’s not problem. But we wondered if giving them an option on the page to get the recording could increase signups. Our hypothesis was that there could be many people choosing to not register because they can’t make the time work – and there’s no indication on the page that there will be a recording available.
The treatment placed a “Get the Recording” button in the time and date bar. The thought was that this is the key point in which someone would be deciding if they can make it or not. The button opened a lightbox with a dedicated form to sign up for the recording.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding the recording option led to an 8.6% decrease in registrations. However, it did not reach a desired level of confidence of 95%. We also looked at webinar attendee rate and playback based on which treatment they signed up from, and the rates are nearly identical. So with the potential for the recording button to decrease registrations – and making no impact on attendance or playback rate – we will not offer the recording option in this format.
It’s possible that the option to get a recording was placed too far away from the form itself. A future test idea would be to make the recording an option within the registration form as that could be the key point in which someone is deciding whether or not they can make the live event.

experiments.title: How a grid layout affects clickthrough on a membership benefits page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a grid layout lead to more clicks into the checkout process?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2019 - 01/26/2020
In this experiment, we wondered if a grid style layout would be effective at showing visitors the various benefits of our 3 membership tiers, and thus lead to more clickthroughs to the checkout process.
The control used a bulleted list to describe the benefits of each tier. The treatment used a large grid with Xs and checkmarks to visually show what was included with each tier.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we didn’t reach a 95% level of confidence, the experiment had been running for a full month and has been leaning towards validity. The consequences of being wrong in this case a fairly low. But we will conduct follow-up testing to see if we can confidently improve clicks and conversions in the membership sign-up process.
The results indicate that this more visual approach is more effective at demonstrating the member benefits and showing the value of membership. It’s possible that more info is need on the page to clarify what some of the benefits are. And we also need to ensure in future testing that we can track all the way through to a purchase.
It also looks like a new element was added into the control variant during the test. And it’s likely that this skewed the final result.

experiments.title: How more personal phrasing in a reply email impacts opens

rows.name: Can You Please Answer One Question For Me?
Can You Please Let Me Know If You Want to Come?

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct subject line increase opens and responses?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/05/2019 - 11/06/2019
For an upcoming full-day certification workshop, we sent an email asking people to reply with a ‘yes’ or ‘no’ on whether or not they are interested in attending as a way to a) get commitment but also b) get a sense of numbers to plan the event properly. We wanted to see if being more direct in the subject line by telling them they will be asked to answer a question or being a bit more personal and casual subject line that isn’t as direct about a question would increase opens.
experiments.keyLearnings: While not statistically valid, the more personal, casual approach and not letting them know about the question seems to be a better approach but more testing is needed.

experiments.title: How more personal phrasing in a reply email impacts opens

rows.name: Can You Please Answer One Question For Me?
Can You Please Let Me Know If You Want to Come?

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct subject line increase opens and responses?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/05/2019 - 11/06/2019
For an upcoming full-day certification workshop, we sent an email asking people to reply with a ‘yes’ or ‘no’ on whether or not they are interested in attending as a way to a) get commitment but also b) get a sense of numbers to plan the event properly. We wanted to see if being more direct in the subject line by telling them they will be asked to answer a question or being a bit more personal and casual subject line that isn’t as direct about a question would increase opens.
experiments.keyLearnings: While not statistically valid, the more personal, casual approach and not letting them know about the question seems to be a better approach but more testing is needed.

experiments.title: How an additional design element affected registrations from a webinar email

rows.name: Plain Text with Webinar Image and Button

experiments.researchQuestion: Will using an additional design element at the bottom of the email lead to greater webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2019 - 06/14/2019
The majority of our emails are hyper-personal and text only. But recently, we’ve seen digest-style emails perform better using some basic design elements including simple images and HTML buttons. We believe the reason for this is that our emails can be very heavy. And if you’re not reading every line, a design element can quickly show what the offer is and provide a clear call-to-action.
In this case, we wondered if a similar approach could help improve registration rates in our webinar invitation emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment was just shy of reaching the desired 95% confidence level. The additional design element led to 20.7% more webinar registrations than the control. This seems to validate the hypothesis that these design element help give people a better understanding of the offer and call to action by adding visual clarity around the title, date, time, and content.
However, it is possible that the novelty of this design itself is leading to an increase. We will need to test again to make sure this increase is an ongoing trend and not a one-off result.

experiments.title: How email design affects click-through in a digest or newsletter email
experiments.researchQuestion: Will additional design elements in a digest email lead to greater click-through?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2019 - 03/18/2019
Email appeal experiments had given us a predisposition to think that email with button, images, and minor design elements are ineffective. But we had really only discovered that to be true in the specific context of a donation appeal.
In this experiment, we wondered if basic design elements could actually lead to greater click-throughs. The control was a plain-text style email in the format we had tested into, and the treatment added section images and clickable html buttons.
experiments.keyLearnings: While this didn’t quite reach our desired level of confidence of 95%, it was very close to validating a 32% increase in clicks. With this type of a result, we’re inclined to test it again to get greater confidence in the learning. There’s the possibility of a novelty effect – it improved clicks simply because it is different, and not necessarily because it is better long term.

experiments.title: How a Facebook ad image can affect conversion for a free online course offer [Housefile]
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an ad image that is more closely aligned with the title of the course increase clarity and conversion rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/14/2017 - 10/23/2017
Prior to launching this experiment, we had leaned that it was more effective to use bullet points to clearly convey key objectives of the course to our house file audience.
We hypothesized that we could further increase clarity by visually reinforcing the title of the course “Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors.” So we took our original ad image and tweaked to show the Facebook logo pointing towards a dollar icon.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although this experiment did not reach full validity, it strongly pointed towards the control as being more effective at converting course registrants. The test was turned off due to the significant increase in cost-per-registration that the treatment ad was creating.
It’s possible that the additions to the original image did not communicate the intended message as clearly as we had thought it might. It’s also possible that our original ad already garnered more attention in the newsfeed as a result of likes and shares from the time it had been running before this experiment.

experiments.title: How Bullet Points in a Facebook Ad Affect the Clarity of the Value Proposition [Blackbaud]
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of bullet points in a Facebook ad make the value of the course clearer, and lead to more course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 10/23/2017
When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below are from the ad set targeting Blackbaud followers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not reach full validity as it was turned off due to increase cost-per-registration. The result was leaning towards the bulleted copy as the winner, which is the only result in this series of experiments that runs counter to our hypothesis that bullets keep a potential new subscriber from understanding the full weight of the value proposition.
Although, since this experiment did not reach full validity, I’m more inclined to trust the results of the valid tests in this series.

experiments.title: How Bullet Points in a Facebook Ad Affect the Clarity of the Value Proposition [Web Traffic]
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of bullet points in a Facebook ad make the value of the course clearer, and lead to more course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 10/23/2017
When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below are from the ad set targeting our website traffic, excluding the house file.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not reach validity. It was pointing towards the control as being the winner, further validating the hypothesis that bulleted does not help a potential new subscriber to understand the full value proposition. Whereas, with a contact on our house file, they have a pre-established trust and are simply looking to see if this offer is relevant to them.
This experiment was ended before full validation due to the escalating cost-per-registration.

experiments.title: How a video ad affects the cost of traffic acquisition through Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a video ad drive more traffic to the website?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/24/2017 - 03/07/2017
NextAfter runs a series of Facebook ads that promotes their research to a house file look-alike audience. The intent is to get people to self identify that they are interested in nonprofit content, before they would be targeted with an acquisition ad.
We thought that using a video advertisement could capture the attention of more people and generate more clicks. So we developed 3 ads.
The control was an illustration like what we have used in the past. Treatment 1 was used a still image of the experiment break-down video. And Treatment 2 was a 15 second video teasing the experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The still image of the video started to approach validity with a 36.3% increase in clicks, but the video itself made virtually no difference in click rate what-so-ever.
The most significant metric in this test to us was the Cost-Per-Click (CPC). Regardless of what the click rate is, we want to get the most traffic for the lowest spend possible.
Oddly enough, treatment 1 had a 40% higher CPC than the control even though it had a higher click rate. Treatment 2, the video ad, had a 65% higher CPC than the control even though the click rate was essentially the same.
This test shows us that it is more cost effective for us to use the illustrated ads than it is to use this type of video to drive new traffic to our website.

experiments.title: How removing details in an email affects traffic to the landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the date and time from the email drive more traffic to the registration page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2017 - 02/15/2017
When promoting a webinar, we typically try to include the date and time in the emails that we send in order to provide as much clarity as possible to our recipients. But we wondered if putting the date and time in the email was actually disqualifying some people right away because they already knew they would be busy. We thought these elements were creating unnecessary friction at this stage in the registration process.
Our hypothesis was that by removing the date and time from the email, we could drive more click-throughs to the registration page. And once someone was on the landing page, we displayed an exit-intent pop-up offering to send a recording even if they couldn’t make the time work with their schedule.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment saw a lift of 15.3% in click-through rate. Unfortunately, our experiment did not quite reach our desired level of confidence of 95%. It came just short at 92.4%.
After analyzing conversion rates, we saw that there was no statistically significant difference between the control and treatment when it came to how many people actually registered.

experiments.title: How a different value proposition affects the conversion rate on an email acquisition page

rows.name: Learn to Answer Your Donor's Number One Question

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a value proposition that focuses on the donor affect the conversion rate of the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/23/2016 - 09/06/2016
With the release of the Why Should I Give to You? study of nonprofit value proposition, we decided we needed to drink our own medicine and put the value proposition of the study to the test.
The control version of the landing page to download the study focused on communicating to the visitor that this research study will help them craft an effective value proposition for their organization.
We hypothesized that the control assumed that visitors to the landing already knew the value of crafting an effective value proposition. So a treatment was created that focused on a more universal goal for the type of traffic visiting the page: “Answer your donor’s number one question: Why should I give to you?”
We split the traffic 50/50, and put them to the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment saw a decrease in conversions by 10.5%.
This shows us the the type of traffic being driven to this landing page is more motivated to download the eBook when the copy focused on helping them construct an effective value proposition.
Follow up tests from this will focus on clarifying and reinforcing the value proposition of the control.

experiments.title: How the use of video in a Facebook Ad affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which Facebook Ad variation will get more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/05/2016 - 08/12/2016
At NextAfter, we’re trying to distribute our research blog to the wider nonprofit audience in an effort to educate the marketplace about the effect that optimization can have on fundraising.
As part of this effort, we were running a series of Facebook Ads to our housefile lookalike audience. The initial click rates were lower than desired, so we wanted to test a new treatment.
Our hypothesis was that using a video advertisement could generate a higher click rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The video Facebook Ad received a 71.5% lift in clicks. The reasons for this increase could be:
The movement and action of the video was more effective at catching the eye of Facebook users scrolling through their newsfeed, causing them to pay more attention to the content of the ad. Or…
The video demonstrated and reinforced the value proposition of the blog in a way that helped increase clarity and appeal.
We will continue to test video in Facebook Ads to try and determine exactly what affect it has on Facebook Ad audiences.

experiments.title: How call to action and value proposition affect email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will changing the headline and intro copy strengthen the value proposition and increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2016 - 06/16/2016
We were hosting a live broadcast to unpack the first article of the Fundraiser’s Creed: People Give to People. We had set up a landing page to allow people to register for the broadcast. Upon peer review, we thought that a few small tweaks could increase the force of the value proposition on the page.
The first hypothesis had to do with the text above the headline. On the control, this copy said “Upcoming Live Internet Broadcast”. This was correct, but it gave the user no motivation or orientation in regards to what we wanted them to do. For the treatment, we changed this copy to “Register now for the live broadcast”.
The second hypothesis centered around the first paragraph. It was intended to address the reader and frame the problem, but we hypothesized that it might bury the actual value proposition for the broadcast, without adding much value. In the treatment, this paragraph was removed.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 24% lift in email acquisition. This shows the importance that each piece of copy has — copy can either orient and direct the visitor, or distract them and weaken the value proposition.

experiments.title: How adding a brand name in the sender line affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a brand name to the sender line increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2016 - 03/07/2016
NextAfter sent an email promoting their new eBook “Turning Likes Into Donors” to a rented email list of nonprofit development professionals. The email was sent from Tim Kachuriak, and meant to look like a personal email. However, we wondered — if one of the recipients of the email wasn’t familiar with Tim, would they be likely to open and click the email? We developed a test that added “NextAfter” to the sender line to give the recipient better clarity in regards to the sender and launched an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings: The added brand name reduced clickthrough rate by 20.7%. This shows that the presence of a brand name actually decreases the likelihood that the user will engage. This may be because the brand is as unknown as the name. This experiment also may suggest that people are more willing to engage with a name they don’t know, but not if it’s evident that they are being marketed to.

experiments.title: How a new value proposition focus in a sales email affects mystery donor audit applications

rows.name: What are your 2019 online fundraising plans?
Here’s a great way to increase giving for 2019 and beyond

experiments.researchQuestion: Will focusing the value proposition on the lack of time to plan and research lead to greater applications?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2018 - 12/20/2018
In this experiment, we wondered if a different value proposition approach to a mystery donor audit sales email would lead to greater applications. The control we had tested into already focused on how expert opinions are really just that : opinions. If your fundraising isn’t based on research, data, and testing, then you’re just relying on intuition.
The treatment focused on the lack of time to the necessary research to create a plan for 2019. We could step in with this mystery donor audit and do the leg work for you to help you create a strategy for your next year of fundraising. Both the subject line and body copy of the email were included in the treatment variables.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version saw a 70% decrease in clicks through to the landing page. While we didn’t reach full statistical confidence, we were approaching confidence at 89.5%. While we didn’t have enough applications to validate off of conversions (3 for the control and 1 for the treatment), this decrease in clicks is a strong indicator that focusing on time constraints is not the right value proposition focus.

experiments.title: How a different Facebook ad image affects webinar registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will showing the format of the webinar lead to an increase in webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2018 - 10/30/2018
In this experiment, we wondered if showing the format of the webinar and putting the production quality on display in the imagery would lead to more webinar registrations. The control used a typical webinar ad design – abstract representation of the topic, with å clear focus on the title and content.
experiments.keyLearnings: While this didn’t quite reach 95% validity, this treatment decreased registrations by 45% across all targeted Facebook audiences. It’s possible that what we thought was an added value (seeing the production quality) was actually a distraction from the actual topic, and the image made it more difficult to quickly see what the content was about as someone scrolled through the Facebook newsfeed.

experiments.title: How the length of copy in an email vs the landing page affects sales lead conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will shifting the narrative from the landing page to the email lead to greater sales leads?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2018
In this experiment, our goal was to generate sales qualified leads to set up a call related to new mystery donor audit. The control had a brief email explaining the topic, and landing page that went into full detail.
We have seen in the past (particularly with fundraising emails) that emails are often the best place to spend more time explaining a narrative. So for our treatment, we wondered if we could increase conversions by shifting a large portion of the copy from the landing page to the email. The copy is almost identical between the two versions, with a few minor adjustments in the treatment to make the email and landing page flow together naturally.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment email and landing page saw a 215% increase in conversion rate at a 93% level of confidence. While we ideally aim for a 95% level of confidence, the impact on sales leads seems apparent and strong enough for us to move forward with treatment decisively.
This further confirms a hypothesis that highly personal emails are more effective at explaining a narrative and story, and that the common “best practice” of writing short email copy to boost clicks is not a reliable way to increase the ultimate goal of conversion.

experiments.title: How a question in a banner ad affects clickthrough
experiments.researchQuestion: Will asking a question in a banner ad increase clicks to the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/12/2018
In this experiment, we were advertising applications for the Nonprofit Innovation & Optimization Awards using a sticky bar on the NIO Summit site. The control used pretty straightforward copy saying you could be recognized for innovative fundraising. The treatment asked a question that we thought might be disarming and more inviting.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment didn’t reach a level of confidence of 95% like we would have liked, but it starts to indicated that the control was the better performer. This could be a result of a loss of clarity in the treatment. By asking a question, the specifics of the awards got moved to smaller text that may have gone unnoticed – leading to less clicks.

experiments.title: How removing a call-to-action link and asking for a reply affects registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will asking for a reply lead to greater webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/17/2018
In this experiment, we wondered if we could further optimize our resend email that we use in promoting our monthly webinars. For the control, we used the template we had previously tested into. For the treatment, we wondered if an even more personal call-to-action would increase registrations. So rather than insert a CTA link like a marketing email would, we simply asked if they wanted to get a recording of the webinar – and waited for a reply.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we didn’t reach our desired 95% level of confidence, this experiment certainly was approaching validity with a 24.8% increase in webinar registrations for the treatment. This further confirms our overarching hypothesis that people are more likely to engage and respond to personal email than something they view as marketing.
In order for this method to scale, we’ll have to find a way to more efficiently get people signed up who reply to our emails.

experiments.title: How a direct call to action influences clicks on a sticky-bar
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer call-to-action drive more traffic to the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/11/2018
In this experiment, we were launching a t-shirt design contest for the 2018 NIO Summit. We placed a sticky bar on the homepage of www.niosummit.com to let visitors know about the contest. We tested two different calls to actions: Enter Your Design vs Learn More
experiments.keyLearnings: The “Learn More” call-to-action drove 53.4% more traffic to the landing page, although the experiment did not reach the 95% level of confidence we would have liked. Nevertheless, this is a strong indicator that a softer call-to-action for general traffic on the homepage is more effective at increasing clicks.

experiments.title: How a more direct call-to-action affect conversion from a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct call-to-action drive higher course registration conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2018 - 04/20/2018
We ran this experiment across several different audience groups in our Facebook advertising of our new email fundraising course. We wondered if a more direct call-to-action could be effective in driving higher conversions. The control used a call-to-action of “Learn More,” while the treatment used a call-to-action of “Active Your Free Online Course.”
experiments.keyLearnings: The more direct call-to-action saw an 84.9% decrease in conversion for this audience group (Network for Good). This runs counter to the result of the same experiment with the Nextafter.com traffic. Together, these are showing that people who have not been exposed to NextAfter need more exposure to the value propositon in order to trust that our offers are worthwhile. Those who have engaged with us in any capacity already have a high level of trust, and simply need to see whether or not the offer is relevant to them.

experiments.title: Will clarity in a Facebook ad headline increase blog traffic?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a clearer Facebook ad headline increase click to the blog post?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/04/2018
In this experiment, we had set up multiple promoted blog posts for the month of March. When setting up a promoted post for a blog comparing direct mail and email fundraising, we wondered if the blog headline was not clear enough to convey the point of the post. So we created a treatment that used clearer language.
experiments.keyLearnings: By changing the word “cannibalizing” to “stealing,” we were able to add additional clarity that led to a 32.4% increase in clicks to the blog post. This demonstrates the need for continual clarity in the way communicate. It’s easy to use jargon or insider phrases that mean something to us, but may not be as clear to the end user.

experiments.title: Can a “Start My Gift” Button on the Main Donation Page Reduce Friction for Year-End Donors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a “Start My Gift” button near the top of the main donation page for visitors during the year-end campaign will increase donor conversion by reducing friction for highly motivated donors and allowing them to quickly begin the giving process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2025 - 12/31/2025
During the year-end campaign, many donors arrive on the main donation page after clicking through from ads, emails, and other campaign touchpoints. While the page includes a detailed value proposition explaining the impact of giving, the team was concerned that the longer messaging could introduce friction for highly motivated donors who were already ready to give.
Specifically, visitors had to scroll past the page’s text before reaching the donation form, which could delay donors who simply wanted to give after responding to an appeal.
However, the team also feared that shortening the page would be counterproductive, as many visitors arrive still unconvinced to donate.
To address these concerns, we decided to test a “Start My Gift” button near the top of the donation page. The test variants were:
Control Version: visitors saw the standard donation page experience, where they would scroll down the page to reach the giving form
Treatment Version: a “Start My Gift” button was placed near the top of the page. When clicked, the button automatically scrolled the visitor directly to the donation form, allowing donors to skip the body copy and begin the giving process immediately
The goal of this test was to determine whether reducing friction for highly motivated donors would increase the donation page conversion rate during the year-end campaign. The test ran from December 19 – December 31.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall Conversion
Conversion rates were virtually identical between the two versions — 12.9% for the control and 12.8% for the treatment. Adding the "Start My Gift" button didn't hurt overall conversion, suggesting that a faster path to the form didn't undermine the page's persuasive messaging.
Revenue Per Visitor
There was no significant difference in overall one-time revenue per visitor. However, a meaningful shift emerged among larger donors: for gifts of $1,000 or more, the treatment increased revenue per visitor by 34.9% at 93.5% confidence. This suggests the button reduced friction for highly motivated mid-level and major donors who arrived ready to give and simply wanted a faster way to do it.
Monthly Sustainer Gifts
The clearest tradeoff was in recurring giving. The control generated 50 sustainer gifts compared to 31 for the treatment — a 36.8% decline at 95.8% confidence. A likely explanation: the button allowed donors to skip past the page's value proposition, reducing exposure to messaging that might have inspired a monthly commitment.
Mobile vs. Desktop
No meaningful differences emerged between devices. The button performed similarly across mobile and desktop.
Key Takeaway
Reducing friction helps unlock larger one-time gifts without hurting overall conversion — but it may come at the cost of recurring giving. Further testing should focus on whether a fast-access path can be designed in a way that preserves exposure to the monthly giving ask.

experiments.title: Does Problem–Solution Messaging Increase Year-End Main Donation Page Conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that clarifying and sharpening the problem statement on the year-end main donation page will increase donor conversion because clear problem–solution messaging strengthens donors’ understanding of the problem and their motivation to give. because the clear problem–solution messaging strengthens donors’ understanding of the problem and motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2025 - 11/25/2025
Recently, we tested stronger problem–solution messaging on the main donation page, clarifying families’ challenges and positioning the donor’s gift as a direct solution. This language led to a 20% increase in gifts, suggesting that clearer articulation of the problem and the donor’s role effectively boosts  motivation to give.
Based on these results, we aimed to test if applying problem–solution framing to the year-end donation page would also increase donor motivation and improve conversion rates.
Control Version: focused on the “Help Deliver Hope and Joy” theme, highlighted the matching gift, and listed ministry activities supported by donations.
Treatment Version: reframed the message to highlight the crisis families face and positioned year-end gifts as ways to restore hope, strengthen marriages, equip parents, and help children experience Christ’s love. It also reinforced the ministry’s impact and directly linked gifts to outcomes.
The test goal was to see if clearer problem framing and a stronger donor-impact message would boost conversion during this critical year-end fundraising campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment version of the page generated 119 gifts with a 9.6% conversion rate, compared to 138 gifts and a 10.8% conversion rate on the control, representing an 11.2% decrease in gifts. However, this result did not reach statistical significance (68.4% level of confidence), meaning the two versions of the page performed very similarly overall.

The test also did not reach the required sample size of 4,807 visitors, receiving only 2,513 total visitors before it was paused. Because of this limited traffic, it is difficult to draw a definitive conclusion about the impact of the messaging change on donor conversion.

In terms of donor behavior, both versions performed similarly in generating monthly sustainer gifts (5 on the control vs. 7 on the treatment), suggesting the messaging change did not meaningfully affect donors’ likelihood to start recurring giving.

However, the test was paused after six days due to a concerning trend in revenue performance. While conversion rates were relatively close, the average gift size differed significantly:
Control average gift: $44.95
 	Treatment average gift: $24.38
45.8% less revenue
93.6% level of confidence
very large gifts ($1,000+)
Control adjusted average gift: $14.84
 	Treatment adjusted average gift: $12.67
14.6% decrease in average gift
statistically inconclusive 78.4% level of confidence
directional rather than definitive
20% increase in gifts
continuing to test stronger value propositions and problem–solution framing

experiments.title: How does a radical landing page redesign impact email signups for a video series?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more structured and visually cohesive landing page design will improve readability and increase email signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2024 - 08/23/2024
This organization frequently creates and promotes free video series as a key strategy in their email acquisition efforts. Each video series is housed on a dedicated landing page, but over time, these landing pages have become visually inconsistent and difficult to scan—especially on mobile.
To improve the user experience and increase email signups, we designed a radical redesign of their landing page. The redesigned page includes several key updates aimed at enhancing readability and structure:
A more centered layout with narrowed text width
Addition for the logo
A light background behind the email acquisition form
A distinct background color behind the episode list (or impact statement list)
A new footer that includes a link to the Privacy Policy
Because the formatting of their video series pages varies, we divided them into two separate test groups: Standard pages and Lighthouse pages. For each group, we created two versions of the page. This write-up details results from the Lighthouse landing pages:
Control: The original, existing landing page
Treatment: The redesigned landing page with improved readability and structure
Traffic was split 50/50 between the Control and Treatment in both versions of the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
The redesigned landing page did not lead to a significant improvement in email signups compared to the original landing page. In fact, there was a slight decrease in email acquisitions when the Treatment page was used.
 	The statistical level of confidence for this experiment was only 47.7%, which is below the 95% threshold needed to consider the results valid. This indicates that the results could be due to random chance rather than a true difference between the control and treatment pages.
 	Given the inconclusive results of this experiment, it is important not to make any definitive conclusions or changes based on these findings. It would be advisable to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes and potentially different designs to determine the true impact on email signups.
 	In future experimentation, it is important to consider factors such as the layout, design elements, and overall user experience when testing landing page variations. It may also be beneficial to explore different hypotheses and iterate on design changes to find the most effective approach for increasing email signups.

experiments.title: How does a radical landing page (standard) redesign impact email signups for video series?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more structured and visually cohesive landing page design will improve readability and increase email signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2024 - 08/23/2024
An organization frequently creates and promotes free video series as a key strategy in their email acquisition efforts. Each video series is housed on a dedicated landing page, but over time, these landing pages have become visually inconsistent and difficult to scan—especially on mobile.
To improve the user experience and increase email signups, we designed a radical redesign of their landing page. The redesigned page includes several key updates aimed at enhancing readability and structure:
A more centered layout with narrowed text width
Addition of the logo
A light background behind the email acquisition form
A distinct background color behind the episode list (or impact statement list)
A new footer that includes a link to the Privacy Policy
Because the formatting of their video series pages varies, we divided them into two separate test groups: Standard pages and Lighthouse pages. For each group, we created two versions of the page. This write-up details results from the Standard landing pages:
Control: The original, existing landing page
Treatment: The redesigned landing page with improved readability and structure

experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the redesigned landing page did not perform better than the original landing page in terms of email acquisition for all traffic. There was a 0.7% decrease in email signups with a statistical confidence level of 31.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results of the test are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the results and ensure that the statistical significance meets the minimum threshold before making any conclusions or implementing changes based on the test. Additionally, considering other factors like user feedback, usability testing, and further iterations of the redesigned landing page could help in determining what changes need to be made to improve email signups effectively.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more structured and visually cohesive landing page design will improve readability and increase email signups.
experiments.summary:

An organization frequently creates and promotes free video series as a key strategy in their email acquisition efforts. Each video series is housed on a dedicated landing page, but over time, these landing pages have become visually inconsistent and difficult to scan—especially on mobile.

experiments.title: Will a campaign countdown or a daily countdown resonate better with users

rows.name: Control - Wiki with Recurring Donor Countdown
Treatment - Wiki with Daily Recurring Donor Countdown

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing the campaign goal with a daily goal for the campagin wiki  will achieve more transactions because by breaking the donor goal into the day we can make the numbers small which can lead to an increased buy in from users.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/21/2025 - 03/26/2025
Focus on the Family is a well known global ministry.
Right now, FOTF is in the middle of a recurring donor drive. To support this campaign, we wanted to add some elements to parts of the website that receives a lot of traffic.
For this test, we wanted to see if we could improve on our tried and true campaign wiki that sits at the top of the homepage by replacing the campaign goal to a daily goal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
- The hypothesis that replacing the campaign goal with a daily goal would lead to more transactions did not hold true in this experiment.
- The treatment, which displayed a daily goal instead of a campaign goal, resulted in a 12.0% decrease in Total Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control group.
- The statistical level of confidence for this result was 0.0%, indicating that the results are not reliable and should not be used for future decision making.
Future Experimentation:
- When designing future experiments, it is important to carefully evaluate the potential impact of changes to the website, such as replacing a campaign goal with a daily goal.
- It is essential to ensure that experiments are run for a sufficient duration and with a large enough sample size to generate statistically significant results.
- Since the results of this experiment are inconclusive, further testing may be needed to determine the effectiveness of displaying a daily goal versus a campaign goal for donor drives.
- It is important to continue testing and iterating on website elements to optimize for user engagement and donation conversions.

experiments.title: Will having shorter copy on the monthly donation page hurt or help recurring transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the copy for the monthly donation page will achieve more recurring transactions because the shorter copy reinforces the need to give without the extra copy that may create decision friction in the process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2025 - 01/29/2025
Historically, the homepage monthly and episodic donation pages for Focus on the Family have been hard to get a valid test to help increase transition and/or revuene. Last Spring and through the summer we found that mentioning “Biblical Reosurces” on the pages had a positive impact on donations and revenue. We wondered, if we could build off of that finding to improve the donation page copy even more.
For this test, we shortened the copy on the monthly donation page to only communicate about the impact that a donor wold have in empowering families with biblical resources. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that shortening the copy on the monthly donation page did not result in an increase in recurring transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a 7.8% decrease in transactions compared to the control group.

Since the statistical level of confidence is only 20.7%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making. It is important to run future experiments for a longer period of time to gather more data and ensure the results are statistically significant.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to test different messaging strategies on the donation page to see what resonates most with donors. Additionally, it could be helpful to explore other factors that may impact donation behavior, such as the design of the page, the suggested donation amounts, or the overall user experience. By continuing to test and iterate on different elements of the donation page, Focus on the Family can optimize for increased donations and revenue.

experiments.title: Will adding urgency to web elements increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing urgency for the lower third banner will achieve more transactions because the added urgency will motivate people to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2024 - 01/01/2025
During CYE we went throughs several versions of web elements to see which one would be the best to show on content pages. We found that adding what we’re calling a ‘lower third’ banner to the bottom of the page increased transactions compared to a dear reader.
Our next step was to find out how to optimize the lower third to present it in the best way possible. For this test, we wanted to see if adding urgency to the lower third element would increase transactions. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding urgency to the lower third banner can potentially increase transactions. Although the treatment group saw a 4.5% increase in transactions for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 19.9%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be deemed reliable for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is adequate to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further refine the concept of urgency and test different variations to see which one resonates most with the audience. It is also valuable to continue testing and iterating on different elements of the lower third banner to identify the most effective strategies for increasing transactions on the website.

experiments.title: Will adding a countdown timer to a donation page effectively increase urgency?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown timer to the donation page for the last week of the camapgin will achieve more transactions because the countdown timer will reinforce that time is running out and your donation is needed now.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2024 - 01/01/2025
For CYE this year we wanted to see if we could increase urgency on the donation page by adding a countdown timer to when the campaign ends.
We started this test the last week of the campaign when urgency should be at it’s peak.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a countdown timer to the donation page during the last week of a campaign can potentially increase revenue for all traffic. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was at 0.0%, which means that the results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to yield statistically significant results. It may be beneficial to run similar experiments with a larger sample size to accurately determine the impact of adding a countdown timer on donation page conversions.

Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is important to use caution in interpreting the findings and to prioritize statistical significance in making data-driven decisions for future campaigns and optimizations.

experiments.title: Will adding a donor testimonial to the sustainer donation page increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonal for the campaign donation pages will achieve more transactions because because the donor testimonial will lend to social proof and increase trust.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/11/2024 - 12/19/2024
This CYE we wanted to see if we could present a donor testimonail on the donation page to increase social proof and trust, in turn increasing transactions.
For this test we added a box right above the giving from that had a donor testimonal in it. We added this to all of the main campaign giving pages. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donor testimonial to the campaign donation pages did not lead to an increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, since the statistical confidence level is below 95%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

It's also important to note that this test only ran for a couple of days due to the stark decrease in conversions. In the future, it would be worth testing this again to see if it just got off to a slow start.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different elements on the donation page to see what resonates best with donors. It may be worth exploring other forms of social proof or trust-building elements, such as testimonials from beneficiaries, impact stories, or trust seals. It is also important to ensure that experiments run for a longer period of time in order to gather more reliable and valid data for decision making.

experiments.title: Will adding a sticky bar with a donor countdown on the episodic donation page impact transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar  for the campaign episodic donation page will achieve more transactions because the sticky bar will increase the urgency and motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/06/2025
For this test, we wanted to see if adding additional urgency/ motivation on the campaign episodic donation page would impact the number of transactions made.
We tested this on the monthly donation page and found that it did increase transactions and revenue. You can see the results of that test here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar to the campaign episodic donation page can increase transactions, potentially due to the increased urgency and motivation it provides to donors. However, the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, with a confidence level of only 53.8%.
Similar to the test on the recurring donation page, we also saw an increase in average gift when the stickybar was present on the page. ($288/avg. gift vs. $388/avg. gift).
This campaign was ran towards the end of December when urgency is at it's highest. It's important to note that the treatment was winning up until the 28th (last day this was scheduled to run). In future testing it might be worth testing this approach earlier in the campaign and not at the end with the urgency for most people to give is already there.

experiments.title: How a headline on the donation page achieve a higher donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that saying ‘deliver hope’ instead of ‘give hope’ for the family episodic donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the fotf audience across their website has responded better to it.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/04/2024 - 11/20/2024
In our testing series of figuring out if ‘deliver hope’ vs. ‘give hope’ resonates more with the fotf audience. We’ve found that on the homepage and monthly donation pages are showing promising results of the ‘deliver’ message being the more effective across their website.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the 'deliver hope' messaging did not result in a higher donor conversion rate on the family episodic donation page compared to the 'give hope' messaging. The treatment group actually experienced a 6.4% decrease in transactions for all traffic, indicating that the control group performed better in this case.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 56.8%, which means that these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making. It is important to have a statistical level of confidence of at least 95% to draw reliable conclusions from an experiment.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is large enough and that the statistical analysis is robust to generate valid and actionable results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to continue testing different messaging strategies to see what resonates best with the Focus on the Family audience, taking into account the results of previous experiments to inform future decision-making.

experiments.title: Will changing the headline on the donation page increase donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing ‘give hope’ to ‘deliver hope’ for the family monthly donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because we’ve seen the fotf audience respond better to deliver hope vs give hope.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/04/2024 - 11/20/2024
Following suit in our first couple of experiments for CYE, we wanted to see if we changing out ‘give hope’ to ‘deliver hope’ would have the same effect as it did on the wiki header and the sohl monthly donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing 'give hope' to 'deliver hope' on the family monthly donation page resulted in a significant increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment group saw a 176.8% increase in Transactions - One-Time for All Traffic compared to the control group. However, since the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, these results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the language used in donation appeals to see if 'deliver hope' consistently outperforms 'give hope' with a higher level of statistical confidence. It may also be valuable to explore other variations of messaging to further optimize donor conversion rates. Additionally, it is important to ensure that experiments run for a longer duration to gather more data and achieve a higher level of confidence in the results.

experiments.title: Will adding a personal ask to a campaign wikibanner increase donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personalizing the wiki banner ask CTA for the cye campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate on this high traffic page because the donor will connect with Jim, which adds a personal element to the ask during this season of highly motivated giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2024 - 12/19/2024
For all Catalytic campaigns, FOTF has tested having a wiki banner on their home page. In previous campaigns, we have tested several different tactics and messages and have combined all of the winners to get to where the control is.
For this campaign, we wanted to see if personalizing the ask by adding an image of Jim and having the ask be from Jim would affect the donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. Adding an image of Jim and having the ask come from him in the wiki banner for the CYE campaign led to a statistically significant increase in donor conversion rates compared to our control.

2. The treatment group saw an 11.8% increase in transactions for all traffic, indicating that donors may connect more with a personal appeal from a specific individual like Jim.

3. While the results were statistically significant at a 78.8% confidence level, they did not meet the standard threshold of 95% for validity. This means that the results should be interpreted with caution and should be used in further testing.

4.This treatment approach was ran at a time of year where donor's motivation to give is high. Our team would like to retest during another campaign and during an "evergreen" month to see if the results would be similar.

5. Ordinarily, we'd test this test longer but during a high urgency campaign like CYE, we have a more aggressive testing schedule.

experiments.title: What’s the Impact of Adding Additional Giving Options on Content Pages Through a Lower Third Banner?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the lower third instead of a wikipedia ad for a direct ask on content pages will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the visibility of the ask will be greater and there are more options to give on this specific element..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/25/2024 - 12/09/2024
During previous catalytic campaigns. FOTF has tested into having what we call “Wikipedia” ads on the top scroll of their content pages. We want to know if adding a new look to our CTA during CYE help draw more eyes to the opportunity to give and if having additional options to give increases donor conversion and to what degree.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment, which involved adding lower thirds instead of a dear reader for a direct ask on content pages, resulted in a 65.5% decrease in Revenue for All Traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence for these results is 0.0%, meaning that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider other factors that could have influenced the outcome, such as the timing of the experiment, the audience segment targeted, or the messaging used in the lower thirds.

It is recommended to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size and to take into account all possible variables that could impact the results. Additionally, it may be helpful to test different variations of the ask, including both dear reader messages and direct asks, to determine the most effective approach for increasing donor conversion rates on content pages.

experiments.title: What happens when you replace an ad with a dear reader
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing an ad style cta with a dear reader for users that are on the article pages will achieve a higher donor converstion rate because a dear reader contains more value proposition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2024 - 04/16/2024
Focus on the Family receives a ton of traffic to their article pages every day. In an effort to further engage this audience there are several ad style CTAs on the page to lead users to different video series or content offers. We wondered if we could test an “ad style” cta with a dear reader.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that replacing an ad style CTA with a "dear reader" message on article pages resulted in a 32.6% increase in revenue for all traffic. This indicates that although more people don't convert when seeing the dear reader, they do give more generously when they get to the donation page.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the effectiveness of the "dear reader" message against other CTAs or variations. By conducting further experiments with larger sample sizes and ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence, more conclusive insights can be gained to inform decisions related to optimizing donor conversion rates on article pages.

experiments.title: How adding the gift amount in a donation form’s submit button affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donor’s gift amount to the submit button for eBook campaign donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the increase in clarity on the donation amount will lead more people to donate. because  the increase in clarity on the donation amount will lead more people to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/11/2024 - 11/08/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running ads for their Election Fraud eBook offer, where visitors fill out a form to receive a copy of the eBook and then are redirected to a donation page. In an attempt to increase donor conversion rate on the campaign’s donation page, they wanted to test if adding the donor’s selected gift amount to the donation form’s submit button would increase clarity and lead more people to feel secure in their donation.
The control’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT”
The treatment’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT OF $[amount]”
The control and treatment each received 50% of traffic from Facebook ads, prospecting and housefile emails, and on-site popups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the donor's gift amount to the submit button for the eBook campaign did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 7.4%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.
Therefore, we should not make any conclusive decisions based on the results of this experiment. It is important to recognize that sometimes variations in performance may occur due to random chance rather than a true impact of the changes made. In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that a sufficient sample size is used and that statistical significance is reached before drawing conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test other variations or factors that could potentially impact donor conversion rates to gain a better understanding of what drives donor behavior.

experiments.title: How lowering the gift array on the instant donation page of an ebook affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that decreasing the amounts on the gift array for people who have signed up to receive an eBook will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the lower suggested gift amounts will make giving a gift feel more attainable to a larger amount of people.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/26/2024 - 09/12/2024
An organization has an offer where people sign up to receive an eBook and then are led to a donation page where they can make a gift to support the organization’s efforts. In an attempt to increase the donor conversion rate on this page, they wanted to test whether lowering the gift array amount options would lower the barrier to donating and in turn increase the amount of donors.
The control’s gift array was $25, $50, and $100.
The treatment’s gift array was $10, $25, and $50.
The experiment ran for one month and the control and treatment each received %50 of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that decreasing the amounts on the gift array may have a positive impact on the donor conversion rate. The treatment group, which had lower gift amount options of $10, $25, and $50, saw a 9.4% increase in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical confidence level of 61.6% is below the threshold of 95%, meaning that these results are invalid and may not be reliable.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide statistically significant results. It may also be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and increase the confidence level of the results. Additionally, it could be useful to test different variations of gift amounts to see which combination yields the best results in terms of donor conversion rates.

Overall, while the results of this experiment suggest that lowering the gift array amounts can potentially increase donor conversion rates, further testing is needed to confirm these findings and make informed decisions for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How will increasing the readability on the donation page affect conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the readability for the main donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it will give a better user expereience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/21/2024 - 09/03/2024
The statement ‘many donors that make up the bulk of the program are over the age of 65’ reign true.
As we think about being user friendly and making the experience easier for users, it’s important to look at the demographic of those who frequent the donation pages.
For this test we wanted to see what would happen if we increased the readability of the donation page. For this we increased the font size, increased the margins, increased the line spacing, etc..
Here is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that increasing the readability of the donation page did not lead to a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group experienced a decrease in revenue compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was below the threshold of 95%, making these results inconclusive and not suitable for future decision making.

Therefore, it is important to be cautious when implementing changes based on assumptions without conducting thorough testing. Future experimentation should continue to focus on user experience and readability but should be done so with careful monitoring and analysis of results before making any significant changes. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider other factors such as donor demographics and preferences to tailor the donation page for the specific audience.

experiments.title: Will having a guided reading experience improve transactions on a donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a guided reading expereince for the campaign donation page will achieve a higher number of conversions because the donation page will explain what the campaign proves to solve and how the donor can help in a easy to digest way.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/17/2024 - 11/01/2024
Each year this organization runs a a campaign that drives traffic to a main campaign donation page.
We wanted to see what would happen if we added a guided reading experience to the donation page that listed out the problem, the solution, and what you can do to help would increase conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the addition of a guided reading experience to the campaign donation page resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic by 64.3%. This indicates that the guided reading experience likely helped to better explain the problem, the solution, and how donors can help in a more easily digestible way, leading to higher conversions.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the impact of guided reading experiences on donation pages to further validate the initial results. It may also be beneficial to explore different formats or variations of the guided reading experience to see if there is an optimal design that maximizes conversions. Additionally, conducting further tests over longer periods of time with a larger sample size would provide more robust and reliable results to confidently conclude the effectiveness of the guided reading experience in driving donations.

experiments.title: Will adding in the value proposition copy from the donate button to the monthly donation page increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding biblical resources for the monthly donation page will achieve will increase transactions because their is congruency between the donate button and the copy on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2024 - 10/29/2024
During our testing travels with the duel donation buttons in the main navigation bar we found that the value proposition of providing biblical resources led to an increase in clicks to the donation page.
We wanted to see what would happen if we adding that same value proposition to the donation page copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the value proposition of providing biblical resources to the donation page copy significantly increased revenue for all traffic. The treatment group, which included the added biblical resources copy, saw a 157.6% increase in revenue with a 97.6% level of confidence, indicating that this change had a substantial impact.

This indicates that there is a strong congruency between the messaging on the donation button and the copy on the donation page, which likely contributed to the increase in transactions. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring ways to align the messaging throughout the donation process to maintain this congruency.

Additionally, it would be worthwhile to test other potential value propositions or messaging strategies on donation pages to see if they also lead to an increase in donations. By continuing to test and optimize the messaging and value propositions on donation pages, and can further enhance its donation performance and maximize its impact.

experiments.title: Will Adding ACH as the Preferred Method of Payment Decrease Donor Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by showing ACH as the preferred of payment for donors coming the monthly giving page will achieve longer donor retention in the long run because banking information expires with less frequency over credits cards. An added benefit is the reduction in credit card processing fees as well.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/27/2024 - 09/23/2024
An organization worked on identifying ways to retain their monthly partners long-term. Because bank account information vs. debit/credit card information tends to change or expire less often than credit cards, will we retain our donors longer? Simultaneously, we also need to see if showing ACH as the preferred method of payment on the monthly giving page hurts our current donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that showing ACH as the preferred method of payment on the monthly giving page resulted in a 6.2% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 20.2%, which is below the 85% threshold for validity. This shows us an important finding. Including ACH as the preferred method of payment on the monthly donation page DOES NOT hurt donor conversion and can be considered for monthly giving pages as the preferred method of payment as it will help retain monthly partners longer.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the call to action below a button impacts clicks to activate a free membership trial
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding copy below each button on the landing page for site visitors will achieve an increase in clicks through to the membership activation page because it reinforces the call to action that they will get a free 30 days of membership.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/27/2024
We noticed a decline in clicks through to activate a free NextAfter Institute membership trial, so we looked back at earlier, better-performing versions of our landing page and noticed there used to be copy reinforcing the CTA below each button on the page.
