So, we tested adding the copy back to see how that would impact clicks through to the activation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no distinct difference in the results between the control and the treatment. The test did not validate. We will test this again during a time where we are pushing more traffic to this page to see if we can validate a result.

experiments.title: Does Aligning the CTA and the Value Propostion Make a Difference in Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the cta and the value prop for the main donate buttons will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there will be less decision friction in the process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/22/2024
This is the third test in the series of the dual donate button test for FOTF.
The first test we wanted to see if aligning the CTA would make an impact. The second test we wanted to see if aligning the value proposition would make an impact (this test did!)
For the third iteration, we wanted to see if we could test “Give” vs. “Donate” as the CTA to see which language aligns better with the audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using "Donate" as the call-to-action (CTA) for the main donate buttons resulted in a 11.9% decrease in donations compared to using "Give". However, the statistical level of confidence was only 73.9%, which is below the 85% threshold for validity.
The key finding during this testing series is that the main CTA does nto make much of an impact, but using as much specificity as possible when describing the impact of a donor's gift - even at the button level - does.

experiments.title: Does adding a personal story to a dear reader connect with donors more?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a personalized story for the SeeLife dear reader will achieve a higher conversion rate  because the story aspect to the copy will make donors connect more so than just with the campaigns copy. because  the story aspect to the copy will make donors connect more so than just with the campaigns copy.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/22/2024 - 05/31/2024
During previous High Urgency Campaigns FOTF has tested into putting Dear Readers on their article pages.
In previous tests we have tried several different variations to get to what our control is now. However, we wondered if it could still be optimized. For this test we wanted to see if we could adjust the copy to be about “Rachel” to see if we could connect with donors in a different way than just creating urgency.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the personalized story about "Rachel" did not lead to a higher conversion rate compared to the original control with the urgent copy. The treatment actually saw a decrease in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, meaning these results are not considered valid.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the messaging and how it resonates with donors. While personalization can be effective in connecting with donors, it is crucial to test different approaches to see what works best for the target audience. Additionally, ensuring a sufficient sample size and statistical confidence level is important to draw valid conclusions from the experiment. This experiment should not be used to make any definitive decisions, but rather as a learning experience to inform future testing and optimization efforts.

experiments.title: Will a “Give” button have a higher donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing donate to give for the sticky button will achieve a higher donor conversion because give could be more cohesive with the messaging during year end.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/04/2024
For this experiment we wanted to see if changing the Donate sticky button on the side of the web pages to say “Give” would increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that changing the sticky button from "Donate" to "Give" did not achieve a higher donor conversion. In fact, it resulted in a decrease in donations for all traffic.

experiments.title: How an in-line banner ask on an article page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a wiki style banner for the article pages will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the additional value prop on the page can motivate people to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2023 - 01/01/2024
For a high urgency campaign we wanted to test if having an ask on an article page above the main hero image would impact donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: Having the wiki style banner ask on the articles pages increased the total number of transactions by 6.6% but with only a 41% LOC. While the transactions did increase, the revenue actually saw a decrease by 7.6%. This leads me to believe that there could have been an large gift in the control that should not be taken into account when reviewing the results for this experiment.

experiments.title: How using a personal image on a sticky bar impacts clicks to a webinar page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky bar that puts a face to the name of the presenter for our website traffic will achieve a higher click-through rate to the registration page because it adds authority to the ask and makes it more personal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2024 - 06/27/2024
Something we’ve tested into a lot with clients is a “humanized sticky bar.” In an effort to humanize our messaging more and improve our user engagement, I wanted to apply that concept to registration for a LinkedIn Livestream.
The design of the sticky bar stayed the same with the only difference being the addition of Nathan’s (the presenter’s) headshot.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test did not validate. We ran this during a very short window of time, so I would like to test this again during a longer period of time to see if we can get a larger sample size and get it to validate.

experiments.title: How Adding a Content Offer Exit Intent Affects Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a second cta  for when a user abondends the page will achieve a higher email acq. rate because signing up for a content offer is a less committed ask than making a donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/31/2024 - 06/19/2024
Focus on the Family ran a SeeLife campaign which drives a ton of traffic to their campaign donation page. When a user decides not to make a gift, we wanted to see if we could get them to convert on a content offer instead.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment include:
1. The treatment, which involved offering a second call-to-action for users who abandoned the donation page, resulted in a 1.4% increase in Transactions - One-Time for All Traffic. This indicates that providing an alternative action for users who choose not to make a donation could potentially lead to more conversions.
2. The Email Acquisition metric experienced a significant 96.1% increase with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that offering a content offer as an alternative to making a donation can be an effective way to acquire email addresses and potentially nurture leads for future donations.
3. While the results showed a positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 15.1%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not considered statistically valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:
1. Continue testing alternative actions for users who do not convert on the primary call-to-action, such as offering content offers or other engagement opportunities. These alternative actions can help capture leads and potentially convert them into donors in the long run.
2. Pay attention to the statistical significance of the results. Results with low levels of confidence should be viewed with caution and further testing may be needed to validate the findings before making any strategic decisions based on the experiment.
3. Use the insights from this experiment to inform future testing strategies and prioritize initiatives that focus on email acquisition and lead nurturing, as these can play a critical role in driving conversions and donor engagement.

experiments.title: How Changing a Headline to a Universal Value Proposition Affects Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating a universal headline that focuses on the value proposition for the GOAA/SOAA pages will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the headline will not be talking about the transaction, but rather the value that a person would get if they choose to make a donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/15/2024 - 05/31/2024
Focus on the Family has several GOAA/SOAA (gift of any amount/ sustainer of any amount) pages that they will drive traffic to each day from various sources. Each page is only advertised for a day or two. For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could create a headline that could lead people to convert more, but also make the headline broad enough that it could be used for all of the pages. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that creating a universal headline that focuses on the value proposition for the donation pages can potentially lead to an increase in donor conversion rates. In this particular case, the treatment version of the headline resulted in an 18.5% increase in Transaction - Recurring for All Traffic, although the statistical level of confidence was only 59.5%.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing headlines that focus on the value proposition for donors rather than the transaction itself. This approach seems to resonate with potential donors and may lead to higher conversion rates. It is also important to ensure that the experiments are run for a longer period to gather more data and achieve a higher level of statistical confidence before making any definitive conclusions.

experiments.title: How using a lighter background on a video series landing page impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a light background for the video series landing page will achieve an increase in clicks to the checkout page because the contrast from the white background will improve readability for the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2024 - 04/25/2024
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a large Christian ministry organization that creates and shares free resources like video series to encourage families. They created a landing page for a new paid video series and wanted to know if the current, black background of the page was harming the user experience.
We decided to test a light-background version of the page against the current dark background. Our hypothesis was that the white background would lead to more clicks to the checkout page because white backgrounds in web design are shown to enhance readability and reduce visual strain.
We ran this as a split URL test in VWO, with a random 50% of the traffic going to the dark background page. The other 50% were instead shown a light background version of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment was that changing the background color of the video series landing page from black to white did not result in the desired increase in clicks to the checkout page. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 2.4% decrease in visits to the Focus Store Checkout Page compared to the control group.

Additionally, the statistical level of confidence was only 15.3%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results of this experiment were essentially flat and shouldn't be considered a valid test result.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that changing the background color alone may not be enough to drive increased engagement with the checkout page. Further research and experimentation may be necessary to identify the most effective strategies for improving user experience and encouraging clicks to the checkout page.

experiments.title: How reducing the size of the Express Checkout button on donation pages impacted revenue and donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the size of the Express Checkout button for visitors to our video series acquisition donation pages will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the smaller button will provide less of a competition for the donor’s attention as they’re making their gift, and this means that the friction will be reduced.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/11/2024 - 06/26/2024
The team at Focus on the Family (FOTF) has been testing various elements of their donation pages to determine which factors could improve donor conversion and revenue. One element that we wanted to test was the Express Checkout button that appears in the donation form below the gift array. Users who click this button can choose to donate through Google Pay and the idea is that this makes the donation process easier and thus increases donor conversion. However, we wondered if this button could serve as a distraction during the donation process and actually negatively affect donor conversion.
FOTF first tested removing the Express Checkout button on their main donation page and the results were very flat. They then tested whether reducing the Express Checkout button’s size would drive a different outcome. This test found that the smaller button size led to a 21.3% increase in transactions, with a 95.7% level of confidence.
The purpose of this experiment is to see if that lift in transactions will occur when we test the smaller button on FOTF’s video series acquisition donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this test were completely flat; we didn't see any significant changes in revenue or transactions between the control and treatment. This means that, even though we didn't see any statistically-significant improvement on these metrics, the smaller button didn't hurt our donor conversion or average gift.

Because the smaller button saw a positive lift on Focus on the Family's other donation pages -- and did not harm their acquisition donation page conversion -- it's recommended that the smaller button treatment be rolled out on all of their donation pages.

experiments.title: How using shorter, more conversational language on a content offer instant donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shorter and more conversational language on an instant offer donation page will increase donor conversion by removing friction, particularly for mobile audiences.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/05/2024 - 04/04/2024
Focus on the Family uses dozens of free video series content offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. For the last year, their offer instant donation pages have all used the same template sharing “5 things you should know.” Reviewing this design, their team felt it could have been more conversational and shorter, especially on mobile devices.
We created a shorter, “3 things you should know” template used VWO to split donation page traffic for their popular Kingdom Mindset offer. The test ran a full month.
Our hope was to at least find no negative impacts from the treatment as it was preferred by Focus’ team and ours. Ideally, we want to see if we could validate the increase.
experiments.keyLearnings:
During the first three weeks, we experienced a significant increase in donor conversion, hovering around an 80% or greater confidence level. However, in the final week of the test, that confidence diminished.
When we examined the results, we saw that every visitor segment and device category preferred the new version to the control, but again, at a lower than 95% Confidence level. Mobile audiences saw the largest increase in donor conversion at 25% over control but there were only 5 donations in the mobile segment.
The Focus team decided to continue testing the new, shorter version and leave behind the longer one since they did not experience a negative result during the test and overall preferred the way the new template looked on mobile devices. We will continue to test and refine this new template.

experiments.title: How Changing the Headlines on GOAA/SOAA Pages to be focused on the value of the content vs the organization impacted donor transaction rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the headlines to be more value focused for the GOAA/SOAA pages will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because traffic coming to these pages are more focused on receiving the premium being offered rather than the organizational value prop.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/26/2024
Focus on the Family have GOAA/SOAA (gift of any amount, sustainer of any amount) pages where you can make a donation of any amount and receive the premium that is being offered. These pages are often called out on their broadcast for specific days. Usually the headline is focused around the organization. However, we wondered if we could change the focus of the headline to be focused on the value that they would be receiving.
Because these pages only draw traffic for a day or two we had to string multiple days together to be able to test. Each day offers a different premium, therefore no two days had the same headlines.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the headlines on the GOAA/SOAA donation pages to be more value-focused did not yield the desired results. In fact, the treatment group, which received the value-focused headlines, saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 11.1%.

experiments.title: How adding autonomy to a “Wiki” banner impacts revenue V.1
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a decision point to make an impact for people who land on the homepage during a HUC will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the donor has complete autonomy to click “yes” and participate or “no” and close out of the ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2024 - 03/13/2024
Would adding a question and a simple decision to make an impact increase donor conversion on the homepage campaign wiki banner? By adding a level of autonomy to the donor experience and giving them “control” to say “Yes, I’d like to make an impact” or “No, I’m not interested today,” would we invite more participation in the campaign and insight additional donor conversions over our “regular” campaign messaging with a gift array?
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a question and a simple decision to make an impact on the homepage campaign wiki banner resulted in a 21.5% increase in revenue for all traffic. This suggests that giving donors the autonomy to choose whether they want to participate or not can positively impact donor conversion rates. Further investigation will need to be done in order to see if the increase in revenue is actually related to the experiment or if there is a large gift that needs to be discluded.

experiments.title: Adding a Callout Above the Monthly Gift Frequency Button on the Campaign Giving Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a brief but impactful message above the monthly gift frequency button on our sustainer campaign giving pages will increase the donor conversion rate because of the social proof and added exclusivity.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2024 - 03/26/2024
By adding a brief but impactful message above the monthly gift frequency button on our sustainer campaign giving pages, will this element help add additional direction to those motivated to give and who will have already landed on the campaign giving page? By adding a donor countdown animation within the message, we hope to add an additional layer of “encouragement” to give during the giving process by showing donors can be part of the answer and help reach an impactful goal through their gift by “becoming one of” the donors needed. We believe adding a handwritten, donor countdown callout above the monthly gift frequency button will insight more donors to get involved and make a monthly donation.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of a handwritten, donor countdown callout above the monthly gift frequency button showed a 9.7% increase in recurring transactions at a 23% level of confidence. This test overall was pretty flat. In the future, we would like to rerun this test on a campaign-giving page for a longer amount of time to see if we can reach validity.

experiments.title: Removing Express Checkout on the Sustainer Campaign Giving Pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing express checkout for HUC donation pages will achieve a higher percentage of conversion because there will be less decision friction once a donor hits the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2024 - 03/12/2024
On previous tests for FOTF, we’ve seen varying degrees of success when testing the removal or decrease in size of the Express Checkout method of payment on FOTF-giving pages. Would removing the Express Checkout on the Sustainer campaign giving pages also yield the same results?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. Removing the Express Checkout for the Sustainer campaign giving pages resulted in a significant decrease in Transaction - Recurring by 60.1%.
2. There was a 61.2% statistical level of confidence in the result for Transaction - Recurring, indicating that the control group performed better.
3. The Transactions - One-Time experienced an 861% decrease with a 100% level of confidence, and Transactions experienced an 840% decrease with a 100% level of confidence.
Moving forward, it is important to note that the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid due to the statistical level of confidence being below 85%. Therefore, it is not recommended to make decisions solely based on these results. In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to reconsider the hypothesis and perhaps conduct further testing with a larger sample size or different variations to draw more conclusive findings.

experiments.title: How adding autonomy a wiki banner affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding autonomy language to the Wikipedia CTA will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because we are giving the choice to the donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/02/2024
By adding an opportunity for FOTF article readers to determine whether they wanted to participate in the Sustainer campaign – giving them an opportunity to say “Yes, I want to strengthen our nation’s families” or “No, I’m not interested today” — would help inspire more readers to interact with the campaign Wikipedia buttons and insight more donors to give during the sustainer campaign. By adding this language approach, will we see higher donor conversion with this campaign treatment (Wikipedia campaign ad) we’ve seen do well in the past?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding autonomy language to the Wikipedia CTA did not result in a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment saw a 5.3% decrease in donations for all traffic, with a statistical level of confidence of 25.0%. However, since the confidence level is below the standard threshold of 85%, these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be important to continue testing different approaches to see what resonates best with donors. It's possible that the autonomy language did not have the desired effect in this particular context, but that doesn't mean it won't be effective in other scenarios. By conducting further tests and analyzing the results with a higher level of statistical confidence, you can gather more reliable insights to inform future campaigns and strategies.

experiments.title: How focusing on impact rather than dollar amount impacted donation conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the buttons on the gift array for people who are wanting to give on the homepage will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it is more appealing for people to see the difference that they will be making.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2023 - 12/15/2023
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could increase donor conversion by allowing a prospective donor to choose the impact that they wanted to make rather than a dollar amount that they would give.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that allowing prospective donors to choose the impact they want to make instead of a specific dollar amount might lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment group, which had the new buttons on the gift array, saw an 11.2% increase in revenue compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 66.1%.

experiments.title: How modeling ad targeting after just your most qualified contacts impacts qualified lead generation results
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that modeling after our most highly qualified contacts for our target audience will achieve an increase in qualified leads generated (and/or decrease our cost per new qualified lead generated) because it will be based upon a better source list.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/01/2023 - 01/31/2023
As a part of our recent experimentation with our own acquisition efforts, we decided to try specifically modeling a lookalike audience in Facebook (Meta) Advertising based upon our most engaged/best qualified contacts in the database, rather than our control approach (which is a mixture of interest-based targeting, lookalike models from our overall email database, and directly uploaded prospecting lists).
Would this improve the overall volume of the new qualified leads (and decrease the cost per qualified lead)?
experiments.keyLearnings:
After a month of experimenting with this hypothesis, we were unable to fully validate this concept, although we have some directional data that suggests that it is not necessary to model your prospecting audience within Facebook (Meta) Advertising solely based upon your most qualified contacts.
It's worth documenting some other movement on KPI's as a part of this study, although it did not validate.
Those are:
The control list had a 41% lower cost per qualified lead generated when compared to the treatment list.The control list generated 8% less "generic email domain" leads when compared to the treatment list.The control list did have a slightly higher cost per lead generated (+5.3%) when compared to the treatment list.
Conclusion:
Our hypothesis as to "why" this is that it could simply be due to pure audience size — whereas the "control" list was 41.9% larger than that of the "Qualified Contact Lookalike" list used within the control. This manifests itself in the CPM (or "Cost per Thousand Impressions Served") metric — which was 11.9% less expensive for the larger control list when compared to the treatment.
With all of that said, this evidence suggests that it's not worth the effort to model just off of your most qualified contacts within Facebook (Meta) Advertising, at least as it pertains to our own B2B marketing campaigns. It may differ within a donor program, and is worth further experimentation in that regard.

experiments.title: How leads generated through Facebook Lead Ad Forms performed through our welcome series

rows.name: Control - Ad Click to a Hosted Landing Page/Form

experiments.researchQuestion: How leads generated through Facebook Lead Ad Forms performed (from an engagement status) once they made it through the welcome series of emails to onboard them as a new subscriber.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/20/2022
Recently, we experimented with using Facebook Lead Ads for our own acquisition campaign efforts, which we discovered that we could increase email signup rates by +131.5% (LoC: 100%), but the real question was “How would these leads perform downstream.”
We marked two check-in points, which were:
To review unsubscribe, bounce, disengagement, and never-opener rates after all leads generated in the experiment have “completed the welcome series.” And,
60-days downstream to see if either lead segment produced a more engaged subscriber well after the welcome series had ended, and they were introduced to 2-months of “live messaging.”
This study is focused on the first of these two check-in points, so we wanted to review how the leads performed now that all leads in the study had enough time to make it through the welcome series of emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Now that we're through the "welcome series" of messaging, we could not fully validate an increase in email engagement rates, but do see a slight improvement on subscriber engagement from the treatment segment.

This is actually very encouraging news, as it indicates that there is no discernible difference between using the control lead generation experience when compared to using the Facebook Lead Ad Form treatment approach.

To put it another way: The treatment experience has increased email signup rates by +131.5% (with 100% level of confidence), but HAS NOT produced a lower quality of email subscriber (at least through the point where all leads have successfully made it through the "welcome series" for new subscribers).

Perhaps this means that the hypothesis that Facebook Lead Ad Forms generate "bad emails" is debunked — at least in our own case.

We'll continue to monitor performance and look forward to checking back in at the 60-day mark from the point of when these emails were acquired to see if there is any noticeable difference between the quality of the two approaches to generating leads.

experiments.title: How content preview impacted the click-through rate on a digest email

rows.name: Some cool new things for you, Vilavanh
Some cool new things for you, Danielle

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing preview content from a digest email for our general subscribers will achieve more click-throughs than a digest email with preview content.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/31/2022 - 09/02/2022
We previously tested adding preview links to a digest email as a strategy to increase click-through rate on a digest email (https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/4515). In that test, there was a 30% increase in click-throughs on the treatment email.
To further test this, we wanted to go one step further and test using the preview links with our normal content previews beneath vs. just the preview links. We were curious to know if the extra content added value to digest recipients, or if it had no impact on their email engagement.
experiments.keyLearnings: Results did not validate. It seems removing preview content had no real impact on the click-through rate of the digest email, but results were trending in favor of the control version with preview content.

experiments.title: How creating intrigue in an email promoting new research impacted email signup rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that building intrigue for subscribers we are promoting new research to will achieve increase email signup rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/08/2022
As a part of launching our new research study for how the industry handles a New Donor Welcome Series, we decided to experiment with a bullet list of questions that the research would answer, thereby increasing the “intrigue” factor for the reader.
Our thought was that this intrigue would drive more motivated clicks and increase email signup rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Unfortunately, this treatment did not have any substantial impact on the email signup rate. Although it shows a -7.6% email signup rate, the level of confidence is less than a coin flip probability to be repeated.

This means that the treatment did not create more (or less) motivation for a subscriber to get our new research.

This could simply be due to the fact that new research is just in such high demand that any lower-level experiments to manufacture "more demand" aren't necessary. On the other hand, it could be due to the fact that this approach was ineffective and should be retested in future opportunities to re-test the concept.

experiments.title: How the “fear of loss” positioning impacted email signup rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that telling someone they’ll lose something instead of telling them what they’ll get for prospective customers will achieve increase email signup rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/09/2022
As a part of the marketing campaign to promote the launch of a new online course for NextAfter, we were promoting email signups to participate in our online “training cohort” program (for a guided tour through the course to maximize course consumption and learning retention).
The day before the course was launched, we decided to write a “last chance” (to signup) email and send it the day before the course went live.
In reviewing the copy for the course promotion, we decided to test using the “fear of loss” concept to see if it would drive a higher email signup rate for the training cohort/course.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Unfortunately, we did not generate enough results to scientifically validate the lift found in the treatment. With only a 54.1% level of confidence, these results are sadly no more reliable than a "coin flip chance" of producing the same lift percentages shown.
This was due to a much smaller audience size on the email resend, which ultimately just didn't produce enough samples.
This concept should be retested in the future to see if pairing "exclusive access" and "the loss of that exclusive access" if the reader doesn't take action can actually substantially lift response rates in the future.

experiments.title: How the “fear of loss” positioning impacted email signup rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that telling someone they’ll lose something instead of telling them what they’ll get for prospective customers will achieve increase email signup rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/09/2022
As a part of the marketing campaign to promote the launch of a new online course for NextAfter, we were promoting email signups to participate in our online “training cohort” program (for a guided tour through the course to maximize course consumption and learning retention).
The day before the course was launched, we decided to write a “last chance” (to signup) email and send it the day before the course went live.
In reviewing the copy for the course promotion, we decided to test using the “fear of loss” concept to see if it would drive a higher email signup rate for the training cohort/course.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Unfortunately, we did not generate enough results to scientifically validate the lift found in the treatment. With only a 54.1% level of confidence, these results are sadly no more reliable than a "coin flip chance" of producing the same lift percentages shown.

This was due to a much smaller audience size on the email resend, which ultimately just didn't produce enough samples.

This concept should be retested in the future to see if pairing "exclusive access" and "the loss of that exclusive access" if the reader doesn't take action can actually substantially lift response rates in the future.

experiments.title: How an urgency-focused sticky bar on an email acquisition page affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an urgency-focused sticky bar for landing page visitors will achieve greater ebook downloads.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2020 - 12/15/2020
In this experiment, we wondered if adding a sticky bar callout on a new ebook’s download page could create a heightened sense of urgency and lead to more conversions on the page.
The book was focused on strategies to help grow fundraising results during the last week of the year. So the sticky bar emphasized that the last week of the year is coming soon.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw no significant difference between the two variants. The sticky bar had a small decrease of 4.8% but at a low confidence level (67%).

Now, we have seen sticky bars like this perform well in other areas. Likely, the specific emphasis of the copy didn't create the intended level of urgency - so it had nearly no effect on conversion.

It would be worth re-testing the sticky bar with new messaging or even a countdown clock to try and invoke greater urgency and see if that can lead to a significant result.

experiments.title: How running a brief cultivation series impacted the amount NIO ticket revenue by a sale deadline
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending cultivation emails for half of our NIO housefile will achieve an increase of NIO Summit ticket revenue by the end of the early bird deadline.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2021 - 06/13/2021
Approaching the NIO Summit Early Bird ticket deadline, we wanted to test whether cultivating half of our file would increase the number of tickets sold by the end of the deadline.
We split our housefile in half and sent our regular marketing emails and NIO emails to both lists, while sending 4 bonus emails to the treatment group. These emails included various NIO resources like past sessions and blog posts. The thinking was that sending these resources will show the value of NIO Summit and increase excitement for the event.
This is the calculation of the total revenue brought in by each segment. These results were calculated by removing all test transactions and free tickets sold.
experiments.keyLearnings: Because of the low percentage in confidence in these results, this hypothesis requires more testing.

experiments.title: How a copy-heavy popup ad affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more value heavy ad for Top Nonprofits visitors will achieve more email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2020 - 12/20/2020
In this experiment, we were running a pop-up ad with a nonprofit marketing partner. For the sake of time, we whipped up version A with minimal copy and a very simple design.
But once the campaign launched, we wondered if running a very text-heavy ad would actually lead to emails since it communicated the value far more effectively.
We assumed we would get lower clicks, but we thought the clicks we would get would be more qualified – thus leading to greater conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw the exact same amount of conversion from both ads. As predicted, version B had significant decrease in clicks (158 VS 67), but a much higher conversion rate for those that viewed the landing page (6.6% VS 15%).

In this case, the drop in clicks and increase in conversion rate meant that our overall acquisition was a wash.

This indicates to me that there may be a happy medium between these two ad variations. It would be worth testing a new ad treatment with some value prop copy to help qualify the click, but leave more to be desired to discover on the next page.

experiments.title: Will a more personal email inviting people to a webinar increase engagement and signups?

rows.name: How can you improve your online fundraising, Steph
How can you improve your online fundraising, Gianc

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing a more human, personal email for email subscribers will achieve more clicks and therefore webinar registrants.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/03/2021 - 02/05/2021
NextAfter does at least one webinar a month and sends an email to big part of their email file to invite them to it. Previous testing has shown that a personal style email with some design elements works best. These ‘personal’ emails are often from someone, in this case Brady, but written by someone else, in this case Riley. For this test, we wanted to see if an even more personal email from and written by Brady with more personal anecdotes and tone would increase engagement (clicks) and therefore webinar signups.
experiments.keyLearnings: In the end the added personalization didn't help or hurt. When we looked at conversions there was a 3.2% increase in signups (not significant) and a slight click to conversion rate increase (82% vs. 81%, also not significant) but neither were valid. When we looked at replies back there was also a slight increase in replies to the more personal email but also not valid. So the more personal email with Brady's tone had no significant difference in driving engagement, clicks, and signups.

experiments.title: Will a more clear and direct subject line lead to more course signups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being more clear with the offer in the email in the subject line for email subscribers will achieve more email course signups.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/21/2021
We wanted to let people know about an updated online course that was now available for free. We’ve seen how more mysterious subject lines can evoke a sense of curiosity and get more opens but we’ve also seen that a clear direct subject line can sometimes lead to more action. So we put it to the test. The control was a more mysterious subject line that said there was ‘exciting news for you’ but didn’t say anything about what it was while the treatment was clear with the fact that there was a ‘new, free course for you’. Both subject lines used personalization in the first name in the subject line.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the final result on conversion wasn't statistically significant, there was a directional lift towards the Treatment increasing conversion. If we go 'up the funnel' or 'down the mountain' and look at clicks, we actually observed 15.8% increase in clicks with a 91% LoC which does indicate more intent to sign up from the clean subject line compared to the mysterious subject line. And when we also see that open rate for the clear subject line was 11% lower with 100% LoC, it does seem to validate both the hypothesis that:
Mysterious subject lines are better at generating opens
 	Clear subject lines are better at generating actions

experiments.title: Will a more clear and direct subject line lead to more course signups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being more clear with the offer in the email in the subject line for email subscribers will achieve more email course signups.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/21/2021
We wanted to let people know about an updated online course that was now available for free. We’ve seen how more mysterious subject lines can evoke a sense of curiosity and get more opens but we’ve also seen that a clear direct subject line can sometimes lead to more action. So we put it to the test. The control was a more mysterious subject line that said there was ‘exciting news for you’ but didn’t say anything about what it was while the treatment was clear with the fact that there was a ‘new, free course for you’. Both subject lines used personalization in the first name in the subject line.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the final result on conversion wasn't statistically significant, there was a directional lift towards the Treatment increasing conversion. If we go 'up the funnel' or 'down the mountain' and look at clicks, we actually observed 15.8% increase in clicks with a 91% LoC which does indicate more intent to sign up from the clean subject line compared to the mysterious subject line. And when we also see that open rate for the clear subject line was 11% lower with 100% LoC, it does seem to validate both the hypothesis that:
Mysterious subject lines are better at generating opens
 	Clear subject lines are better at generating actions

experiments.title: Will sending a voicemail increase the liklihood of attending a webinar live?
experiments.researchQuestion: Are people more likely to attend a webinar live if they get a voicemail?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/30/2020
For a webinar in March, we wondered if a ‘thank you’ voicemail from Brady that reminded them of the date and time of the webinar would increase the likelihood of them attending live (as opposed to watching later or not watching at all). Using Zapier and Slybroadcast to automate the process, new webinar registrants would get a thank-you voicemail soon after they had signedup with the message of thanks and when/where to join.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw no difference in performance

experiments.title: How a different offer affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer will lead to more emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Ended On:
In this experiment, we had a list of attendees from an event we sponsored. We want more people to sign up for our own event, but ultimately, we just want to get them to opt-in to communication so we can develop a relationship.
So we wondered…is it more effective to send this list an email about our conference and ask them to register for a free ticket? Or should offer them something of immediate value like a research study?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The donation page offer increased conversion by 72%, although it only reached a 78% level of confidence. Although this experiment didn’t reach the level of confidence we would like to see, it’s an indicator that NIO Summit might not be the best initial email acquisition point for an email prospecting campaign.
The donation page offer did increase clicks by 58% and reached statistical validity.

experiments.title: How a progressive form field impacts webinar conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a progressive form field to ask for approximate annual revenue impact conversions?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/29/2020
Internally, we’re wanting to find ways to get more data on the size of the organizations that subscribe to our emails. But we didn’t want to sacrifice conversion rate from our webinars for the sake of getting a little more data.
We wondered if we could add a field asking for their organization’s approximate annual revenue. We had previously tested into asking if they work for a non-profit vs for-profit. So we figured the least invasive way to ask for revenue was as a “progressive form field.” This means that the field only showed up if they checked that they worked for a nonprofit.
*This was the 2nd of an identical test to see if greater volume would impact significance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw a marginal shift in conversion rate – a drop of 3.9%. But this drop didn’t come anywhere close to reaching statistical significance.

This was the 2nd of an identical test after seeing an “invalid” result the first time. We thought that more volume could give a stronger indication of impact. The combined results of this experiment yield the same result…a 3.6% decrease, but with an invalid result.

If we determine that the benefit of getting this user-reported revenue data more valuable than the slight risk of small decrease in conversion, we’ll roll this treatment out.

experiments.title: How a progressive form field impacts webinar conversion rate

rows.name: Progressive Form Field Asking for Organization Size

experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a progressive form field to ask for approximate annual revenue impact conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/04/2020 - 06/16/2020
Internally, we’re wanting to find ways to get more data on the size of the organizations that subscribe to our emails. But we didn’t want to sacrifice conversion rate from our webinars for the sake of getting a little more data.
We wondered if we could add a field asking for their organization’s approximate annual revenue. We had previously tested into asking if they work for a non-profit vs for-profit. So we figured the least invasive way to ask for revenue was as a “progressive form field.” This means that the field only showed up if they checked that they worked for a nonprofit.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw a marginal shift in conversion rate – a drop of 3%. But this drop didn’t come anywhere close to reaching a significant level of confidence. It’s possible that running this for longer and getting more traffic to the page could prove that this decrease is in fact real – but the odds are low that this makes a significant impact over time.
We will continue to test the placement of this field, but knowing that the likelihood of a negative impact is very low. And the upside is that we get better segmentation and targeting for future outreach efforts.

experiments.title: How unique email messaging to key audience segments affects webinar registration
experiments.researchQuestion: Will sending unique copy to key audience segments increase webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2020 - 05/12/2020
In this experiment, we wondered if sending uniquely tailored messaging to our key audience personas would impact their likelihood of registering for a webinar. The control used general messaging to a portion of the housefile. The treatment used tailored messaging to fundraising executives and fundraising practitioners.
experiments.keyLearnings: While it seemed that registration was trending up for the segmented messaging group, we did not reach a statistical level of confidence of 95% or greater. This requires further testing, and leaning into the persona based messaging even more.

experiments.title: How unique email messaging to key audience segments affects webinar registration
experiments.researchQuestion: Will sending unique copy to key audience segments increase webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2020 - 05/12/2020
In this experiment, we wondered if sending uniquely tailored messaging to our key audience personas would impact their likelihood of registering for a webinar. The control used general messaging to a portion of the housefile. The treatment used tailored messaging to fundraising executives and fundraising practitioners.
experiments.keyLearnings: While it seemed that registration was trending up for the segmented messaging group, we did not reach a statistical level of confidence of 95% or greater. This requires further testing, and leaning into the persona based messaging even more.

experiments.title: How video ads on Facebook affect webinar registration
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a talking head video ad lead to greater webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/04/2020 - 03/12/2020
In a previous experiment, we saw indications that a “talking head” style Facebook ad video was performing better when it came to getting people to sign up for an upcoming webinar. But whenever we’re testing something brand new like this, it’s always a good idea to re-test and see if it holds up over time.
This can help fight against the novelty affect where we see an uptick for a time purely because something is new and different.
So for the control, we used the static title image for the webinar. And for the treatment, we used a video of me (Nathan) explaining what folks would learn in the webinar. All the rest of the ad was the same – copy, headline, cta, landing page, etc.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw mixed results from audience segment to audience segment, but no individual audiences saw a significant difference in performance when looking purely at registrations. Overall, an 11.6% increase was noted, but at an extremely low level of confidence.
But looking at cost per registrations paints a slightly different picture. Overall, the static image ad acquired webinar registrants for $7.58. But the video ad acquired registrants for just $4.70. That’s a 37% decrease in cost.
Now, a couple audience segments flipped the other direction in terms of cost and those will be noted for the future. But all in all, this video ad strategy seems to be performing better from a cost perspective, even if the conversion rates are the same as the static image.

experiments.title: Will an emoji in the subject line help increase open rate?

rows.name: Fundraising Content You’ll Love… see what I did there…
Fundraising Content You’ll ❤️… see what I did there…

experiments.researchQuestion: Will an emoji in the subject line help increase open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2020 - 02/28/2020
People use emoji’s all the time and we get asked how they can or should be used in emails and subject lines. We didn’t have any good experiments available to justify our thinking that it isn’t a worthwhile test so decided to test it for ourselves. We were sending a digest email on Valentines day so tied in the subject line to ‘love’ for the control and for the treatment we used a heart emoji in its place.
experiments.keyLearnings: Short answer: nope, didn’t do anything. With a 25% level of confidence, the emoji didn’t seem to have any impact on the likelihood of a subscriber to open.

experiments.title: Will bolding in an email increase click rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding some bold treatment throughout the email copy increase click rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/28/2020
For event invitation emails we take a more long form approach as we’ve seen that be most successful in the past but are always interested in seeing how we can improve and not alienate scanners who aren’t looking to read. So for this experiment we bolded some of the key points of the email like what the event was and when, what it was based on, where to learn more, and evidentials like past event attendee survey responses.
experiments.keyLearnings: We sent to a targeted and smaller list so the result was difficult to validate but there was a directional decrease for the bold/treatment. There was no difference in unsubscribe rate so it doesn’t appear the bolding really upset people but could just be that it felt a bit more ‘marketing-y’ and didn’t get the same engagement. Possibly worth re-testing with a larger email list size but could be risky.

experiments.title: How a new webinar landing page design affects registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a time/date bar on the landing page affect webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/07/2020 - 01/20/2020
One of the most common replies we get to webinar notification emails is people trying to clarify what time the webinar is actually at. Despite our best efforts to mention the time and time zone in every piece of communication we send, there still seemed to always be some level of confusion or clarity needed.
To accommodate for this, we added a time/date bar and widened the column of the template in the treatment version.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw no change in registration rate as a result of this experiment. In this case, we see that as a success. We were able to make the time/date of the webinar much clearer and more apparent without diluting or distracting from the primary value proposition.

experiments.title: How a talking head video in a Facebook ad affects webinar registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a talking head style facebook ad lead to more webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/07/2020 - 01/20/2020
I see video-based Facebook ads all the time for different courses and trainings, and they always drive me crazy. But if they’re being used all the time, I wondered if they might just be more effective than static images. In this experiment, we tested using a static webinar title image (which has been our typical ad design for many months) against a “talking head” style video.
The video is of me in our webinar studio giving a high-level description of the webinar and what viewers can expect. It contained subtitles so that it can easily be understood with the sound off.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When looking at results across all audiences, we saw a slight lift in conversion rate. But it did not reach a statistically significant level of confidence. However, we did find that the video ads delivered results that were 34% cheaper.

We will continue to test this in other contexts to see if the results hold up or perform better for specific audiences.

experiments.title: How a shortened digest email using bullets affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Will simplifying the design and content of the digest email lead to more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/20/2020
This experiment was inspired by a recent experiment from DTS. They took their designed newsletter email and simplified it down into a short intro blurb and a few bullet points. While we had previously tested into this more designed digest email format, it was only in the context of a long-form digest email with 6-8 different pieces of content to share.
For this experiment, we wondered if simplifying the design and content of the digest email down to a few bullet points could increase clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While there was a slight uptick in clicks, it wasn’t anywhere close to reaching a statistically significant result. As a result, it appears that this change in design made no real difference. It’s possible we might see a more significant result with a larger sample size.
This experiment shows us that both design styles may be acceptable and equally effective. This gives us more flexibility in how we write and craft these types of emails moving forward.

experiments.title: How more mystery in a subject line affects click-rate

rows.name: The webinar recording is ready for you

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a subject line with more mystery lead to more opens and clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/27/2019 - 07/22/2019
In this webinar follow-up email, we had been using the same kind of subject line for a while. While the sample size is low and hard to validate on, we wanted to put a hypothesis to the test that a subject line with more mystery would lead to more opens and clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings: As we assumed, our sample size was not large enough to validate the result, despite seeing an 11% increase in clicks from the treatment version. A more radical test will be required in order to see a statistically confidence result.

experiments.title: How offering more content options in an update email affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Will sending a newsletter style email with more content options lead to create click-throughs?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2018 - 12/10/2018
In this experiment, we were testing a first iteration of a more newsletter-like email. We have more content than we are able to share through these long-winded personal emails, so we wanted to see if sending more content options in more of newsletter or digest format would affect our click-rates.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no significant difference in click between the two versions, indicating that this change in formate does not have a measurable impact the likelihood of someone visiting our website. That being said, option B allows us to share more types of content which could theoretically lead to greater engagement.

experiments.title: How a Facebook ad image can affect conversion for a free online course offer [Lookalikes]
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an image that more clearly conveys the title of the course result in greater registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/14/2017 - 10/23/2017
When running a Facebook ad campaign promoting our online course on Facebook advertising, we wondered if we could increase registrations by adding clarity to the Facebook ad image. We added a couple additional elements to the ad image that clearly showed the idea of “Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors.”
The results below are from the Lookalikes audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment was ended early due to a large jump in cost-per-registration from the control. Although it did not reach full validity, the experiment was leaning towards the treatment as the winner. This indicates that the updated image brought additional clarity to the value proposition – something that helped this lookalikes audience understand the its relevancy.

experiments.title: How Bullet Points in a Facebook Ad Affect the Clarity of the Value Proposition [Nonprofit News Sources]
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of bullet points in a Facebook ad make the value of the course clearer, and lead to more course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 10/23/2017
When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below are from the ad set targeting an aggregate audience including follower of the Nonprofit Times and Chronicle of Philanthropy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test did not reach validity based on conversion. The control ad was turned off due to a significantly higher cost-per-registration in the treatment.
Neither the control or treatment was appearing to approach validity, and so it appears that adding bullets into the copy had no significant effect. Although, a low statistical power implies that we may not have had a large enough sample size to detect an effect if there indeed was one to be detected.
In any case, it is possible that the audience by nature is more prone to reading with closer scrutiny given that they are followers of nonprofit industry news sources. If this is the case, they would have reached the same understanding of the value propositon, regardless of whether or not they saw the control or the treatment.

experiments.title: How a case study example in an email affects webinar registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a follow-up webinar email that uses a case-study example increase registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/10/2017 - 10/12/2017
When sending a re-targeted email to invite non-responders to register for an upcoming webinar, we wondered if there was a way to write a more appealing follow-up email than simply re-hashing the content of the prior email.
For the treatment, we thought that we could help the user experience the quality of the content we would be presenting by showing a real case study that applied to year-end fundraising.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw no measurable difference in opens, clicks, or conversions between the control and treatment.
This tells us that neither method is better than the other to get more people to register for a webinar. Further testing would be needed to determine whether or not this type of email makes a difference for various segments of the non-responder audience (i.e. Non-opens VS Opens without conversions).

experiments.title: How a content focused homepage affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a content-driven homepage increase the number of clicks to additional content on nextafter.com?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/13/2017 - 08/15/2017
The NextAfter homepage had been focused on the work of our consultancy since the web site was first created. Over time, we have started to produce more and more content based on our online fundraising research. We’ve realized that this content is most often the first point of contact that someone has with NextAfter. The perceived problem is that our content is not being featured on the homepage.
Elevating our top content to our homepage has the potential to improve SEO, improve a user’s ability to navigate to the content they’re looking for, and gain a stronger understanding of our research. A homepage structured around our content will also give us more flexibility in the future to produce, feature, and market new content types and offers.
We decided to test this content driven homepage to see 1) if it would improve or maintain bounce rates, and 2) improve or maintain conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The number of clicks to view other content on nextafter.com saw a 5% increase, although it only reached a 77% level of confidence. Additionally, we did not see a statically valid difference in conversion rates on the homepage.
We also evaluated the average session duration of the control and treatment, finding that the content driven homepage had an 18% increase in session duration with a 91% level of confidence.
With 2 of 3 key metrics trending towards an increase, and conversion rates having no statistically valid change, we will begin to roll out this new homepage. In this case, even having no change is a win for us because this new design and layout will help us craft and organize our future content more effectively.

experiments.title: How a stylized email template affects conversion rate for a webinar
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a stylized email template affect the conversion rate for a webinar?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2017 - 04/05/2017
We had tested out of using email templates with our nonprofit research partners, but we had never tested what affect an email template would have with our own audience.
So we set up a treatment for a webinar promotional email that included a branded header and footer, as well as an image of the guest speaker. We hypothesized that this change could increase the credibility of both the speaker and the event itself.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was no statistically valid difference between the performance of the plain-text email and the stylized email template.
Moving forward, we will continue to use the plain-text version since it is easier to set-up, and it is consistent with what we recommend to our nonprofit partners.

experiments.title: How adding a phone number field affects name acquisition conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a required phone number field to the sign-up form affect the conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2016 - 07/20/2016
At NextAfter, we have a monthly live broadcast in which we present findings from the research we conduct with nonprofits, break down tests and experiments, and explore trends that demonstrate what makes a donor give.
We wanted to add a phone number field to our live broadcast sign-up form, but not at the expense of losing attendees.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We did not reach a significant level of confidence to conclude that adding the phone number field made a difference in conversion. If anything, the results pointed to a higher conversion rate with the phone number.
Moving forward, we will keep the phone number field knowing that it does not create significant enough friction in the sign up process to affect our attendance numbers, and it gives us another way to keep in touch with our attendees.

experiments.title: How adding a qualifying question affects conversion rate on an opt-in form

rows.name: Do you work for a nonprofit?

experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a qualifying question on a general opt-in form affect the conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2019 - 03/14/2019
In a previous experiment on a webinar sign-up page, we learned that asking registrants if they worked for a nonprofit or not led to a significant increase in webinar registrations. We wondered if we could duplicate the same affect on a general opt-in form.
experiments.keyLearnings: The additional qualifying question made no significant impact on conversion rate. At first, this was a bit of let down seeing that it saw a 32% increase on a webinar page. But in reality, even seeing no change in conversion is a positive because it allows us to be more targeted with our segmentation and messaging without negatively impacting opt-ins.

experiments.title: How noting the specific content offer type in the subject line affects conversion

rows.name: A full year’s worth of new fundraising ideas to test
A full year’s worth of new fundraising ideas to test [WEBINAR]

experiments.researchQuestion: Will noting that the email is about a webinar in the subject line increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/25/2019
In this experiment, we wondered if clarifying the type of content that the email was about in the subject line would lead to greater conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: This change had no affect on conversion. It’s possible that it may have had a more significant effect if placed at the beginning of the subject line, rather than the end. The end of the subject line could tend to get cut off on many devices and email platforms.

experiments.title: How a “Submit” button affects email acquisition on an email opt-in
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the word “submit” on a call-to-action button impact the likelihood of someone signing up for email updates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2018 - 01/14/2019
In this experiment, we wondered what affect the typical “submit” language on a call-to-action button would have compared to something that communicates more specifically what action is being taken. The control button said “Sign Me Up” and the treatment button said “Submit.”
experiments.keyLearnings: The “Submit” button had no significant impact on conversion. While we expected the submit button might lead to a decrease in conversion, it actually remained flat. It’s possible that neither of these call-to-actions are really communicating any distinct value. It’s also possible that we may have seen a more powerful result with a larger sample size. Follow-up testing will be needed to determine if a more value-focused call-to-action can increase conversion on this offer.

experiments.title: How more visual and design elements on a landing page affect downloads
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding more design and visual elements lead to more downloads?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/10/2019
NextAfter produces a number of reports, studies, and eBooks each year and has a ‘template’ for the download landing page which is very simple and copy heavy. With The Canadian Online Fundraising Scorecard there were some strong visuals and infographics within the report so we wondered if we shared more of the visual components on the landing page and gave the landing page a bit more of an aesthetic that matched the report itself if people would be more willing to download the report.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a very slight increase in downloads on the more designed version so it is worth looking at. The treatment had a signup option right away as opposed to the control where you have to scroll/read before you provide your information which means you may choose to download, or not, before you experience the offer or value so we could keep more of the conversational flow similar while adding more visual elements to see if that makes a difference. The visitor also has a pretty high motivation when they hit the page so perhaps the quick download added value. Again, worth looking at in future experiments.

experiments.title: How a key insight in a Facebook ad affects eBook downloads
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an ad focused on a key insight lead to greater eBook downloads?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2018 - 08/15/2018
In this experiment, we wondered which ad approach would be most effective for the “Nonprofit News Sources” audience when promoting a recently published study about fundraising teams.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this case, we saw a contradictory result to the same experiment conducted with the Nonprofit News Sources audience; although, this experiment did not reach validity. I have two hypothesis as to why the results are different. First, it’s possible that the makeup of the lookalike audience is more practitioners than managers and executives, and the specific insight on the ad image was more of a turnoff than it was interesting – where as the nonprofit news sources audience may skew towards people in a leadership role.
The second hypothesis is that the book cover image portrayed more practical ideas of how to “optimize your team,” which may have been more attractive to our typical audience.
Although this experiment didn’t reach validity, there was a substantial difference in cost per acquisition. The control was acquiring emails for $3.17, and the treatment for $5.45.

experiments.title: How a more direct call-to-action affects conversion from a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct call-to-action increase online course registration conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2018 - 04/20/2018
We ran this experiment across several different audience groups in our Facebook advertising of our new email fundraising course. We wondered if a more direct call-to-action could be effective in driving higher conversions. The control used a call-to-action of “Learn More,” while the treatment used a call-to-action of “Active Your Free Online Course.” This experiment was run on the Nextafter.com traffic Facebook audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The more direct call-to-action saw an increase of 186%, but it did not reach full statistical validity. We chose the treatment as the winner before reaching full validity since it had a 50% lower cost-per-conversion compared to the control.

experiments.title: How link placement in a course offer email affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a link placed higher in a course registration email increase clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2018 - 04/11/2018
In this experiment, we wondered if having a link to the course registration page near the top of the email would increase clicks to the registration page. The control looked like our normal, longer form personal emails. The treatment naturally flowed into a link to the page early on, and the rest of the copy was the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment didn’t significantly affect clicks to the landing page, indicating that link placement in a personal email like this does not affect the likelihood of getting a recipient to click. The value proposition is the primary influencer of the click.

experiments.title: How an additional incentive impacts webinar registration
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an additional incentive increase webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2018 - 02/27/2018
For February’s webinar on email acquisition, we wanted to see if offering an additional resource would help incentivize people to signup for the webinar. In this case, we offered the 6 Ways to Grow Your File eBook since it was related to the topic we were covering on the webinar – email acquisition. We simply added an offer box highlighting the fact that when people signed up to attend the webinar they would also get the eBook for the treatment and the control only had who the presenter would be (as usual).
experiments.keyLearnings: The increase was slight and the sample size was too small so we cannot determine if the eBook incentive helped with webinar registrations. Worth testing again in the future.

experiments.title: How copy length in a webinar promotional email affects registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will shorter and to-the-point email copy increase webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2018 - 02/14/2018
In this experiment, we were sending the initial promo for an upcoming webinar. We wondered if a shorter and more straight to the point email would affect webinar registrations.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment made no significant difference in webinar registrations. What this means moving forward is that we don’t always have to write a long-narrative email to get webinar registrations. It’s possible to convey enough of the value proposition in a shorter email to move motivated people to the landing page to register.

experiments.title: How adding more text links affects clickthrough rate in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding more text links increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/04/2018 - 02/07/2018
In this experiment, we were getting ready to send a research update email featuring recent content and learnings. Our typical email includes only raw url links, and focuses in a select few pieces of content. We wondered if adding additional text links, and more in line opportunities to view content could increase total clicks from the email to the website.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although it didn’t reach the level of confidence we would like, the treatment using more text links was leaning towards the winner with a 14.2% increase in clicks. This indicates that we can drive more traffic to our website from email marketing when we included additional links and more varied content.

experiments.title: How removing an extra decision point in an email affects webinar registration
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing an extra decision point in the email increase webinar registration?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/11/2018
In this experiment, we were promoting a new webinar on donor cultivation. The control version of the email asked a question in the middle of the copy before introducing the the actual call-to-action. We hypothesized that this question would create an extra decision point – forcing readers to say “yes” or “no” before actually presenting them with the call-to-action.
We thought that by removing this question, we might see an increase in webinar registration.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although we saw a 3.4% increase in conversion rate, we did not reach a statistical level of confidence for this to be definitive. It’s possible that this result could hold true, but we would need a much larger volume in order to validate this result.
